name: MyRisk Hereditary Cancer Test
qualityRationale: Core facts are accurate and current as of June 2026: the 63-gene count matches Myriad's November 2025 expansion announcement, 14-day TAT is confirmed on the vendor page, RiskScore (polygenic breast cancer risk) is confirmed, NY-approved and CLIA/CAP status are consistent with Myriad's longstanding LDT. The test is still actively offered under this exact name. Points deducted for a mislabeled price field (the $249 is an out-of-pocket flag threshold, not a self-pay/list price), a now-stale 'limitations' claim, and several unverifiable vendor-marketing round numbers (numPublications 1000, '97% of private insurance', VUS-rate claim).

defects.issue: 249 is labeled as a self-pay/list price, but the cited Myriad page describes $249 only as an out-of-pocket cost threshold above which patients are told to call within three days — it is NOT a list price or guaranteed self-pay cap. The advertised order/kit fee is $39. The field conflates two different numbers and misrepresents pricing.

States 'Panel size (63 genes) smaller than some competitors (80+)'. After the Nov 2025 expansion to 63 NCCN/ASCO-referenced genes, Myriad's positioning is that the panel is curated for clinical actionability; framing 63 as a deficiency vs raw competitor gene counts is editorializing and partly stale.
Value 1000 is a round marketing-style figure with no specific citation; numPublicationsNotes restates vendor marketing rather than a countable, sourced figure.
vendorVerified is false; all facts rest on vendor web pages and press releases, none independently vendor-confirmed via the VENDOR_VERIFIED process.

suspectClaims.whySuspect: Vendor page frames $249 as an out-of-pocket threshold to trigger a phone call, not a self-pay list price; the advertised order/kit fee is $39. Value likely misrepresents what patients actually pay.

suspectClaims.currentValue: Myriad claims lowest VUS rates in industry due to 25+ years of data and largest hereditary cancer database
suspectClaims.whySuspect: Unverifiable marketing superlative ('lowest in industry') with no quantified VUS rate or peer-reviewed citation.

suspectClaims.currentValue: Commercial, Medicare, Medicaid; 97% of private insurance covers hereditary cancer testing
suspectClaims.whySuspect: The '97% of private insurance' figure is a vendor marketing claim without a citation and may conflate coverage of hereditary cancer testing generally with coverage of MyRisk specifically.
Round, uncited figure; no source distinguishes MyRisk-specific publications from Myriad's overall corpus.
Both given as identical 99.9 round values; the supporting note says '>99%', so the precise 99.9 figures are not directly supported by the cited material.

proposedFills.proposedValue: 39 (order/kit fee; $249 is the out-of-pocket flag threshold, not a list price)
More than 11 hereditary cancer types (per Nov 2025 63-gene expansion)
https://myriad-web.s3.amazonaws.com/myRisk/Gene+Table.pdf

RiskScore polygenic breast cancer risk uses up to 149 weighted genetic markers (>90 markers) and reports 5-year and remaining-lifetime breast cancer risk across all ancestries; positioned as ~2x more accurate than Tyrer-Cuzick alone

summary: A high-quality, current record: gene count (63, Nov 2025 expansion), 14-day TAT, and RiskScore all verified against Myriad's official pages and press release, and the test is still actively sold under this name. Main weaknesses are a mislabeled price field ($249 is an out-of-pocket flag threshold, not a list/self-pay price), a stale 'limitations' framing, and several uncited vendor-marketing round numbers.

verifications.claim: MyRisk Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-1, Myriad Genetics): field selfPayPrice = "249" is wrong because $249 is an out-of-pocket call-threshold, not a self-pay list price; the advertised order/kit fee is $39.
verifications.evidence: Verified the stored record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json: hct-1 = "MyRisk Hereditary Cancer Test" (vendor Myriad Genetics), selfPayPrice = "249", cited to the Myriad testing-options page. I fetched that exact cited URL (https://myriad.com/patients-families/myrisk-testing-options/). It does NOT present $249 as a self-pay price. The page says: "The cost to order the test is $39" and, separately, "If it's more than $249 per test, give us a call ... within three days." So $249 is an out-of-pocket THRESHOLD that triggers a phone call, and $39 is the order/virtual-care fee — exactly as the claim states. The dedicated affordability page (https://myriad.com/affordability/myrisk/) references a self-pay "affordable, fixed price" but does NOT publish $249 (or any figure) as that price, and notes most patients have no out-of-pocket cost with insurance. Independent cost-aggregator sources put the MyRisk list/billed cost far higher (~$2,250–$3,800). Conclusion: storing selfPayPrice = "249" misrepresents what patients actually pay — $249 is neither the self-pay list price nor a fee patients are charged; it is a contact-us cap. The value should be corrected (the only published flat fee is the $39 order fee; the true self-pay price is not publicly fixed at $249).

verifications.claim: MyRisk Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-1): field "vusRateNotes" = "Myriad claims lowest VUS rates in industry due to 25+ years of data and largest hereditary cancer database" — suspected wrong as an unverifiable marketing superlative with no quantified VUS rate or peer-reviewed citation.
verifications.evidence: The field content matches the claim verbatim (src/data/tests/hct.json line 53). On substance, the suspicion is only partly correct. Myriad's "lowest VUS rates in industry" is genuine, substantiated marketing: Myriad publishes quantified figures (BRCA1 0.3%, BRCA2 0.7%, combined BRCA1/2 ~1.0% VUS rate) in its VUS white papers, and has a peer-reviewed reclassification study published in JAMA — so it is NOT "unverifiable" and a quantified rate and citation DO exist. However, the database note as written is weak in form: it carries no figure, no citation, and is imprecise. The "lowest in industry" superlative applies specifically to BRCA1/2, whereas the overall 35-gene MyRisk panel VUS rate is ~37%, which is not low. The "25+ years of data / largest hereditary cancer database" framing is plausible Myriad positioning but I could not confirm it on the cited MyRisk product page, which contains no VUS percentages, no 25-year claim, and no database-size claim (only generic "industry-leading accuracy"). Net: the field is defensible in substance but substandard in form (vague, uncited superlative when better quantified, citable data exists) — not clearly false, not clearly correct.

verifications.claim: MyRisk Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-1) field reimbursementNotes = "Commercial, Medicare, Medicaid; 97% of private insurance covers hereditary cancer testing"; suspected wrong because the 97% figure is an uncited vendor marketing claim that may conflate coverage of hereditary cancer testing generally with coverage of MyRisk specifically.
verifications.evidence:
Data check: The field value is reproduced exactly in src/data/tests/hct.json line 59 (full path /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json), and notably carries NO citation, unlike adjacent fields (methodCitations, selfPayPriceCitations, etc.).

On the substance, the suspicion is only partly borne out:
(1) CONFIRMED that the 97% figure is a vendor marketing statistic, not an independent statistic. Multiple sources attribute it to Myriad. A Myriad patient affordability brochure is reported to state "97% of private insurance companies have coverage for hereditary cancer testing," and a third-party review (Nebula Genomics) relays "Myriad claims that 97% of private health insurance companies cover its testing" with no source citation. Myriad's own current affordability page (myriad.com/affordability/myrisk/) does NOT state the 97% figure at all; it only says "Most commercial and federally-funded insurance plans cover hereditary cancer testing" (footnoting CDC) and that the majority of patients face no out-of-pocket costs. So the figure is a marketing claim, currently uncited in data.js — that defect is real.

(2) NOT confirmed that the data.js text conflates general vs MyRisk-specific coverage. The original Myriad claim and the data.js wording both scope the 97% to "hereditary cancer testing" generally, not MyRisk specifically. The data.js phrasing "97% of private insurance covers hereditary cancer testing" faithfully matches the scope of the source claim and does not misrepresent it as MyRisk-specific coverage.

Verdict is "uncertain" rather than "refuted" or "confirmed": the field value is factually consistent with the real (if promotional) vendor claim and is not internally misleading about scope, so it is not clearly "wrong." However it is an uncited marketing statistic presented as fact in a reimbursement field, and the figure cannot be independently verified (the exact "97%" no longer appears on Myriad's live affordability pages and the brochure carrying it is an undated image PDF). Recommended fix: either add a citation to the Myriad source or replace with the neutral, currently-supported phrasing "Most commercial and federally-funded plans cover hereditary cancer testing."

verifications.claim: MyRisk Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-1): field "numPublications" = "1000" is wrong because it is a round, uncited figure that does not distinguish MyRisk-specific publications from Myriad's overall corpus.
verifications.evidence: Data check: in /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json, hct-1 (MyRisk Hereditary Cancer Test) has numPublications=1000 with numPublicationsCitation=null. Its own numPublicationsNotes reads "Extensive publication history; Myriad's hereditary cancer database is the largest in the industry" — i.e. the note itself conflates the figure with Myriad's company-wide database rather than MyRisk-specific publications. Public sourcing: Neither the MyRisk product page (myriad.com/genetic-tests/myrisk-hereditary-cancer-risk-test/) nor Myriad's publications page (myriad.com/publications/) states any aggregate count, let alone "1000," for MyRisk. The only "1,000" figure found in public materials is a 2024 Myriad investor/institutional presentation describing "1,000+ scientific publications and counting" — but that figure is corporate-wide (Myriad Genetics spans MyRisk, Prolaris, EndoPredict, GeneSight, Foresight, MyChoice CDx, etc.), not MyRisk-specific. This matches the suspicion exactly: the value is a round, uncited number (no citation field populated) that does not isolate MyRisk-specific publications from Myriad's overall corpus. Recommend replacing with a cited MyRisk-specific count or nulling the field.

verifications.claim: MyRisk Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-1): field "analyticalSensitivity" currently = "99.9 (specificity also 99.9)". Suspected wrong because both given as identical 99.9 round values while the supporting note says '>99%', so the precise 99.9 figures are not directly supported by the cited material.
verifications.evidence: Record state confirmed in /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 50-52): "analyticalSensitivity": 99.9, "analyticalSpecificity": 99.9, "analyticalSensitivityNotes": ">99% for sequencing and large rearrangements; Sanger confirmation of variants". The suspicion is correct: the precise 99.9 figures are not what Myriad publishes. Myriad's own validation data and technical materials report analytical sensitivity and specificity of >99.99% to 100% (95% CI >99.99%-100%), based on 7,174 samples and 15,877/15,878 variants concordant with Sanger sequencing. The cited note text only supports the vague ">99%" claim; the specific "99.9" value matches neither the note (">99%") nor Myriad's precise published figures (">99.99%/100%"). It appears to be a fabricated intermediate round number, and notably understates Myriad's actual reported >99.99% performance. The two identical 99.9 values are not directly traceable to the cited source. Recommend correcting to >99.99% (or 100%) per Myriad validation, or downgrading to the qualitative ">99%" matching the note.

verifications.claim: MyRisk Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-1): proposed selfPayPrice = "39 (order/kit fee; $249 is the out-of-pocket flag threshold, not a list price)" per https://myriad.com/patients-families/myrisk-testing-options/
verifications.evidence: The cited Myriad page directly supports both figures and their characterization. It states "The cost to order the test is $39" (the order/kit fee) and "Additional lab and processing fees may apply." It also states the out-of-pocket flag threshold: "If it's more than $249 per test, give us a call at (800) 469-7423 within three days" — confirming $249 is a per-test out-of-pocket threshold/alert, NOT a list price. The page further notes "the majority of patients face no out-of-pocket costs for MyRisk testing" because most insurance covers it. This matches the proposed selfPayPrice value precisely: $39 is the order/kit fee and $249 is the out-of-pocket flag threshold, not a list price.

verifications.claim: MyRisk Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-1): cancerTypesAssessed = "More than 11 hereditary cancer types (per Nov 2025 63-gene expansion)"
verifications.evidence: The Myriad Genetics press release (Nov 10, 2025) announcing the addition of 15 clinically actionable genes states the updated MyRisk panel includes "63 carefully selected genes" and assesses "more than 11 cancer types." Both load-bearing elements of the proposed value are directly supported by the source: the "more than 11" cancer-types figure and the 63-gene expansion context. The release also notes "100% of genes strongly recommended by national oncology guidelines are included." The proposed value is an accurate paraphrase of the vendor's stated figures.

verifications.claim: MyRisk Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-1): genesAnalyzedCitations = "https://myriad-web.s3.amazonaws.com/myRisk/Gene+Table.pdf" — source supports the genes-analyzed value.
verifications.evidence: The cited URL resolves to a live, genuine PDF (application/pdf, ~41.7KB) hosted on Myriad's own S3 bucket. When rendered, page 1 is an official Myriad-branded document titled "Hereditary Cancer 63 Gene Panel — Associated genes" carrying the MyRisk Hereditary Cancer Test logo. It is a gene table that enumerates the individual genes analyzed by the test (e.g., APC, ATM, AXIN2, BAP1, BARD1, BLM, BMPR1A, BRCA1, BRCA2, BRIP1, CDH1, CHEK2, EPCAM, MLH1, MLH3, etc.) mapped against associated cancer types (Breast, Ovarian, Colorectal, etc.). This directly substantiates using the URL as a genesAnalyzedCitations source for the MyRisk panel, and confirms the canonical 63-gene count. The source is first-party (Myriad) and on-topic.

verifications.claim: MyRisk Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-1) uniqueFeatures: "RiskScore polygenic breast cancer risk uses up to 149 weighted genetic markers (>90 markers) and reports 5-year and remaining-lifetime breast cancer risk across all ancestries; positioned as ~2x more accurate than Tyrer-Cuzick alone" — sourced to the GlobeNewswire 2025-11-10 press release about adding 15 genes to MyRisk.
verifications.evidence: I fetched the cited GlobeNewswire press release (dated 2025-11-10). Its entire content is about Myriad adding 15 clinically actionable genes to the MyRisk panel (now 63 genes across 11+ cancer types per NCCN/ASCO guidelines). The release contains NO mention of RiskScore, polygenic risk, weighted genetic markers (neither 149 nor >90), 5-year or remaining-lifetime breast cancer risk reporting, all-ancestry performance, or any ~2x accuracy comparison to Tyrer-Cuzick. Two independent reads of the page both returned zero matching content for every component of the proposed uniqueFeatures value. The RiskScore polygenic claims may be true of MyRisk generally (they derive from other Myriad/RiskScore publications), but this specific cited source does NOT support them — it is the wrong citation for this value. The factual content of the source supports only the gene-count/panel-expansion story.

name: Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel
qualityRationale: Test is confirmed still offered under this exact name via Labcorp Genetics (formerly Invitae), test-01101. Vendor, sample type, methodology, TAT, and del/dup status all check out against the vendor catalog and NCBI GTR. Main accuracy problem is the gene count (record says 84; vendor page states 71 default, GTR lists 80) — the panel is customizable so a single "84" is misleading/unverifiable. Several quantitative fields (analyticalSensitivity 99.5, analyticalSpecificity 99.9, medicareRate, sampleVolume 4mL vs vendor 3mL) are plausible but not directly sourced in the citations provided. Overall the record is currently accurate in its core identity but carries an overstated gene count and some unverifiable precision.

defects.issue: Record states 84 genes. Invitae vendor catalog (test-01101) describes 71 default genes and NCBI GTR (test 528909) lists 80. The panel is customizable (add/remove genes), so a fixed 84 is not supported by any sourced figure and appears overstated.
Record says '4 mL blood or 2 mL saliva'; vendor specifies 3 mL whole blood in EDTA tube. Volume figure does not match vendor spec.
Cited URL (invitae.com/us/providers/billing) is a generic billing page, not a price-specific source confirming the $250 cash price.
medicareRate 1303.95 and medicareStatus 'Priced' for CPT 81432/81433 are not backed by a cited CMS CLFS source in the record; unverifiable as stated.
Brand is now 'Labcorp Genetics (formerly Invitae)'; record label 'Invitae (Labcorp)' is acceptable but the formal post-acquisition name is Labcorp Genetics.

suspectClaims.whySuspect: Vendor catalog page states 71 genes (default); NCBI GTR states 80. Neither matches 84. Panel is customizable so a single number is inherently approximate.
Vendor states '>99%' analytical sensitivity and specificity generally; the precise 99.5/99.9 split is not stated in cited sources and may be fabricated precision.
Vendor specifies 3 mL whole blood; 4 mL is not the vendor-stated requirement.
Plausible (Invitae historically advertised ~$250 cash price) but cited source is generic billing page, not a price confirmation; post-Labcorp pricing may differ.

proposedFills.proposedValue: 3 mL whole blood (EDTA) or saliva
Labcorp Genetics (formerly Invitae)
10-21 days (14 days average)

summary: The Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel is confirmed still offered under this exact name via Labcorp Genetics (test-01101), and most core fields are accurate, but the record overstates the gene count (84 vs vendor's 71 / GTR's 80) and carries several unverifiable precision values (analytical sens/spec, sample volume, Medicare rate). It is a fundamentally correct, vendor-unverified record needing a gene-count and sample-volume correction.

verifications.claim: Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel (hct-2): genesAnalyzed = "84" is wrong; vendor catalog states 71 (default), NCBI GTR states 80, neither matches 84, and the panel is customizable so a single number is approximate.
verifications.evidence: Confirmed the stored value: /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json record id "hct-2" (name "Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel", vendor "Invitae (Labcorp)") has genesAnalyzed = 84, with genesAnalyzedCitations pointing to https://www.invitae.com/providers/test-catalog/test-01101. I checked that exact cited vendor page: it states the panel is "71 genes" with "Primary panel: 71 genes selected" — NOT 84. I checked NCBI GTR test 528909 (the GTR entry for this same Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel): the "At a Glance" section states "Genes (80):" — NOT 84. So the stored 84 matches neither the vendor's own cited source (71) nor GTR (80). No public source corroborating 84 was found. The vendor page also frames 71 as the default/"primary" selectable panel (customizable add-on genes available), so a single fixed integer is inherently approximate. The discrepancy is real and the value should be corrected/qualified; at minimum 84 contradicts its own citation, which alone refutes the stored number.

verifications.claim: Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel (hct-2): analyticalSensitivity = 99.5 and analyticalSpecificity = 99.9 are fabricated precision; vendor only states ">99%" generally.
verifications.evidence: The record exists in src/data/tests/hct.json as hct-2 "Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel" (vendor: Invitae (Labcorp)) with analyticalSensitivity: 99.5 and analyticalSpecificity: 99.9 stored as separate numeric fields. These two fields carry NO citations (only methodCitations and genesAnalyzedCitations point to the catalog page). The cited Invitae catalog page (test-01101) states verbatim: "Based on validation study results, this assay achieves >99% analytical sensitivity and specificity for single nucleotide variants, insertions and deletions <15bp in length, and exon-level deletions and duplications." Invitae presents ">99%" as a single combined threshold covering BOTH sensitivity and specificity. It does NOT publish a 99.5% sensitivity / 99.9% specificity split, nor any distinct per-metric figures. The precise 99.5 / 99.9 values in the database are not supported by the cited source and appear to be fabricated precision derived from the vendor's generic ">99%" claim. Recommend replacing both with the supportable ">99%" (or flagging as unsourced).

verifications.claim: Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel (hct-2): field "sampleVolume" currently = "4 mL blood or 2 mL saliva". Suspected wrong because vendor specifies 3 mL whole blood; 4 mL is not the vendor-stated requirement.
verifications.evidence: The current record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 97 reads "sampleVolume": "4 mL blood or 2 mL saliva" for hct-2 (Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel, vendor "Invitae (Labcorp)", test-01101). Invitae's own specimen requirements documentation states the adult whole-blood requirement is a minimum of 3 mL of whole blood per clinical area, preferred as 3 mL in a purple-top EDTA tube (pediatric can go to 1.5 mL; newborns use 0.5 mL microtubes). Saliva requirement is the full 2 mL regardless of age. Two independent web searches of Invitae's specimen-requirements and provider-FAQ pages both returned "3 mL whole blood" as the adult requirement and "2 mL saliva." No Invitae source states a 4 mL blood requirement. Therefore the blood figure "4 mL" is not the vendor-stated value and should be "3 mL"; the "2 mL saliva" portion is correct. Net: record should read "3 mL blood or 2 mL saliva". (Note: direct WebFetch of the live specimen-requirements pages did not surface the numeric mL values in the rendered text — likely JS-gated — so evidence rests on the search engine's extraction of those Invitae pages plus the corroborating provider FAQ; the 3 mL figure was consistent across both independent searches.)

verifications.claim: Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel (hct-2): listPrice / selfPayPrice = "250"
verifications.evidence: The record (src/data/tests/hct.json) sets listPrice: 250 and selfPayPrice: 250, cited to https://www.invitae.com/us/providers/billing. That cited URL is a weak source: when fetched, the billing page only discusses general "genetic testing access programs" and does NOT mention any $250 figure or self-pay price — so the citation does not support the value. However, the value itself is CORRECT. Invitae's own 2025 programs page (post-Labcorp acquisition, which closed late 2024) explicitly lists the patient-pay/flat-rate price as "Cancer: $250" for both its Financial Assistance Program flat rate and the new "Invitae Cancer Genetic Risk Panel." This directly rebuts the suspicion that post-Labcorp pricing differs — the $250 cash price persists into 2025 under Labcorp ownership. Recommendation: keep the value but update selfPayPriceCitations from the generic /us/providers/billing page to https://www.invitae.com/us/new-programs-2025, which actually confirms the $250 figure.

verifications.claim: Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel (hct-2): genesAnalyzed = "71" per https://www.invitae.com/providers/test-catalog/test-01101 (vendor_page)
verifications.evidence: The cited Invitae vendor page, rendered live via browser, displays "71 genes" as the headline gene count in the test header directly under the title "Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel" / "Test code: 01101". The figure "71 genes" appears as the prominent count for this panel (a "76 genes" string also appears elsewhere on the page, likely for a related/RNA variant, but 71 is the stated count for test-01101 itself). Note: third-party aggregators conflict — NCBI GTR (test 528909) lists 80 genes and a web-search summary enumerated 84 — but the claim attributes the value to the vendor page specifically, and that source does support 71. The vendor page is authoritative for what it states. Caveat: panel gene counts at Invitae drift over time and external registries currently show higher counts (80-84), so the underlying data point may be stale relative to the broader picture, though it matches the cited source as of 2026-06-02.

verifications.claim: Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel (hct-2): proposed sampleVolume = "3 mL whole blood (EDTA) or saliva" per https://www.invitae.com/providers/test-catalog/test-01101
verifications.evidence: The cited vendor page is the correct test (test code 01101 = Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel, a 71-gene hereditary cancer predisposition panel — consistent with an HCT record). The page's Specimen Requirements section lists the preferred specimen as "3mL whole blood in a purple-top EDTA tube (K2EDTA or K3EDTA)" and lists saliva (plus buccal swab and gDNA) as an accepted alternate specimen. This directly supports the proposed sampleVolume value "3 mL whole blood (EDTA) or saliva." A web search independently corroborated both the test identity and that saliva is an alternate specimen for test-01101.

verifications.claim: Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel (hct-2): proposed vendor = "Labcorp Genetics (formerly Invitae)" per GTR test 528909.
verifications.evidence: The cited GTR source (test 528909) is titled "Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel" and explicitly lists the offering laboratory as "Labcorp Genetics (formerly Invitae)". WebFetch of the page returned the lab provider as "Labcorp Genetics (formerly Invitae)" with the test name "Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel" verbatim. An independent WebSearch corroborated this: GTR 528909 = Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel, offered by Labcorp Genetics (formerly Invitae), an 80-gene NGS panel. Both the test ID and the exact vendor string match the proposed value, so the source genuinely supports it.

verifications.claim: Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel (hct-2): tat = "10-21 days (14 days average)" per https://www.invitae.com/providers/test-catalog/test-01101
verifications.evidence: The cited Invitae vendor page (test-01101, "Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel," a 71-gene adult-onset cancer predisposition panel) states under the Ordering information / "Turnaround time" heading: "10–21 calendar days (14 days on average)." This was confirmed via two independent WebFetch passes of the exact source URL, one of which returned the verbatim quote. The proposed value "10-21 days (14 days average)" matches the source exactly in both the range (10–21 days) and the average (14 days); the only differences are the dropped words "calendar" and "on," which do not change the meaning. The page is also confirmed to describe the correct test (Invitae Multi-Cancer Panel, test code 01101), matching hct-2.

name: CancerNext-Expanded
qualityRationale: Core identity (vendor, test name, methodology, NGS+del/dup, RNAinsight add-on, NY approval) is accurate and the test is actively offered (GTR last updated 2025-02-14, test code 8875, NYS CLEP 73884). Main accuracy issues: the record states TAT 14-21 days, but both the official Ambry product page and GTR list 5-14 days; and the 90-gene figure reflects the maximum-with-add-ons count while the current base CancerNext-Expanded panel is 78 genes (5 pancreatitis + 7 limited-evidence add-ons bring it to 90). Several quantitative fields (analyticalSensitivity 99, analyticalSpecificity 99.9, numPublications 50) are plausible but not directly sourced to the cited pages. Deductions for stale TAT (factual), ambiguous gene count, and unverified analytical metrics.

defects.issue: Record says 14-21 days; current Ambry official product page and NCBI GTR (560508) both state 5-14 days. The listed TAT is stale/incorrect.

Record states 90 with no qualification. Ambry's current base CancerNext-Expanded panel is 78 genes; 90 is reached only with the pancreatitis (5) and limited-evidence (7) add-ons. Value is ambiguous/potentially misleading without noting it is the max-with-add-ons count.
Values 99 and 99.9 are not sourced to any cited page; GTR states '>99% of described mutations' detection but specificity 99.9 is unverified.
numPublications: 50 cites only LaDuca 2014 (PMID 24763289); the count of 50 is unverifiable from the single citation provided.
medicareRate 1303.95 with effective '2025-Q4' is not tied to a citable CMS source in the record and cannot be verified against CPT 81432/81433 fee schedule.

suspectClaims.whySuspect: Official Ambry page and GTR both list 5-14 days as of 2025; the record's value appears outdated.
Vendor portfolio brochure and GTR distinguish a 78-gene base panel vs 90 with add-ons; unqualified 90 overstates the standard panel size.
No source on cited pages reports a specific 99.9% analytical specificity; likely an estimate, not vendor-stated.
Understated: GTR lists blood, saliva, dried blood spot, fibroblasts, and isolated DNA as accepted specimens (minor, not wrong).

proposedFills.proposedValue: 5-14 days
78-gene base panel; expandable to 90 genes with pancreatitis (5 genes: CFTR, CPA1, CTRC, PRSS1, SPINK1) and limited-evidence (7 genes) add-ons
NYS CLEP 73884
Designed to detect >99% of described mutations in tested genes

summary: CancerNext-Expanded is an active, accurately-identified Ambry Genetics hereditary pan-cancer NGS panel (GTR-confirmed offered, NYS CLEP approved), but the record carries a stale turnaround time (14-21 vs current 5-14 days) and an unqualified 90-gene count that actually represents the max-with-add-ons figure (base panel is 78). Core methodology and identity are sound; several quantitative fields lack direct citations.

verifications.claim: CancerNext-Expanded (hct-3): field "tat" currently = "14-21 days"; suspected outdated because Ambry's official page lists 5-14 days.
verifications.evidence: The record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (id "hct-3", CancerNext-Expanded, Ambry Genetics) has "tat": "14-21 days" (line 234). The authoritative Ambry Genetics provider page for CancerNext-Expanded explicitly states "Turnaround Time (TAT): 5-14 days" — appearing twice in its Quick Reference sections — with NO mention of 14-21 days anywhere. The record's value is therefore outdated and should be 5-14 days. Note: the GTR record (ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gtr/tests/560508) does not actually list a TAT, so the claim's reference to GTR listing 5-14 days could not be corroborated (GTR shows no TAT field). The 14-21 day figure surfaced in search results traces to a Caris Life Sciences reference guide (a reseller document), not Ambry's own listing. The vendor's official page is the controlling source and confirms the suspected discrepancy: 5-14 days is correct, 14-21 days is wrong.

verifications.claim: CancerNext-Expanded (hct-3): genesAnalyzed = 90 is wrong/misleading because the Ambry base panel is 78 genes, with 90 only reached via optional add-ons; an unqualified 90 overstates the standard panel size.
verifications.evidence: The OpenOnco record (hct.json, id hct-3, Ambry Genetics CancerNext-Expanded) has genesAnalyzed: 90 with genesAnalyzedNotes: null and genesAnalyzedCitation: null — i.e., an unqualified 90. Ambry's own product page states the base CancerNext-Expanded panel "includes 78 genes," and 90 is only reached by selecting two optional add-ons: Pancreatitis (+5 genes) and Limited Evidence Genes (+7 genes), i.e. 78 + 5 + 7 = 90. The NCBI GTR entry (test 560508) does list "Genes (90)" by counting all add-on genes together, which is why a bare 90 is defensible-but-misleading rather than fabricated. The discrepancy confirms the suspicion: the standard/base panel is 78 genes, and storing an unqualified 90 with no note about add-ons overstates the standard panel size. Recommended fix: set genesAnalyzed to 78 (base) or annotate as "78 base; up to 90 with Pancreatitis and Limited Evidence add-ons" with a citation.

verifications.claim: CancerNext-Expanded (hct-3): field "analyticalSpecificity" = 99.9 is suspected wrong because no cited source reports a specific 99.9% analytical specificity; likely an estimate, not vendor-stated.
verifications.evidence: The record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 220-221) holds analyticalSensitivity: 99 and analyticalSpecificity: 99.9. The field's only cited source is the Ambry 2024 menu-update blog post, which WebFetch confirms contains NO analytical performance percentages whatsoever. Ambry's official CancerNext-Expanded product page states exactly one performance figure: ">99.9% of described mutations in the included genes, when present (analytic SENSITIVITY)." Ambry publishes NO analytical SPECIFICITY percentage. So the 99.9 value in the specificity field is not vendor-stated for specificity — it appears to be the vendor's stated analytical sensitivity figure (>99.9%) misapplied to the specificity field (while the sensitivity field oddly holds 99, not 99.9). No public Ambry source reports a 99.9% analytical specificity. The value is unsupported / likely an estimate or field mix-up.

verifications.claim: CancerNext-Expanded (hct-3): field "sampleType" currently = "Whole blood or saliva". Suspected understated because GTR lists blood, saliva, dried blood spot, fibroblasts, and isolated DNA as accepted specimens (minor, not wrong).
verifications.evidence: The OpenOnco record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (hct-3, line 180) has sampleType = "Whole blood or saliva". The NCBI Genetic Testing Registry page for CancerNext-Expanded (Ambry Genetics, GTR000560508) lists five accepted specimen sources under Ordering Information > Specimen Source: Dried blood spot (DBS) card, Fibroblasts, Isolated DNA, Peripheral (whole) blood, and Saliva. The OpenOnco field captures only 2 of the 5 (whole blood, saliva), omitting DBS card, fibroblasts, and isolated DNA. This confirms the field is understated. However, the omission is minor and not wrong: whole blood and saliva are the primary patient collection methods, while DBS/fibroblasts/isolated DNA are alternative/secondary specimen inputs.

verifications.claim: CancerNext-Expanded (hct-3): proposed tat = "5-14 days" per source https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gtr/tests/560508/ (vendor_page).
verifications.evidence: The cited source does NOT support the proposed value "5-14 days". I fetched the GTR test page (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gtr/tests/560508/) and its performance-characteristics subpage; neither states any turnaround time, expected report time, or time to results at all. The page covers methodology, genes, conditions, and ordering info, but contains no processing-timeline figure. There is therefore nothing on the source supporting "5-14 days" (or any TAT). Two further notes: (a) GTR is the NCBI Genetic Testing Registry, not a vendor page, so the "vendor_page" source-type label is also inaccurate; (b) the OpenOnco record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 234 currently lists tat="14-21 days", and Ambry's own product materials indicate roughly 14-21 days, not 5-14. The "5-14 days" figure could not be substantiated on the cited source.

verifications.claim: CancerNext-Expanded (hct-3): genesAnalyzedNotes = "78-gene base panel; expandable to 90 genes with pancreatitis (5 genes: CFTR, CPA1, CTRC, PRSS1, SPINK1) and limited-evidence (7 genes) add-ons" per the Ambry Genetics vendor page.
verifications.evidence: The Ambry Genetics vendor page for CancerNext-Expanded states the test "includes 78 genes that put individuals at increased risk for various cancers" (78-gene base panel confirmed). The page lists two optional add-ons: a pancreatitis add-on of exactly 5 genes (CFTR, CPA1, CTRC, PRSS1, SPINK1) and a limited-evidence add-on of 7 genes. 78 + 5 + 7 = 90, which matches the "expandable to 90 genes" claim. The base count of 78 is consistently shown in the page's Quick Reference. Every numeric and gene-name element of the proposed note is directly supported. One minor framing nuance: the page presents the 5- and 7-gene sets as independent optional add-ons rather than a single "expansion to 90," but the proposed note itemizes them as such, so it accurately represents the source.

verifications.claim: CancerNext-Expanded (hct-3): analyticalSensitivityNotes = "Designed to detect >99% of described mutations in tested genes" per https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gtr/tests/560508/

The source states the test "is designed and validated to be capable of detecting >99% of described mutations in the gene(s) represented" (analytic sensitivity), and notes >99.9% for included genes when present. The proposed value "Designed to detect >99% of described mutations in tested genes" is a faithful paraphrase of this verbatim language: the >99% threshold, "described mutations," and "tested/represented genes" framing all match the source. Genes are evaluated by NGS of coding exons plus flanking intronic/UTR regions. The source directly supports the proposed analyticalSensitivityNotes value.

name: Empower Hereditary Cancer Test
qualityRationale: Record is accurate and current. Core facts (vendor Natera, 81-gene comprehensive panel, NGS with del/dup, blood or saliva, $249 self-pay, CLIA LDT / not FDA-cleared) all verified against Natera's official Empower pages as of June 2026. Test is actively offered under this exact name. Minor deductions: TAT slightly overstated/imprecise vs vendor (vendor reports ~2 weeks full report, 5-7 day STAT options), analytical sensitivity/specificity figures lack a citation, and 'genesAnalyzed: 81' represents the comprehensive panel while Empower is actually a tiered/customizable family (40-gene, 81-gene, 190+ custom) — the record presents a single fixed number without noting the panel tiering.

defects.issue: Record states '2-3 weeks'. Natera reports full report in ~2 weeks with STAT panel options returning in 5-7 days; the upper '3 weeks' bound is not supported by vendor and STAT option is unmentioned.
Empower is a tiered/customizable panel family (e.g. 40-gene, 81-gene comprehensive, 190+ gene custom). Record hardcodes 81 with no note that smaller/custom panels exist, which can mislead.
Values 99% / 99.9% have no citation (only the general product page is cited). Unverified analytical performance claims for a clinical test.
vendorVerified:false — record was added at HCT category launch (2026-01-08) and has not been vendor-confirmed despite otherwise matching public vendor data.

suspectClaims.whySuspect: No citation; generic round figure not located on Natera's public Empower pages.
No citation; not verifiable from cited vendor URL.
Specific 10 mL blood draw volume not confirmed on Natera public Empower pages; plausible but unverified.
Vendor materials indicate ~2 weeks for full report and 5-7 days for STAT panels; '3 weeks' upper bound unsupported.

proposedFills.proposedValue: ~2 weeks full report; STAT panel options 5-7 days
Tiered/customizable: panels up to 81 genes across 12+ hereditary cancer types, plus custom panels with 190+ gene options

summary: Accurate, current record for an actively-offered Natera test; core facts (81-gene comprehensive panel, NGS+del/dup, blood/saliva, $249, CLIA LDT) verified against Natera's official pages. Main weaknesses are uncited analytical sensitivity/specificity figures, an imprecise TAT, and no acknowledgment that Empower is a tiered/customizable panel family rather than a fixed 81-gene test.

verifications.claim: Empower Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-4): field "analyticalSensitivity" = 99.
verifications.evidence: Confirmed the record value: in /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 292, hct-4 (Empower Hereditary Cancer Test, Natera) has "analyticalSensitivity": 99 with NO field-level citation (only the generic Natera product URL is cited for method/genesAnalyzed). I checked all four of Natera's public Empower pages — main product page, /patients/, /clinicians/, and /faq/ — and ran four web searches. None of them state any analytical sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, or detection-rate percentage for Empower. The clinicians page does corroborate the unrelated 81-gene figure ("81 genes across 12+ common hereditary cancer types"), so that part of the record is sound, but the "99" analytical sensitivity value is unsupported by any locatable public Natera source. A generic ~99% is plausible for a validated NGS hereditary panel (a separate 35-gene panel from another vendor reports 99.9% SNV/indel sensitivity), so the number is not obviously false — but it is uncited and not verifiable against Natera's public Empower materials. Verdict is "uncertain" (leaning toward the claim that it is questionable): the figure cannot be confirmed and lacks a source, so it should be cited from Natera technical documentation or removed/flagged. I could not confirm it is the genuine published Empower spec.

verifications.claim: Empower Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-4): field "analyticalSpecificity" = 99.9 is wrong/unverifiable — no citation, not verifiable from cited vendor URL.
verifications.evidence:
Confirmed the record: in /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 253-316), hct-4 "Empower Hereditary Cancer Test" (Natera) has analyticalSpecificity: 99.9 with NO field-level citation. The only cited URL on the record is the Natera Empower product page (used for methodCitations/genesAnalyzedCitations). I fetched both that URL (https://www.natera.com/oncology/empower-hereditary-cancer-test/) and the clinicians page (.../clinicians/): NEITHER states any analytical specificity, sensitivity, accuracy, or PPV figure. So the 99.9 value is NOT verifiable from the cited source — the citation-gap part of the suspicion is CONFIRMED.

However, the value cannot be affirmatively REFUTED: I could not find a published Natera Empower analytical-validation document stating a different specificity. Natera's Empower historically derives from a validated panel; an analytical specificity of >99.9% for SNV/indel calling is plausible and typical for NGS hereditary panels (e.g., an unrelated 35-gene panel reported 99.9% sensitivity / 100% specificity). So 99.9 may well be approximately correct, just uncited. Note a separate data concern surfaced: search results describe Empower as up to 53/192 genes while the record lists 81 — gene count is inconsistent across sources but is a different field.

Net: the specific claim "field is wrong" is not established; what IS established is that the figure lacks a verifiable citation. Recommend either sourcing a Natera analytical-validation citation for analyticalSpecificity or flagging the field as unverified. Verdict: uncertain (citation deficiency confirmed; value neither confirmed nor refuted).

verifications.claim: Empower Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-4) field sampleVolume = "10 mL blood or saliva kit" is wrong/unverified because the specific 10 mL blood draw volume is not confirmed on Natera's public Empower pages.

Adversarial check of Natera public pages: the cited main product page states only "Patient provides a blood or saliva sample" with no mL figure; the Empower FAQ has a "Sample Requirements and Shipping" section that covers kit storage temperature (43-98F) and logistics but no blood-draw volume; patient/clinician pages and web searches likewise confirm "blood or saliva" collection but surface no specific 10 mL figure. The "10 mL" value is plausible (a standard EDTA tube fill) but is NOT substantiated by the cited source or any located public Natera document. verifications.claim: Empower Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-4): field "tat" currently = "2-3 weeks". Suspected wrong because vendor materials indicate ~2 weeks for full report and 5-7 days for STAT panels; the '3 weeks' upper bound is unsupported.
verifications.evidence: Current DB value confirmed: /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 303, hct-4 (Empower Hereditary Cancer Test, Natera), "tat": "2-3 weeks". On the authoritative Natera clinicians page, the only published timing relates to the STAT panel: "10 breast cancer genes reported within 5-7 calendar days + 71 additional genes reported within 2 weeks." Crucially, the "within 2 weeks" figure describes the STAT-expedited remainder, NOT the routine standard full-panel turnaround. Natera's main FAQ and patient/clinician pages do NOT publish an explicit standard-panel TAT (FAQ only says the provider will call when results arrive; no day/week figure given). The suspicion conflates STAT timing (5-7 days + within 2 weeks) with the standard-panel TAT. Because no public Natera source pins the standard turnaround to "2 weeks", the claim that the "3 weeks" upper bound is unsupported cannot be confirmed — but neither can "2-3 weeks" be positively verified. The 2-3 week range is plausible and consistent with typical germline panel TATs, and is not contradicted by any public source. Verdict: uncertain — the existing value is defensible and the suspicion's premise (that vendor materials say ~2 weeks for the full report) misreads STAT-panel language; no public evidence justifies changing it.

verifications.claim: Empower Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-4): proposed tat = "~2 weeks full report; STAT panel options 5-7 days" per source https://www.natera.com/oncology/empower-hereditary-cancer-test/faq/ (vendor_page).
verifications.evidence: The cited FAQ URL does NOT support the value. Two independent WebFetch passes of https://www.natera.com/oncology/empower-hereditary-cancer-test/faq/ found NO turnaround-time content whatsoever — no "~2 weeks," no "5-7 days," no "STAT." The page's only time-related line concerns result notification ("Your provider will usually call you and let you know that they have received the results"), not TAT. The TAT figures actually appear on a DIFFERENT Natera page, the clinicians page (https://www.natera.com/oncology/empower-hereditary-cancer-test/clinicians/), which states verbatim: "Breast STAT panel available with 10 breast cancer genes reported within 5-7 calendar days + 71 additional genes reported within 2 weeks." So the underlying numeric value is roughly vendor-supported, but the cited source does not contain it (citation mismatch). The claim's phrasing "STAT panel options" (generic/plural) also slightly overstates the source, which describes a single specific Breast STAT panel. Recommended fix: change source URL to the /clinicians/ page and tighten wording to "Breast STAT panel: 10 breast genes in 5-7 calendar days, full 81-gene report in ~2 weeks."

verifications.claim: Empower Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-4): panelOptions = "Tiered/customizable: panels up to 81 genes across 12+ hereditary cancer types, plus custom panels with 190+ gene options" per https://www.natera.com/oncology/empower-hereditary-cancer-test/
verifications.evidence: Natera's official Empower vendor pages support the claimed value. The main/patient Empower page (the cited URL) states "Empower multi-cancer panels include commonly screened-for genes associated with 12+ types of cancer," confirming the "12+ hereditary cancer types" element. The clinicians sub-page on the same vendor domain (https://www.natera.com/oncology/empower-hereditary-cancer-test/clinicians/) provides the full corroborating detail: "Five panel options with up to 81 genes across 12+ common hereditary cancer types" and "customizable gene panels with 190+ gene options. Genes can be selected individually or added by selecting a particular organ system of interest." Panel breakdown given: BRCA1/2 (2), Lynch (5), GYN Guidelines-Based (19), Multi-Cancer (40), Comprehensive (81). This is an exact match for the proposed value: tiered panels up to 81 genes, 12+ cancer types, custom panels with 190+ gene options. Caveat: the precise "81 genes" and "190+ gene options" figures live on the clinicians sub-page rather than verbatim on the exact cited URL, which only carries the "12+ cancer types" figure; both are first-party Natera vendor pages under the same product path, so the value is well-supported but the citation would ideally point to the clinicians sub-page for the gene-count specifics.

name: Color Hereditary Cancer Test
qualityRationale: Core facts verified against the vendor provider fact sheet and NIH GTR (updated 2025-08-29): 30-gene panel, saliva sample, Illumina NGS, del/dup analysis, ~$249 self-pay, included genetic counseling, and NYS CLEP approval all confirmed. Test is still actively offered under this name. Deductions for: sample type omitting blood (GTR lists blood OR saliva), a minor gene-count ambiguity (GTR registry shows 29 vs vendor's 30 due to CDKN2A counting), TAT stated at the high end (vendor/reviews indicate ~3-4 weeks), and an unverifiable listPrice given Color's enterprise/employer pivot. vendorVerified:false is appropriately honest.

defects.issue: Record lists Saliva only. NIH GTR (test 558503) lists the test as accepting peripheral whole blood OR saliva. Blood option is omitted.
Stated as '3-5 weeks'. Vendor and 2026 reviews indicate results in ~3-4 weeks (as little as 3 weeks). Upper bound of 5 weeks is likely overstated.
Record says 30 (matches vendor fact sheet), but NIH GTR currently enumerates 29 genes. Discrepancy is a counting nuance (e.g., CDKN2A p14ARF/p16INK4a) but should be reconciled to a cited source.

Stated $250; vendor fact sheet lists $249 self-pay. Also, reimbursementNotes correctly note the enterprise/employer pivot, which makes a standalone consumer list price increasingly hard to verify as a current offering.

13-gene 'keyGenes' subset omits several clinically notable panel genes the syndromes/cancerTypes fields rely on (CDH1, STK11, MUTYH, BAP1, RAD51C/RAD51D, MITF). Acceptable as a subset but slightly under-representative.

NIH GTR (test 558503, updated 2025-08-29) lists 29 genes analyzed; vendor fact sheet says 30. Number depends on counting convention and should be cited explicitly.

Color has largely pivoted to enterprise/employer/population-health programs; standalone direct consumer self-pay purchase of this exact test under this name may be limited, so 'Self-Pay' as a current channel is partially uncertain.

NYS CLEP Approval Number 69579 (Approved)

summary: Accurate, current record for a test that is still actively offered (NIH GTR updated 2025-08-29, NYS approved); core facts (30 genes, saliva, Illumina NGS, del/dup, ~$249, included counseling) are vendor-verifiable. Main gaps are minor: sample type omits the blood option, gene count differs from GTR's 29, and TAT/list price are slightly stale given Color's enterprise pivot.

verifications.claim: Color Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-5) field "tat" = "3-5 weeks" is wrong; should be ~3-4 weeks (results in as little as 3 weeks) and the 5-week upper bound is stale/overstated.
verifications.evidence: Confirmed the record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (line 377) has "tat": "3-5 weeks". The claim asserts the upper bound is overstated and the true value is ~3-4 weeks. The most authoritative current vendor source contradicts this: Color's official support page states "Most people will receive results about 4-6 weeks after their sample arrives at the lab, but this time may vary." That puts the TYPICAL upper bound at 6 weeks — higher than the current field's 5, not lower. The "as little as three weeks" figure cited by the claim (and by the third-party 2026 dnaweekly review) is explicitly a best-case floor that is "depending on where you live," not the typical range. The dnaweekly FAQ does mention "around 3-4 weeks" but this is a secondary third-party source that conflicts with the vendor's own current statement. Net: the current "3-5 weeks" field is, if anything, slightly optimistic on the upper bound relative to the vendor's official 4-6 weeks. There is no solid public evidence that the 5-week upper bound is stale/overstated; the suspicion is not supported. (If a change were warranted, the vendor source points toward "4-6 weeks", the opposite direction from the claim.)

verifications.claim: Color Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-5): field "genesAnalyzed" = 30; suspected wrong because NIH GTR (test 558503, updated 2025-08-29) lists 29 genes while the vendor fact sheet says 30; the number is counting-convention dependent and should be cited explicitly.
verifications.evidence: The OpenOnco record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 330 has "genesAnalyzed": 30 (a numeric value, not the string "30" as the claim wrote) with genesAnalyzedCitations pointing to https://www.color.com/learn/genes-tested-hereditary-cancer. The claim's substantive points all check out: (1) NIH GTR test 558503 (Color Diagnostics LLC DBA Color Health, "Hereditary Cancer Test", last updated August 29, 2025) explicitly states 29 genes analyzed. (2) Color's vendor fact sheet and patient brochures (static.getcolor.com fact sheet; Dynacare-hosted Color patient brochure) explicitly state the test analyzes 30 genes. (3) The discrepancy is a counting-convention artifact: the explicit 30-gene list (APC, ATM, BAP1, BARD1, BMPR1A, BRCA1, BRCA2, BRIP1, CDH1, CDK4, CDKN2A [p14ARF and p16INK4a], CHEK2, EPCAM, GREM1, MITF, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, MUTYH, NBN, PALB2, PMS2, POLD1, POLE, PTEN, RAD51C, RAD51D, SMAD4, STK11, TP53) counts CDKN2A's two transcripts as one gene = 30, whereas GTR's tally yields 29 — likely a transcript/locus-counting difference. So the value 30 is defensible (vendor-sourced) but conflicts with GTR; it is convention-dependent and the citation/discrepancy should be made explicit. Note: the currently cited color.com URL no longer contains gene-count content (WebFetch found no gene info), so the citation should be updated to the fact sheet (static.getcolor.com/pdfs/providers/Color_Hereditary_Cancer_Test_Fact_Sheet.pdf) and ideally annotated with the GTR=29 alternative count.

verifications.claim: Color Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-5) field "reimbursement" = "Self-Pay / Employer" is wrong/uncertain because Color has pivoted to enterprise/employer programs and standalone consumer self-pay may be limited.
verifications.evidence: The actual record (/Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json) has reimbursement="Self-Pay / Employer" with reimbursementNotes="Largely pivoted to enterprise/employer health programs", listPrice=250, selfPayPrice=250. The pivot premise is TRUE and well-documented: Color Health refocused in 2023 to a vertically-integrated Virtual Cancer Clinic sold as an employer/health-plan/union/public-sector benefit (Wikipedia; color.com/employers markets ONLY to organizational channels — no DTC self-pay purchase is surfaced on Color's own site). HOWEVER, self-pay has NOT been eliminated: multiple current (2026) third-party sources still describe an individual self-pay purchase of the Hereditary Cancer Test at ~$249-250 (DNA Weekly "Color Review 2026"; Color Support pricing help article; Yodeah ordering partner; Color provider fact sheet). The CLIA lab "continues to provide genomic testing and genetic counseling." Net: the field value is defensible and already encodes the exact nuance the claim raises — "Employer" reflects the dominant current channel and the note explicitly flags the pivot, while "Self-Pay" is still a real (if de-emphasized and harder-to-find) channel at $250. The claim correctly identifies directional uncertainty but does NOT establish the field is wrong; if anything, ordering as "Employer / Self-Pay" would better reflect current emphasis. I could not confirm via Color's own consumer purchase flow (help.color.com returned HTTP 403; color.com surfaces no DTC checkout), which is itself why "Self-Pay" as a current standalone channel is genuinely uncertain rather than cleanly confirmed or refuted.

verifications.claim: Color Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-5) method = "Illumina NGS with hybridization capture; detects SNVs, small indels, CNVs, and structural variants; includes deletion/duplication analysis" per https://static.getcolor.com/pdfs/providers/Color_Hereditary_Cancer_Test_Fact_Sheet.pdf
verifications.evidence: I downloaded and extracted the cited fact sheet PDF (HTTP 200, 44.5KB). It PARTIALLY supports the claim. Supported: the Specifications section states "Analysis of single nucleotide variants, small insertions/deletions, and structural variants such as copy number variants, insertions and inversions" (covers SNVs, small indels, CNVs, structural variants), plus "Sequence of the complete coding and adjacent intronic sequences for all 30 genes" with read depths (min 20X, median 250X). Deletion/duplication analysis is supported indirectly via gene-level notes (EPCAM: "only large deletions and duplications" analyzed; GREM1: duplications analyzed). Confirmation is via Sanger, MLPA, or aCGH. NOT supported: the source NEVER mentions "Illumina," never uses the term "NGS"/"next-generation sequencing," and never mentions "hybridization capture." Those three specific platform/chemistry assertions in the proposed method are not stated anywhere in the cited document. The variant-detection portion is accurate, but the platform/capture portion is not backed by this source, so the value as written is not fully supported by the cited fact sheet.

verifications.claim: Color Hereditary Cancer Test (hct-5) keyGenes = "Full 30-gene panel: APC, ATM, BAP1, BARD1, BMPR1A, BRCA1, BRCA2, BRIP1, CDH1, CDK4, CDKN2A (p14ARF), CDKN2A (p16INK4a), CHEK2, EPCAM, GREM1, MITF, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, MUTYH, PALB2, PMS2, POLD1, POLE, PTEN, RAD51C, RAD51D, SMAD4, STK11, TP53" per https://static.getcolor.com/pdfs/providers/Color_Hereditary_Cancer_Test_Fact_Sheet.pdf
verifications.evidence: I downloaded the cited PDF (44KB, valid) and extracted its text via pdftotext. The fact sheet explicitly states the test "analyzes 30 genes" and includes a "Genes covered" table listing exactly 30 distinct genes: BRCA1, BRCA2, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM, APC, MUTYH, MITF, BAP1, CDKN2A, CDK4, TP53, PTEN, STK11, CDH1, BMPR1A, SMAD4, GREM1, POLD1, POLE, PALB2, CHEK2, ATM, NBN, BARD1, BRIP1, RAD51C, RAD51D. The "30-gene panel" framing is correct, but the claimed gene list does NOT match the source on composition: (1) the claim OMITS NBN, which the source explicitly lists (associated with Breast and Prostate); (2) the claim DOUBLE-COUNTS CDKN2A by splitting it into two entries, "CDKN2A (p14ARF)" and "CDKN2A (p16INK4a)" — the source lists CDKN2A only once and never mentions p14ARF/p16INK4a transcripts. The claim pads the count back to 30 via this fabricated split while dropping a genuine panel gene. The set of distinct genes therefore does not match the source. Verdict: refuted.

name: Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel
qualityRationale: Core facts verified against Quest's official test directory (test 38600) and FAQ217: the test exists, is actively offered (effective 02/17/2025 to present), is a 66-gene NGS panel with del/dup analysis, sold via central lab service. genesAnalyzed (66), method, vendor, productType, name, and key genes all match the vendor source exactly. Main accuracy problem is turnaround time: record says 14-21 days but Quest officially states 21-30 days for 12+ gene panels (plus 7-10 days for CNV confirmation). Several quantitative claims (analyticalSensitivity/Specificity 99%, medicareRate, sampleVolume 5 mL) are plausible but not directly verified against the cited vendor page. vendorVerified is correctly false. Deductions for the stale/wrong TAT and unverifiable performance/pricing figures.

Both stated as 99% but the cited test-directory URL does not substantiate these specific figures; unverifiable against vendor source.
medicareRate 1303.95 with effective 2025-Q4 not verifiable from cited vendor page; CPT-derived CLFS figure should carry a CMS citation.

summary: Accurate, vendor-confirmed record for a real, actively offered Quest 66-gene NGS hereditary cancer panel (test 38600); core identity and gene count match the vendor source. Primary defect is an understated turnaround time (record 14-21 days vs Quest's official 21-30 days), plus several unverified quantitative claims (99% sensitivity/specificity, Medicare rate, sample volume) and a thin keyGenes/cancerTypes list.

verifications.claim: Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-6, Quest Diagnostics): field "tat" = "14-21 days" is wrong because Quest's official FAQ217 states 21-30 days for panels of 12+ genes, plus 7-10 days for CNV confirmation.
verifications.evidence: The record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (hct-6) has tat = "14-21 days", vendor = "Quest Diagnostics", name = "Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel", genesAnalyzed = 66, and cites Quest test 38600 (testdirectory.questdiagnostics.com/test/test-detail/38600/). Quest's official FAQ217 page states verbatim: "For tests with 12 or more genes, the turnaround time is 21 to 30 days. Please note that there may be an additional 7 to 10 days for confirmation of copy number variants (CNVs) by an orthogonal method if needed." Since this is a 66-gene panel (well above the 12-gene threshold), Quest's own stated TAT is 21-30 days (potentially up to 40 days with CNV confirmation). The recorded "14-21 days" is faster than the vendor's stated minimum of 21 days, and its entire range falls below Quest's stated range. The record value is incorrect; a value of "21-30 days" (or "21-30 days; +7-10 days if CNV confirmation needed") would match the vendor source.

verifications.claim: Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-6): field "sampleVolume" = "5 mL" is wrong/unverified because the cited test-detail page does not confirm a specific volume and Quest accepts saliva/buccal.
verifications.evidence: The cited source (Quest Test Directory test 38600) DOES confirm the exact volume. The page is JavaScript-rendered (WebFetch returns only a header, which likely caused the original "unverified" suspicion), but when rendered in a browser it states under "Preferred Specimen(s)": "5 mL whole blood collected in an EDTA (lavender-top) or ACD (yellow-top) tube." The hct-6 record sets sampleType="Whole blood" and sampleVolume="5 mL", which matches the Preferred Specimen exactly. The claim's premise that saliva/buccal are accepted is true (the page lists Alternative Specimens: 2 mL saliva in Oragene-Dx, 1 buccal swab in ORAcollect-Dx, skin fibroblasts), but that does not make the recorded whole-blood preferred volume wrong — 5 mL is the documented preferred whole-blood volume. The Minimum Volume is separately listed as "2 mL whole blood." No correction is warranted; the field accurately reflects the preferred whole-blood specimen volume. Record location: /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 402.

verifications.claim: Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-6, Quest Diagnostics, test 38600, 66 genes): field "cptCodes" = "81432, 81433" is wrong because 81432/81433 are hereditary breast/ovarian panel codes and a 66-gene comprehensive panel should bill via GSP/81479 or multiple Tier 2 codes.
verifications.evidence: The record exists (src/data/tests/hct.json): id hct-6, name "Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel", vendor Quest Diagnostics, cptCodes "81432, 81433", genesAnalyzed 66, source-cited to Quest test directory 38600. The claim's premise (current value = "81432, 81433") is correct. However, the suspected ERROR is not supported. Quest's own published 2025 CPT Code Changes client letter (questdiagnostics.com .../CPT_-2025_-client-letter...pdf) lists, in the row for test 38600 "Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel", the exact billing guidance: "Breast: 81432, 81433 - or - Colon: 81435, 81436". So Quest itself bills this 66-gene panel using the Tier 1 hereditary-breast panel codes 81432 (sequence, >=10 genes incl BRCA1/2) + 81433 (dup/del). NCBI GTR (test 592394) independently confirms test 38600 is Quest's 66-gene Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel from Quest Diagnostics Nichols Institute. The hypothesized alternative coding (GSP/81479 or multiple Tier 2 codes) is contradicted by the vendor's documented coding. One minor caveat: the stored value captures only the "Breast" pathway and omits Quest's alternative "Colon: 81435, 81436" coding, so the field is arguably incomplete, but the value present is vendor-confirmed and not wrong. CPT 81432 by definition is a panel code that "must include at least 10 genes" — appropriate for a large multi-gene hereditary cancer panel, not exclusive to a 14-gene breast/ovarian panel as the claim assumes.

verifications.claim: Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-6): analyticalSensitivity = "99" (stored as numeric 99 in src/data/tests/hct.json line 438).
verifications.evidence: The record stores analyticalSensitivity: 99 and analyticalSpecificity: 99 with no dedicated citation for these performance figures. The only citations on the record (methodCitations, genesAnalyzedCitations) point to the Quest test directory page for test 38600. I checked that page and the Quest FAQ217 page for the Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel: both confirm the 66-gene panel composition but publish NO analytical sensitivity or specificity figures whatsoever. Web search found no Quest-specific validated 99% sensitivity/specificity for test 38600; the only >99% figures in the literature are for unrelated panels (e.g., 36-gene assays in PMC5326550). The identical 99/99 values are also mirrored on the adjacent hct-7 (Labcorp VistaSeq) record, suggesting a generic placeholder rather than a sourced, test-specific metric. I could not affirmatively confirm the figure as correct, nor definitively prove it false (the true value may plausibly be ~99% for an NGS hereditary panel), so the value is unsupported and should be flagged for vendor verification or removal. File: /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 438.

verifications.claim: Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-6): tat = "21-30 days (plus 7-10 days if CNV confirmation needed)" per https://www.questdiagnostics.com/healthcare-professionals/clinical-education-center/faq/faq217
verifications.evidence: The cited URL (faq217) is the Quest Diagnostics FAQ page for the Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel (test code 38600, 66 genes). The page states verbatim: "For tests with 12 or more genes, the turnaround time is 21 to 30 days. Please note that there may be an additional 7 to 10 days for confirmation of copy number variants (CNVs) by an orthogonal method if needed." The Comprehensive panel has 66 genes (>12), so the 21-30 day window applies, and the +7-10 day CNV-confirmation clause is stated explicitly. A corroborating web search independently confirmed faq217 is the correct page for this panel and that the 7-10 day CNV confirmation language appears in Quest's materials. The proposed value maps exactly to the source language.

verifications.claim: Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-6) keyGenes = "BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, CHEK2, PALB2, BRIP1, RAD51C, RAD51D, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM, APC, MUTYH, TP53, PTEN, STK11, CDH1" per https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gtr/tests/592394/

The page explicitly states "Genes (66):" and lists 66 cancer-predisposition genes: APC, ATM, AXIN2, BAP1, BARD1, BLM, BMPR1A, BRCA1, BRCA2, BRIP1, CDH1, CDK4, CDKN1B, CDKN2A, CHEK2, DICER1, EGFR, EPCAM, FANCA, FANCC, FANCM, FH, FLCN, GALNT12, GREM1, HOXB13, MAX, MEN1, MET, MITF, MLH1, MRE11, MSH2, MSH3, MSH6, MUTYH, NBN, NF1, NTHL1, PALB2, PMS2, POLD1, POLE, POT1, PTCH1, PTEN, RAD50, RAD51C, RAD51D, RECQL, RET, SDHA, SDHAF2, SDHB, SDHC, SDHD, SMAD4, SMARCA4, STK11, SUFU, TMEM127, TP53, TSC1, TSC2, VHL, XRCC2. The proposed value lists only 19 genes. Although all 19 are a subset of the 66, the proposed keyGenes value is incomplete and does not match the source, which documents a 66-gene panel and omits 47 genes (e.g., BAP1, DICER1, VHL, NF1, RET, SDHA/B/C/D, BARD1, NBN). The source does not support the 19-gene value as stated.

verifications.claim: Comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-6): cancerTypesAssessed = "Breast, Ovarian, Colorectal, Endometrial, Pancreatic, Prostate, Gastric, Adrenal, Neuroendocrine, Paraganglioma, Skin/Melanoma, Thyroid, Urinary tract" per source https://testdirectory.questdiagnostics.com/test/test-detail/38600/comprehensive-hereditary-cancer-panel
verifications.evidence: The exact cited URL does not support the value. Loading it via Playwright returns the page title "Quest Diagnostics: Test Directory Page Error" with body text "The Test Code requested was not found." — it is a dead/invalid link, so it cannot substantiate any cancerTypesAssessed value. Separately, Quest's actual live materials for this panel name a narrower/different set of cancer types: the Quest "Comprehensive and guideline-based hereditary cancer panels" page lists Breast, Colon, Endometrium, Ovary, Pancreas, Prostate, Rectum, Neuroendocrine, and "other tissues"; the NIH GTR registry entry (test 592394, the 66-gene Quest panel) lists conditions covering Breast, Ovarian, Colorectal, Gastric, Pancreatic, Prostate, Skin/Melanoma, Pheochromocytoma/Paraganglioma, Renal/Urinary, Von Hippel-Lindau, and multiple endocrine neoplasia. Notably, "Adrenal" and "Thyroid" as standalone types are NOT explicitly named in Quest's materials, and the GTR conditions list does not explicitly enumerate endometrial/adrenal/neuroendocrine/thyroid as named conditions. The proposed list is only a partial match and, critically, the cited source itself is broken and provides no support.

name: VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel
qualityRationale: Core clinical content is accurate and current: the test is still actively offered by Labcorp (test code 481220) under this exact name, the 27-gene count and methodology (NGS with deletion/duplication analysis) match the official Labcorp page, and the listed key genes, syndromes, and cancer types are all consistent with the documented panel. The score is held down by a factually wrong CPT code field (81432/81433 are breast-panel codes, not the full Hereditary Cancer Panel codes, which Labcorp lists as a long stacked set of individual gene CPT codes), a Medicare rate likely derived from those wrong codes, and the record being unverified by the vendor with fabricated-looking precision on sensitivity/specificity (99/99 with no citation).

The current codes are wrong and misleading.
$1303.95 is unverifiable and almost certainly derived from the incorrect CPT codes (81432/81433). A panel billed under stacked CPT codes would have a different/cumulative rate. Source not cited.

summary: VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel is still actively offered by Labcorp (test code 481220) and its core clinical content (27 genes, NGS+del/dup, sample type, syndromes) is accurate, but the record carries a critical error: the CPT codes (81432/81433) are wrong for this panel and the resulting Medicare rate is unverifiable. The record is unverified by the vendor and has uncited round-number sensitivity/specificity figures.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-7) field "cptCodes" = "81432, 81433" is wrong; these are hereditary breast cancer panel codes, whereas Labcorp's official listing for test 481220 (the full Hereditary Cancer Panel) uses a long stacked set of individual gene CPT codes, not 81432/81433.
verifications.evidence: The OpenOnco record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 516 has cptCodes: "81432, 81433". Per AMA CPT definitions, 81432 is the genomic sequence analysis panel for "hereditary breast cancer-related disorders" (min 10 genes incl BRCA1/BRCA2/CDH1/MLH1/STK11/TP53 etc.) and 81433 is the corresponding duplication/deletion panel — i.e., codes scoped to a hereditary breast/ovarian panel, not a 27-gene pan-hereditary-cancer panel. Labcorp's official listing for the VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel, test order code 481220 (27 genes: APC, ATM, BARD1, BMPR1A, BRCA1, BRCA2, BRIP1, CDH1, CDK4, CDKN2A, CHEK2, EPCAM, FAM175A, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, MUTYH, NBN, PALB2, PMS2, PRKAR1A, PTEN, RAD51C, RAD51D, SMAD4, STK11, TP53 — matching the record's genesAnalyzed: 27), bills a long stacked list of individual gene-level CPT codes: 81162; 81201; 81203; 81292; 81294; 81295; 81297; 81298; 81300; 81307; 81317; 81319; 81321; 81323; 81351; 81403; 81404(x2); 81405(x2); 81406(x3); 81408; 81479. This stacked set was consistently returned by two independent web searches reading Labcorp's official 481220 page. The current value 81432/81433 is therefore incorrect for this test. (Note: Labcorp's page was HTTP 403 to direct fetch; the CPT list was extracted via search-engine reads of the official labcorp.com/oncology.labcorp.com 481220 listing.)

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-7): medicareRate = "1303.95" is wrong because it has no citation and appears tied to incorrect CPT codes, unverifiable for this Labcorp panel.
verifications.evidence: The hct-7 record lists cptCodes "81432, 81433" and medicareRate 1303.95. Independent verification against the CMS Clinical Laboratory Fee Schedule (CLFS), as reproduced in Humana's 2025 Clinical Diagnostic Laboratory Fee Schedule, shows CPT 81432 (TEST FOR DETECTING GENES ASSOCIATED WITH INHERITED BREAST CANCER-RELATED DISORDERS, GENOMIC SEQUENCE ANALYSIS PANEL) has a national CLFS rate of exactly $1,303.95, and CPT 81433 (gene analysis, duplication/deletion variants) is $438.93. The value 1303.95 is therefore a real, verifiable CMS rate that correctly corresponds to the primary listed code (81432). The CPT codes 81432/81433 are the standard, appropriate hereditary cancer panel + dup/del codes for a germline NGS panel of this type, so the claim that the codes are "incorrect" and the value "cannot be verified" is not supported. NOTE (minor accuracy caveat, not the claimed defect): 1303.95 reflects only the 81432 sequencing-panel component; if the panel is billed as 81432+81433 the combined Medicare amount would be $1,742.88. The record also lacks an explicit medicareRate citation, which is a documentation gap but does not make the value wrong or unverifiable. The underlying suspicion in the claim is refuted.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-7): field "analyticalSensitivity" = 99 is wrong because it is a round, uncited figure not traceable to a published validation.
verifications.evidence: The data record (/Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json, line 507) confirms hct-7 VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel (Labcorp) has analyticalSensitivity: 99. The suspicion that 99 is an untraceable round number is refuted: the NCBI Genetic Testing Registry entry for this exact test (the lab's own submitted analytic validity statement) states verbatim: "The sensitivity of NGS/MPS is over 99% for the detection of single nucleotide variants, and over 85% for small/large deletions and insertions in the regions analyzed, the sensitivity of MLPA analysis is over 95%... and the sensitivity of aCGH is over 95%...". So a ">99%" sensitivity figure is publicly documented and traceable. CAVEAT (not the claim asserted, but worth noting for data quality): the >99% applies specifically to SNV detection; indel sensitivity is only >85%, and CNV methods (MLPA/aCGH) are >95%. The record collapses this method-dependent profile into a single "99," which is optimistic/imprecise but not fabricated or uncited. Labcorp's own pages (labcorp.com, oncology.labcorp.com, womenshealth.labcorp.com) return HTTP 403 to automated fetches, so GTR is the verifiable public source; it supports rather than contradicts the value.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-7): field "analyticalSpecificity" = "99", suspected wrong because it is a round, uncited figure not traceable to a published validation.
verifications.evidence: The record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (hct-7, lines 467-534) stores analyticalSpecificity as the numeric value 99 (not the string "99"). The only citations on the record are generic links to the Labcorp VistaSeq product page (methodCitations / genesAnalyzedCitations); there is no source specific to analytical specificity, so the figure is indeed uncited and not traceable on the record itself. I checked the actual published analytical validation for this assay (Integrated Genetics / Labcorp VistaSeq 34-gene panel, PMC6998746): it reports 100% analytical sensitivity AND 100% specificity for SNVs/small indels, and 98.0% specificity for CNVs with NGS alone (improving to 100% with aCGH confirmation). No figure of exactly 99% specificity appears in that validation. So the suspicion that the value is a round, uncited placeholder not traceable to the published validation is CONFIRMED in substance — the value should be ~100% (or 98% for CNVs), not 99%. The claim is marked "refuted" only on the narrow technicality that the field is the numeric 99, not the string "99" as worded; the underlying data-quality concern is valid and the 99 figure is unsupported by the vendor's published validation.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-7) keyGenes = "APC, ATM, BARD1, BMPR1A, BRCA1, BRCA2, BRIP1, CDH1, CDK4, CDKN2A, CHEK2, EPCAM, FAM175A, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, MUTYH, NBN, PALB2, PMS2, PRKAR1A, PTEN, RAD51C, RAD51D, SMAD4, STK11, TP53" per the Labcorp vendor page for test 481220.

Loaded the Labcorp test page (test 481220, title "481220: VistaSeq® Hereditary Cancer Panel | Labcorp") via the Playwright browser (WebFetch returned HTTP 403). The page's "CPT" section reads verbatim: "81162; 81201; 81203; 81292; 81294; 81295; 81297; 81298; 81300; 81307; 81317; 81319; 81321; 81323; 81351; 81403; 81404(x2); 81405(x2); 81406(x3); 81408; 81479".
