verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-7) cancerTypesAssessed = "Breast, Ovarian, Melanoma, Pancreatic, Colorectal, Endometrial, Gastric, Prostate" per https://oncology.labcorp.com/tests/481220/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer-panel
verifications.evidence: I rendered the exact cited URL via a headless browser (WebFetch returned 403; the page is behind Cloudflare bot protection). The page's "CANCER TYPE" field reads verbatim: "Breast, Colorectal cancer (CRC), Endometrial, Gastric, Hereditary breast and ovarian, Hereditary cancer, Ovarian, Prostate, Skin". The proposed value does not match this. Specifically: the word "pancreatic" does NOT appear anywhere on the page, and "melanoma" does NOT appear (the page lists "Skin" instead). Six of the eight proposed terms (Breast, Ovarian, Colorectal, Endometrial, Gastric, Prostate) are present, but Pancreatic and Melanoma are not supported by this source, and the page also lists "Hereditary breast and ovarian"/"Hereditary cancer" which the proposed value omits. Note: a general descriptive sentence using the phrase "such as breast, ovarian, melanoma, pancreatic, colorectal, endometrial, gastric, and prostate cancers" exists on OTHER VistaSeq pages on the labcorp.com domain (e.g. the oncology.labcorp.com/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer landing page), but it is NOT on the specifically cited test-detail page 481220. So the cited source as given does not support the value.

name: xG / xG+ Hereditary Cancer
qualityRationale: The test is still offered under the xG / xG+ names and the high-level framing (germline panel paired with xT, NGS+del/dup, blood/saliva, ~2 week TAT, US, BRCA/Lynch genes) is broadly correct. But the record is anchored to the 2021 GeneDx era and is materially stale on its two most distinctive facts: the performing/partner lab and the gene counts. As of March 2024 Tempus moved germline to Ambry Genetics (CancerNext / CancerNext-Expanded), and Tempus acquired Ambry outright on Feb 3, 2025. The record (added 2026-01-08, vendorVerified:false) still says GeneDx performs the testing and lists 52/88 genes, whereas current Ambry-powered panels are ~40 (xG) / 77-78 (xG+). These are critical factual errors for a record dated 2026. Several quantitative claims (analyticalSensitivity 99, specificity 99.9, selfPayPrice $250) are unsourced/unverifiable from the cited vendor page.

summary: The xG / xG+ test is still offered under this name but the OpenOnco record (added Jan 2026, unverified) is anchored to the retired 2021 GeneDx era: it wrongly states GeneDx performs the germline testing and lists 52/88 genes, whereas Tempus moved germline to Ambry Genetics (CancerNext ~40 / CancerNext-Expanded 77-78 genes) in March 2024 and acquired Ambry in Feb 2025. Several quantitative claims (sensitivity 99%, specificity 99.9%, $250 self-pay) are unsourced and need verification.

verifications.claim: xG / xG+ Hereditary Cancer (hct-8): fields "uniqueFeatures" and "limitations" reference GeneDx as the germline partner lab ("germline analysis performed by partner lab (GeneDx)" / "Germline testing outsourced to GeneDx"), suspected obsolete because Tempus moved to Ambry Genetics in March 2024 and acquired Ambry in early 2025.
verifications.evidence: The hct-8 record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 595-596) does contain "germline analysis performed by partner lab (GeneDx)" in uniqueFeatures and "Germline testing outsourced to GeneDx" in limitations. The GeneDx reference was accurate at xG's 2022 launch (Tempus's own xG launch material historically named GeneDx as the performing lab). However, Ambry Genetics' press release dated March 12, 2024 announced a collaboration in which "Ambry is performing germline sequencing assays for Tempus, powered by Ambry's CancerNext and CancerNext-Expanded assays." Tempus then announced on Nov 4, 2024 a $600M ($375M cash + $225M shares) agreement to acquire Ambry, describing Ambry as currently serving as "Tempus' main reference lab in the [hereditary cancer] category"; the acquisition closed Q1 2025. The current germline reference lab for Tempus hereditary cancer testing is therefore Ambry (now Tempus-owned), making the GeneDx reference obsolete. Both fields should be updated. (Minor caveat: I found no source using the literal word "replaced GeneDx," but the Nov 2024 statement that Ambry is currently the main reference lab is mutually exclusive with a current GeneDx arrangement.)

verifications.claim: hct-8 "xG / xG+ Hereditary Cancer" (Tempus): genesAnalyzed/genesAnalyzedNotes = "52 (xG) / 88 (xG+)" is wrong/outdated; current panels are ~40 (xG) and ~77 (xG+).
verifications.evidence: The OpenOnco hct-8 record (src/data/tests/hct.json) is a TEMPUS product, not Ambry directly: genesAnalyzed=52, genesAnalyzedNotes="xG = 52 genes; xG+ = 88 genes", and the record still names GeneDx as the partner germline lab. The 52/88 counts are the 2022 GeneDx-era configuration (confirmed by Tempus-xG_Overview.pdf dated Sept 2022 and University of Michigan MLabs mirror pages, which still state 52 and 88 genes — these are stale). Current Tempus official sources (tempus.com hereditary-cancer-testing and genomic-profiling/xg pages, plus 2025-dated Hereditary xG requisition forms) consistently state: xG = 40-gene panel (= Ambry CancerNext) and xG+ = 77-gene panel (= Ambry CancerNext-Expanded). Tempus hereditary testing is now performed by Ambry Genetics, a Tempus company (collaboration announced March 2024, Ambry now a Tempus subsidiary), replacing the GeneDx arrangement. So the claim's gene-count premise (40 / 77-78) is correct, though it slightly mis-framed the vendor: xG/xG+ is the Tempus brand for the Ambry CancerNext / CancerNext-Expanded panels. Two corrections are warranted on the record: (1) update gene counts to 40/77, and (2) update the partner-lab narrative from GeneDx to Ambry Genetics. Conflicting stale source: MLabs pages still report 52/88, but these mirror the discontinued GeneDx-era panel.

verifications.claim: xG / xG+ Hereditary Cancer (hct-8): field "selfPayPrice" currently = "250". Suspected wrong because no current public Tempus/Ambry source lists a $250 self-pay price for xG.
verifications.evidence: Verified the record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json: hct-8 = "xG / xG+ Hereditary Cancer", vendor Tempus, selfPayPrice = 250, with NO selfPayPriceCitations or pricing note. Web research found no public Tempus or Ambry source listing a $250 self-pay price for xG. The closest public figure is $249: Mercy (a Tempus hereditary-testing partner) states verbatim on its Hereditary Cancer Test FAQ page, "The self-pay cost is $249." Tempus's own hereditary-testing pages (tempus.com/oncology/hereditary-cancer-testing/, access.tempus.com) describe a self-pay option and financial assistance but publish no flat dollar price; the Tempus product page could not be fetched (HTTP 429) but search snippets surfaced no $250 figure. Conclusion: the stored value "250" does not match any public source — the documented figure is $249, so the field is incorrect as written (uncited and off by $1, likely a rounded transcription of $249). It should be corrected to 249 with a citation, or have the value sourced/removed.

verifications.claim: xG / xG+ Hereditary Cancer (hct-8): analyticalSensitivity = 99 is wrong because it is not stated on the cited vendor page and appears to be a generic asserted value.
verifications.evidence: Record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 574 sets analyticalSensitivity: 99, citing methodCitations/genesAnalyzedCitations https://www.tempus.com/oncology/genetic-testing/hereditary-cancer/. The citation-quality part of the claim has merit: that cited Tempus page (HTTP 403 to automated fetch) is a marketing/overview page and searches did not surface any 99% analytical-sensitivity figure on it; the gene counts on Tempus pages (xG=40-gene CancerNext, xG+=77-gene CancerNext-Expanded) also differ from the record's 52/88. HOWEVER, the numeric value itself is NOT refuted. Tempus xG/xG+ germline testing is performed by Ambry Genetics (a Tempus company) using the CancerNext and CancerNext-Expanded assays, and Ambry publicly states analytical sensitivity of ">99% of described mutations" for CancerNext and ">99.9%" for CancerNext-Expanded. So ~99% is substantively corroborated by real public vendor documentation — just from Ambry's pages, not the cited Tempus URL. Verdict is uncertain rather than refuted: the value is plausible/corroborated, but the specific cited source does not substantiate it (citation should point to Ambry CancerNext performance data). Separately noted: the record's uniqueFeatures/limitations wrongly state germline testing is outsourced to GeneDx; the actual partner lab is Ambry Genetics.

verifications.claim: xG / xG+ Hereditary Cancer (hct-8): analyticalSpecificity = "99.9"
verifications.evidence: The field is confirmed present (analyticalSpecificity: 99.9, hct.json line 575), but the cited source does not substantiate it. The cited vendor URL (tempus.com/oncology/genetic-testing/hereditary-cancer/) now returns 404 "Page Not Found". The current live Tempus hereditary/xG page (tempus.com/solutions/hereditary/, redirected from the xG product path) contains NO analytical specificity figure at all — no "99.9", no "specificity", and no sensitivity values anywhere in the page text. WebSearch surfaced a ">99.9% specificity" figure only for Tempus's SOMATIC xT assay and LIQUID xF assay — not the germline xG hereditary panel — indicating the 99.9 was likely borrowed from a different Tempus product. Strong corroborating red flag for a generic asserted value: the identical analyticalSpecificity 99.9 (and analyticalSensitivity 99) appears verbatim on hct-9, a completely different vendor's panel (GeneDx Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel). No public Tempus source publishes an analytical specificity for the germline xG/xG+ test. The value is unsupported by the cited page and appears to be a generic/copied figure.

verifications.claim: xG / xG+ Hereditary Cancer (hct-8): genesAnalyzed = "40", per Ambry Genetics press release #148 (vendor_page).
verifications.evidence: The cited source — the Ambry Genetics press release announcing the Tempus collaboration (press-release/148) — does NOT contain the value "40" or any gene count at all. Two independent fetches of the page confirm it only states that Ambry will perform germline sequencing using its CancerNext and CancerNext-Expanded assays for Tempus's xG and xG+ tests, and that these "identify genetic variants associated with hereditary cancer syndromes," with no panel sizes given. The page is a business-collaboration announcement, not a test spec. Separately, the "40" figure does appear plausible: Tempus's own materials describe xG (CancerNext) as a 40-gene guidelines-based panel and xG+ (CancerNext-Expanded) as ~77-gene. So the VALUE may be correct, but the SPECIFIC CITED SOURCE does not support it — and notably the press release maps both xG and xG+ to two different panels (CancerNext / CancerNext-Expanded), so attributing a single "40" to this URL conflates the two products. The citation does not substantiate the claimed value.

verifications.claim: xG / xG+ Hereditary Cancer (hct-8) genesAnalyzedNotes = "xG = 40-gene guidelines-based panel (Ambry CancerNext); xG+ = 77-78 gene panel (Ambry CancerNext-Expanded). Germline assays powered by Ambry Genetics (Tempus company) as of March 2024." per https://www.tempus.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/Tempus-Onco_Germline-Assay-Guide-Ambry.pdf
verifications.evidence: The panel descriptions are largely correct and supported by Tempus's own materials: xG is a 40-gene guidelines-based germline panel performed by Ambry as CancerNext, and xG+ is the expanded panel performed as CancerNext-Expanded (Tempus states 77 genes; the claim's "77-78" slightly overstates but is within rounding). Ambry Genetics powering the germline sequencing is also correct. HOWEVER, the claim's assertion that Ambry was a "Tempus company as of March 2024" is factually false. Public record: Tempus only ANNOUNCED an agreement to acquire Ambry Genetics from REALM IDx on November 4, 2024 (Tempus Q3 2024 release / BusinessWire), and the acquisition did not COMPLETE until February 3, 2025 (BusinessWire "Tempus Completes Acquisition of Ambry Genetics," Feb 2025). In March 2024, Tempus and Ambry had only a commercial collaboration (GenomeWeb), not ownership. So the "Tempus company as of March 2024" timing is wrong by roughly a year and mischaracterizes the relationship. Because the proposed value embeds this refutable factual error, it is not accurately supported. The cited source PDF itself could not be fetched directly (HTTP 429/403), so the source was not independently confirmed to contain this exact wording; verification relied on Tempus's hereditary-testing pages and acquisition press releases. Recommended correction: change "(Tempus company) as of March 2024" to reflect that Ambry became a Tempus company in February 2025 (acquisition completed; agreement Nov 2024), and that the Tempus-Ambry germline collaboration predates the acquisition.

verifications.claim: xG / xG+ Hereditary Cancer (hct-8) uniqueFeatures = "Pairs with Tempus xT tumor profiling for combined somatic+germline ordering; germline assays powered by Ambry Genetics, a Tempus company (acquired Feb 2025), using CancerNext and CancerNext-Expanded; optional +RNAinsight RNA add-on for variant classification." sourced to Ambry press release #148.
verifications.evidence: The cited source (Ambry press release #148, dated March 12, 2024) supports only PART of the proposed value and contradicts or fails to support key specifics. SUPPORTED: germline assays "powered by Ambry's CancerNext® and CancerNext-Expanded® assays"; paired germline+somatic testing concept; Ambry as "a wholly owned subsidiary of Tempus." NOT SUPPORTED/CONTRADICTED: (1) "Tempus xT tumor profiling" — the page never names xT; it references only "Tempus' xG and xG+ germline assays," so the xT-pairing claim is unsupported by this source. (2) "acquired Feb 2025" — this March-2024 source states no acquisition date whatsoever; the Feb 3 2025 completion date comes from other Tempus/BusinessWire releases, not this page (and is internally inconsistent with a March-2024 page already calling Ambry a wholly owned subsidiary). (3) "optional +RNAinsight RNA add-on for variant classification" — RNAinsight appears only as a navigation/menu item ("concurrent DNA/RNA Testing") with no functional or variant-classification description in the release body, so the stated purpose is unsupported. Because the proposed value asserts several specifics this source does not back, the source does not support the value as written.

verifications.claim: xG / xG+ Hereditary Cancer (hct-8): proposed method = "NGS with deletion/duplication analysis; optional +RNAinsight RNA analysis (Ambry Genetics)" per https://www.tempus.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/Tempus-Onco_Germline-Assay-Guide-Ambry.pdf (vendor_page).
verifications.evidence: The cited source could not be verified directly: the URL https://www.tempus.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/Tempus-Onco_Germline-Assay-Guide-Ambry.pdf redirects to media.tempus.com/legacy_content/... and returns HTTP 404 with an S3 error body ("NoSuchKey / The specified key does not exist"), confirmed via curl and by rendering the page in a real browser (Playwright). So I could not read the PDF body to confirm it states this exact methodology, including the "deletion/duplication analysis" wording. That said, the document is real (Google's index returns it as "TMP-00087_2025-09 ... Germline Assay Guide," Tempus's official Ambry-powered germline assay guide), and the substance of the claim is corroborated by independent official sources: Ambry performs Tempus's germline sequencing using next-generation sequencing CancerNext (xG) and CancerNext-Expanded (xG+) assays (Ambry/Tempus press releases, GenomeWeb), and +RNAinsight is explicitly an optional RNA-analysis add-on for xG/xG+ "powered by Ambry Genetics" that "analyzes RNA to support the classification and detection of DNA variants" (Tempus's own statements). The NGS-with-del/dup framing is consistent with Ambry's standard hereditary-cancer NGS panels (which include CNV/del-dup analysis), but the specific "deletion/duplication" phrase was not found in any readable snippet, and the named source itself is currently a dead 404 that I could not open. Verdict is uncertain rather than confirmed because the cited source does not resolve and its body could not be inspected; it is not refuted because the value is substantially supported by adjacent official Tempus/Ambry evidence.

name: Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel
qualityRationale: The record is internally well-populated and the gene count (49), methodology, and CPT codes match the GeneDx TIS-B275 source. However it is materially out of date and contains at least one factually false claim. GeneDx discontinued hereditary/germline cancer testing effective Sept 30, 2025 (last order date Sept 16, 2025 for the Common Cancer Management Panel line), so this test is no longer purchasable under this name/vendor. Tempus acquired Ambry Genetics (Feb 2025) and Tempus xG/xG+ germline panels are now powered by Ambry's CancerNext/CancerNext-Expanded, NOT GeneDx, making the uniqueFeatures 'powers Tempus xG germline testing' claim now false. Score reflects a record whose core specs were accurate but whose availability/status fields are stale and whose differentiator claim is wrong as of June 2026.

Claims the test 'powers Tempus xG germline testing.' This is no longer true: Tempus acquired Ambry Genetics (Feb 2025) and Tempus xG/xG+ is now powered by Ambry's CancerNext/CancerNext-Expanded panels, not GeneDx.

medicareEffective '2025-Q4' and 'Broad Coverage' imply active commercial offering in late 2025, contradicting the Sept 30 2025 discontinuation; reimbursement currency is unverifiable for a retired test.
vendorVerified is false and vendorRequestedChanges only notes the 2026-01-08 category-launch import; no independent verification of any field against vendor.

Status, reimbursement, and uniqueFeatures fields need correction/retirement.

verifications.claim: Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel (hct-9): uniqueFeatures = "Expertise in rare diseases; often handles complex cases; powers Tempus xG germline testing" is stale/false because Tempus xG germline testing is now powered by Ambry Genetics, not GeneDx.
verifications.evidence: The hct-9 record in /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (line 668) is a GeneDx test whose uniqueFeatures reads exactly: "Expertise in rare diseases; often handles complex cases; powers Tempus xG germline testing" — matching the claim verbatim. Public evidence refutes the GeneDx attribution: Ambry Genetics' March 12, 2024 press release states "Ambry is performing germline sequencing assays for Tempus, powered by Ambry's CancerNext and CancerNext-Expanded assays." Tempus subsequently acquired Ambry, making Ambry (a Tempus company) the germline provider for xG. GeneDx was the original xG germline partner historically, so the field is outdated rather than ever fabricated, but as of 2024-2026 it is false. Recommend correcting/removing the "powers Tempus xG germline testing" clause. (Note: the same stale GeneDx-powers-Tempus assertion also appears nearby at line 595-596 in the same file for the related GeneDx record.)

verifications.claim: Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel (hct-9): genesAnalyzed = 49 is wrong.
verifications.evidence: The claim that the 49 gene count is wrong is itself refuted. The OpenOnco hct-9 record cites GeneDx TIS-B275 as the source, and the authoritative vendor primary source confirms 49. I fetched and extracted the actual GeneDx PDF (TIS-B275, the exact citation in the record). It is titled "OncoGeneDx: Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel" and states verbatim, twice: "The OncoGeneDx Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel offered at GeneDx includes analysis of 49 genes" and "the clinical sensitivity of sequencing and deletion/duplication analysis of the 49 genes included in the OncoGeneDx Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel." The printed PANEL GENE LIST contains exactly 49 genes: APC, ATM, AXIN2, BAP1, BARD1, BMPR1A, BRCA1, BRCA2, BRIP1, CDH1, CDK4, CDKN2A, CHEK2, CTNNA1, EPCAM, FANCC, FANCM, FH, FLCN, HOXB13, MET, MITF, MLH1, MSH2, MSH3, MSH6, MUTYH, NBN, NF1, NTHL1, PALB2, PMS2, POLD1, POLE, POT1, PTEN, RAD51C, RAD51D, RECQL, SCG5/GREM1, SDHB, SDHC, SDHD, SMAD4, STK11, TP53, TSC1, TSC2, VHL (EPCAM and SCG5/GREM1 are del/dup-only, but still counted in the 49). The "29" and "46" figures in the suspicion refer to different GeneDx products (e.g. Common Cancer Management Panel TIS-B751, Custom Cancer Panel TIS-B749) or inaccurate third-party aggregations, not this panel. The record value of 49 matches the cited primary source exactly. Record file: /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json.

verifications.claim: Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel (hct-9): field "reimbursement" = "Broad Coverage" is stale/wrong because the test was discontinued 2025-09-30 and reimbursement cannot be verified as current.
verifications.evidence: The suspected discontinuation is independently confirmed: GeneDx is exiting hereditary cancer testing. The last day to order the Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel was September 16, 2025, with samples required to be received by September 30, 2025, or the order is cancelled. This is corroborated by a Northwell Health Laboratories discontinuation notice dated 09-30-2025 (which points clients to Natera as the replacement) and by the GeneDx provider portal showing only "Rest of" / management variants of the panel remaining. Given the test is discontinued as of 2025-09-30 (over 8 months ago relative to the current date 2026-06-02), it is no longer orderable or reimbursable, so the record's reimbursement: "Broad Coverage" (notes "Insurance/Medicare") is stale and materially misleading. The data integrity problem extends beyond that single field: the hct-9 record (/Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json) contains NO discontinuation flag and instead presents the test as fully active — medicareStatus: "Priced", medicareEffective: "2025-Q4", availableRegions: ["US"], vendorVerified: false, with the only changelog note being "2026-01-08: Added via OpenOnco HCT category launch" (i.e., the record was added ~3 months AFTER the test was discontinued, with no awareness of the discontinuation). Recommendation: flag the test as discontinued and either remove or clearly date-stamp the reimbursement/Medicare/availability fields as historical.

verifications.claim: Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel (hct-9): field "medicareEffective" = "2025-Q4" is wrong because it implies active Medicare pricing in a quarter overlapping/following the product's discontinuation, inconsistent with the test's retirement.
verifications.evidence:
The record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (hct-9, line 655) does contain medicareEffective: "2025-Q4", with medicareStatus: "Priced", reimbursement: "Broad Coverage", and no discontinuation/retirement flag — presenting the GeneDx Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel as actively available and Medicare-priced into Q4 2025 (Oct–Dec 2025).

Public evidence contradicts this. GeneDx retired the Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel: the last day to order was September 16, 2025, with samples required to be received by September 30, 2025 or orders cancelled. This is corroborated by a Northwell (NWH Labs) "Discontinued Test / Test Change 09-30-2025" hereditary-cancer notice. Thus the test was no longer orderable before Q4 2025 even began, making a "2025-Q4" Medicare-effective designation internally inconsistent with the product's retirement, and the surrounding active-pricing fields stale.

Additional independent inconsistency: the recorded CMS rate ($1,303.95 for CPT 81432) carries a CLFS effective date of 1/1/2020 (CLFS rates set on 3-year cycles), so "2025-Q4" does not correspond to any actual CMS effective period either. Both the discontinuation timeline and the CMS-date mismatch indicate the medicareEffective value is wrong.

verifications.claim: Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel (hct-9): stillOffered = "Discontinued — GeneDx exited hereditary cancer testing effective 2025-09-30 (last order date 2025-09-16, samples due by 2025-09-30)." per https://nwhlabs.northwell.edu/sites/northwell.edu/files/2025-10/hereditary-cancer-testing-discontinued-test-test-change-09-30-2025.pdf
verifications.evidence: The source PDF (Northwell Health Laboratories "Discontinued Test" notice) directly supports the core claim. Extracted text reads: "NOTIFICATION DATE: 09/30/2025  EFFECTIVE DATE: 09/30/2025 ... On the effective date, GeneDx Laboratories will discontinue their hereditary cancer testing. Northwell Health Laboratories has several alternative testing options performed by Natera Laboratories." This confirms GeneDx exited hereditary cancer testing effective 2025-09-30. The discontinued GeneDx panels are tabulated, including the comprehensive 81-gene panel (PDM 1659841, "Comprehensive Cancer Panel (81 Genes)") — GeneDx catalogs the exact test "Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel" (providers.genedx.com/.../comprehensive-common-cancer-panel-778), which falls within this discontinued line. The last-order date (2025-09-16) and sample-due date (2025-09-30) were NOT present in my own pdftotext extraction (likely in an image/header region), but WebSearch's index of this same Northwell PDF returns: "The last day to order this test is September 16, 2025. Samples must be received by September 30, 2025, or your order will be canceled." All three dates and the discontinuation fact are attributable to the cited source. Minor caveat: the literal panel name "Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel" does not appear verbatim in the PDF table (which lists "Common Cancer Management Panel (53 Genes)" and "Comprehensive Cancer Panel (81 Genes)"), but the discontinuation applies to GeneDx's entire hereditary cancer line, so the stillOffered value holds regardless of exact panel-name string.

verifications.claim: Comprehensive Common Cancer Panel (hct-9): uniqueFeatures = "Tempus xG/xG+ germline hereditary cancer testing is now powered by Ambry Genetics (CancerNext / CancerNext-Expanded) following Tempus's 2025 acquisition of Ambry, not GeneDx." — cited source: ambrygen.com press release #148 (vendor_page).
verifications.evidence: The cited source supports only PART of the value and contradicts its central causal/temporal framing. The press release (Ambry #148) is dated March 12, 2024 and describes a "strategic collaboration," NOT an acquisition. It confirms the Ambry-powering claim: "Ambry is performing germline sequencing assays for Tempus, powered by Ambry's CancerNext and CancerNext-Expanded assays," and references xG/xG+ ("our germline testing and variant assessment expertise behind Tempus xG and xG+"). However: (1) The proposed value says the Ambry-powered arrangement is "following Tempus's 2025 acquisition of Ambry." The cited source predates that acquisition by ~11 months and instead states Ambry was already "a wholly owned subsidiary of Tempus" at the March 2024 collaboration. The acquisition actually CLOSED Feb 3, 2025 (per Tempus/Nasdaq), which is a separate, later event the cited 2024 press release cannot and does not document — so the source does not support the "following Tempus's 2025 acquisition" causal framing. (2) The "not GeneDx" element (the transition away from GeneDx, which originally ran xG) is corroborated by other coverage but is NOT mentioned anywhere in the cited press release. Net: the underlying facts (Ambry powers xG/xG+ via CancerNext; Tempus acquired Ambry in Feb 2025; xG originated at GeneDx) are individually real, but the specific cited source does NOT support the value as written — it gets the date (2024 collaboration vs claimed 2025 acquisition) and mechanism (collaboration, not acquisition) wrong and omits GeneDx. The source does not actually support this value.

name: Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel
qualityRationale: Core identifying facts (vendor, 154-gene count, NGS + del/dup methodology, 2-3 week TAT, blood/saliva sample, CPT 81432/81433) are accurate and confirmed against Fulgent's official product page and the NCBI GTR entry, and the test is still actively sold under this exact name (also resold via NeoGenomics). The main quality problem is the analytical performance figures: the record states 99.9% sensitivity AND 99.9% specificity, whereas Fulgent's own published numbers are >98% sensitivity, 96% specificity, 97% accuracy — the 99.9% specificity is contradicted by the vendor source. Several commercial/operational fields (Illumina platform, medicareRate $1303.95, NY approval, list price) are unverified against a citable source. vendorVerified:false is correctly set.

verifications.claim: Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel (hct-10): field "analyticalSpecificity" currently = "99.9", suspected wrong because Fulgent's published specificity is 96%.
verifications.evidence: The OpenOnco record (Fulgent Genetics "Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel", hct-10) in /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 725 sets "analyticalSpecificity": 99.9. The authoritative NCBI Genetic Testing Registry (GTR) entry for this exact test (Fulgent, test 531711) states the lab's reported performance: "Analytical sensitivity, specificity and accuracy are >98%, 96% and 97% respectively." A web search summarizing Fulgent's panel documentation independently corroborates the same figures (sensitivity >98%, specificity 96%, accuracy 97%). Therefore the published analytical specificity is 96%, not 99.9% — the field value is wrong and should be 96. (Separately, analyticalSensitivity is recorded as 99.9 vs published >98%, also likely incorrect, but outside this claim's scope.)

verifications.claim: Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel (hct-10): field "analyticalSensitivity" currently = "99.9". Suspected wrong because Fulgent's published sensitivity is >98%; 99.9% is not supported by the vendor source.
verifications.evidence: The record in /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json for hct-10 (Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel, vendor Fulgent Genetics) has analyticalSensitivity: 99.9, analyticalSpecificity: 99.9, and analyticalSensitivityNotes: ">99% at 50x coverage". The cited vendor source is https://www.fulgentgenetics.com/comprehensivecancer-full. The authoritative NCBI GTR registry entry for this exact test (Fulgent's submitted documentation) states verbatim: "Analytical sensitivity, specificity and accuracy are >98%, 96% and 97% respectively." Web search of Fulgent materials corroborates this and adds that panel coverage is ">=99% at 50x" — a coverage-depth metric, not analytical sensitivity. Therefore 99.9 is not supported: the vendor figure for analytical sensitivity is >98% (and specificity 96%, not 99.9). The 99.9 value and ">99% at 50x" note appear to conflate coverage depth with analytical sensitivity/specificity. Claim confirmed: the stored 99.9 is wrong relative to the vendor source.

verifications.claim: Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel (hct-10): field "genesAnalyzed" = 154 is wrong; NCBI GTR test 531711 lists 127 genes for this exact test, and 154 is the customizable marketing figure.
verifications.evidence: The OpenOnco record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 672-685) is hct-10: Fulgent Genetics "Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel" with genesAnalyzed: 154 (stored as an integer, not the string "154" the claim states, but same value). NCBI GTR test 531711 — verified on both the base page (/gtr/tests/531711/) and version page (/gtr/tests/531711.2/) — is the exact same test (name "Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel," lab "Fulgent Genetics") and explicitly lists "Genes (127)" in its At-a-Glance section. The "154 genes" figure traces to Fulgent's broader marketing language describing the Comprehensive Cancer Panels family ("cover 154 genes associated with potential risk for hereditary cancer syndromes"), i.e., the customizable umbrella count rather than the specific 531711 test's analyzed-gene count of 127. The record's genesAnalyzedCitations points only to Fulgent's own marketing page (fulgentgenetics.com/comprehensivecancer-full), not to GTR. Conclusion: 154 is the marketing figure; the authoritative GTR registration for this exact test is 127. The field value is wrong (should be 127). Verdict confirmed.

verifications.claim: Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel (hct-10, Fulgent Genetics): field "nyApproved" = true is correct / confirmed by a cited source.
verifications.evidence: The record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 673-753) sets "nyApproved": true with NO supporting citation field (no nyApprovedCitations), and the only cited URLs on the record (fulgentgenetics.com/comprehensivecancer-full) are bot-protected (HTTP 403) and do not assert NY approval. Public evidence is mixed but does NOT confirm test-level CLEP approval: (1) Fulgent holds a NY State clinical laboratory permit in the category "Genetic Testing - Molecular" since Aug 2019 (GlobeNewswire press release), but that is lab-level licensure, and the release does NOT mention the Comprehensive Cancer Panel or hereditary cancer panels being approved specifically. (2) The NCBI GTR registry page for this exact test (GTR ID 531711) documents NO New York State CLEP/CLIA approval ("FDA Review: Not provided," no state-specific certification listed). (3) I could not retrieve a Fulgent-specific result from the Wadsworth CLEP Approved LDT search to confirm this assay is on NY's approved-test list. So while Fulgent's NY lab permit makes panel approval plausible, no cited source actually confirms test-level NY CLEP approval of this specific panel — matching the claim's stated suspicion. Verdict is uncertain rather than refuted because the lab permit is real and panel approval is likely, just unverified against any cited source. Recommend either adding a verifiable nyApprovedCitations source (Wadsworth approved-LDT entry) or downgrading the flag.

verifications.claim: Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel (hct-10): field "medicareRate" = 1303.95 is wrong/unverified because no CMS citation and CPT 81432/81433 gene-panel pricing is unverified.
verifications.evidence: The record (src/data/tests/hct.json line 673-734) lists cptCodes "81432, 81433" and medicareRate 1303.95 (medicareStatus "Priced", medicareEffective 2025-Q4). Web research confirms 1303.95 is a REAL, attributable figure: the 2025 CMS Clinical Laboratory Fee Schedule (CLFS) national rate for CPT 81432 (hereditary breast cancer genomic sequencing panel, >=5/14 genes) is $1,303.95, and CPT 81433 (deletion/duplication analysis) is $438.93. So the value is not fabricated or wrong — it is exactly the published CLFS rate for the panel's primary code (81432). The claim that the figure is "unverified" is refuted by the CLFS pricing. Two minor caveats remain (documentation gaps, not value errors): (1) the data.js record carries no CMS citation URL for the rate, and (2) because the test bills BOTH 81432 and 81433, the all-in CLFS payment would be ~$1,742.88 (1303.95 + 438.93); 1303.95 captures only the dominant 81432 line. Recommend adding a CLFS citation rather than changing the number.

It states the test is performed "performing full-gene sequencing and deletion/duplication analysis using next-generation sequencing technology (NGS)" and that "this assay achieves >99% analytical sensitivity and specificity for single nucleotide variants, insertions and deletions <15bp in length, and exon-level deletions and duplications." verifications.claim: Invitae Hereditary Breast Cancer Guidelines-Based Panel (hct-12): sampleCategory = "Blood or Saliva" is incomplete because Invitae also accepts buccal swab and extracted genomic DNA as alternate sample types.
verifications.evidence: The record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 792 reads "sampleCategory": "Blood or Saliva". Invitae's official provider specimen/shipping FAQ confirms a broader set of accepted specimen types: it states that "blood, saliva, assisted saliva, and buccal swab collection kits can also be used" and that "We do accept and process DNA specimens extracted in other laboratories for diagnostic and genetic risk testing" (extracted gDNA, e.g. from cultured skin fibroblasts). The web search of Invitae's specimen pages corroborates this: collection kits include blood, saliva, and buccal swabs, and Invitae accepts DNA isolated in other laboratories (with assay limitations). Therefore "Blood or Saliva" is accurate but understates the accepted specimens — it omits buccal swab and extracted genomic DNA. The claim characterizes this as "incomplete, not wrong," which matches the evidence. Note: these specimen requirements are Invitae platform-wide (apply to this panel as an Invitae central-lab service); they are not test-specific overrides. The hct-12 record itself has no granular sampleType field (compare hct-13, which adds one), so the only descriptor present is the incomplete sampleCategory.

verifications.evidence: The hct-12 record (src/data/tests/hct.json) is "Invitae Hereditary Breast Cancer Guidelines-Based Panel" with syndromesDetected exactly = ['HBOC','Li-Fraumeni','Cowden','Peutz-Jeghers','Hereditary Diffuse Gastric Cancer']. The matching vendor product is Invitae test-01206. The vendor page confirms a 13-gene primary panel: ATM, BARD1, BRCA1, BRCA2, CDH1, CHEK2, NF1, PALB2, PTEN, RAD51C, RAD51D, STK11, TP53. Every syndrome in the field maps to a gene actually on the panel: BRCA1/2 -> HBOC; TP53 -> Li-Fraumeni; PTEN -> Cowden; STK11 -> Peutz-Jeghers; CDH1 -> Hereditary Diffuse Gastric Cancer. The "suspected wrong" reasoning is unfounded: STK11 and CDH1 are genuinely included, and PJS/HDGC are their canonical associated syndromes. The field is an accurate reflection of the vendor's gene content. No data change is warranted.

BUT THE "WRONG FIELD" CONCLUSION IS NOT ESTABLISHED (hence uncertain, not confirmed): The product is still actively offered and is literally branded "Invitae Hereditary Pancreatic Cancer Panel" — "Invitae" is the current, accurate product/brand name, not a defunct label. The page describes "Invitae" as the CAP-accredited/CLIA-certified performing laboratory. So listing vendor="Invitae" reflects the real-world brand under which the test is sold; it is not factually false. The legitimate concern is corporate-attribution precision/staleness (operating parent = Labcorp), which is a quality/normalization judgment, not a demonstrable error. Note internal inconsistency: sibling records hct-16/hct-17 (VistaSeq) list vendor="Labcorp" while Invitae-branded panels list "Invitae" — an editorial normalization question, not proof the field is wrong. Recommended remediation if desired: vendor "Labcorp (Invitae)" or add a parent-company note, rather than replacing the brand outright.

This appears to be one of a series of fabricated organ-specific name variants (hct-16 through hct-23 follow the same invented pattern). Vendor and category are correct and the underlying VistaSeq platform is genuinely still offered, but the specific named test is not real, so accuracy is low. The record is also extremely thin (no gene list, no test code, no TAT, no price, no clinical citations, vendorVerified false).

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel (hct-18) name is wrong — Labcorp has no standalone 'Ovarian' VistaSeq panel; ovarian risk is covered under the GYN / Breast and GYN panels and the comprehensive Hereditary Cancer Panel.
verifications.evidence: The record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 915-930) defines hct-18 name="VistaSeq Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel", vendor "Labcorp", with methodCitations pointing to the generic https://oncology.labcorp.com/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer page (not an ovarian-specific page). Public verification against Labcorp's actual catalog shows NO standalone Ovarian panel by this name. Labcorp's real VistaSeq cancer-specific panels each carry distinct test codes: VistaSeq GYN Cancer Panel (test 481330, 11 genes incl. BRCA1/2 — this is the panel that covers ovarian/gynecologic risk), VistaSeq Breast Cancer Panel (481319), VistaSeq Breast and GYN Cancer Panel (481341, 25 genes), VistaSeq Colorectal Cancer Panel (481363) and High Risk Colorectal Cancer Panel (481352), plus the comprehensive 27-gene VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel (481220). The labcatalog.net listing for the Breast and GYN panel explicitly states there is no separate ovarian-specific panel, and Labcorp's GYN panel page confirms ovarian risk is assessed under the GYN panel rather than a dedicated 'Ovarian' product. The hct-18 name does not correspond to any real Labcorp product. NOTE: the same fabrication pattern affects sibling records hct-19 (Endometrial), hct-20 (Gastric), hct-21 (Pancreatic), hct-22 (Prostate), hct-23 (Melanoma) — these single-organ VistaSeq panels do not exist; Labcorp groups its panels as GYN / Breast / Breast+GYN / Colorectal.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel (hct-18): field "cancerTypesAssessed" = ["Ovarian"] is wrong because no real Labcorp SKU is an ovarian-only panel; the closest real product is the VistaSeq GYN Cancer Panel (481330) covering gynecologic cancers (ovarian + uterine/endometrial) collectively, and the comprehensive panel covers many cancers.
verifications.evidence: The OpenOnco record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (line 916-930) has id hct-18, name "VistaSeq Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel", vendor Labcorp, cancerTypesAssessed ["Ovarian"], flagged as a "2026-01-09 batch submission" with vendorVerified:false. Public Labcorp/oncology.labcorp.com test directory shows the REAL VistaSeq site-specific portfolio does NOT include an ovarian-only panel. Labcorp groups ovarian into a GYN panel: test code 481330 "VistaSeq GYN Cancer Panel" (genes BRCA1, BRCA2, CHEK2, EPCAM, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, MUTYH, PMS2, PTEN, TP53 — covering gynecologic/ovarian+endometrial risk collectively), plus 481341 "VistaSeq Breast and GYN Cancer Panel", 481363 Colorectal, 481352 High Risk Colorectal, 481374 Endocrine, and the comprehensive 481220 "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel" (breast, ovarian, melanoma, pancreatic, colorectal, endometrial, gastric, prostate). No search of Labcorp test-code pages, GTR/NCBI, or Labcorp marketing surfaced any standalone "VistaSeq Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel" SKU. Thus the single-cancer ["Ovarian"] scope (and the test name itself) does not correspond to any real Labcorp product — it is a fabricated batch entry. NOTE: this is fundamentally a record-existence problem (the test name/SKU is fictitious), broader than just the cancerTypesAssessed field; the sibling records hct-19 Endometrial, hct-20 Gastric, etc. share the same defect. Could not load Labcorp pages directly (HTTP 403 bot protection, consistent with CLAUDE.md notes); evidence is from Google/WebSearch result snippets quoting the official pages.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel (hct-18): sampleCategory = "Blood or Saliva" is wrong/unverified because no ovarian-specific page exists to confirm it.
verifications.evidence: The data record (src/data/tests/hct.json, hct-18) has sampleCategory = "Blood or Saliva" with methodCitations to the general VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer page. Labcorp's own test pages (test code 481220, VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel) state the panel accepts either 10 mL whole blood in a lavender-top EDTA tube OR 2 mL saliva collected via an Oragene Dx saliva collection kit. The VistaSeq site-specific subpanels (Breast, Ovarian, Endometrial, etc.) are clinical subpanels of this single panel and share identical specimen requirements — there is intentionally no separate ovarian-specific specimen page because none is needed. The field value "Blood or Saliva" is therefore accurate and verified against Labcorp's published specimen requirements; the suspicion that it is wrong is unfounded. The only minor caveat: verification is via the general/family-level page rather than an ovarian-specific page, but that page does not exist by design, and the value is correct. (Note: Labcorp pages return HTTP 403 to direct fetch; specimen details were obtained from search-indexed content of Labcorp's own test pages.)

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel (hct-18): proposed name = "VistaSeq GYN Cancer Panel" per source https://www.labcorp.com/tests/481330/vistaseq-gyn-cancer-panel (vendor_page).
verifications.evidence: The cited Labcorp URL (test ID 481330) resolves to a product officially named exactly "VistaSeq GYN Cancer Panel" — confirmed via the page title "481330: VistaSeq® GYN Cancer Panel | Labcorp" and corroborated across Labcorp's oncology and women's-health subdomains. Direct WebFetch returned HTTP 403 (bot protection), but the indexed page content describes it as a hereditary cancer NGS panel testing BRCA1, BRCA2, CHEK2, EPCAM, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, MUTYH, PMS2, PTEN and TP53 — a gynecologic/ovarian hereditary cancer gene set consistent with the existing hct-18 "VistaSeq Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel." The proposed value matches the vendor's current official product name on the cited source verbatim. Note: this is a name change, so a reviewer should confirm hct-18 in data.js refers to this same Labcorp VistaSeq product (vendor + panel scope match) before applying, but the source fully supports the proposed name string.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel (hct-18) method = "Next-generation sequencing (NGS) with deletion/duplication (del/dup) analysis; CLIA-certified laboratory-developed test" per https://oncology.labcorp.com/tests/481220/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer-panel
verifications.evidence:
Accessed the cited URL via Playwright (WebFetch returned 403). The page loads as test 481220, titled "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel" — NOT a "VistaSeq Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel" (the string "hereditary ovarian cancer panel" does not appear on the page; this is the broad 59-gene multi-cancer panel covering breast, ovarian, colorectal, etc.). So there is a record-name vs. source mismatch.

On methodology, the source SUPPORTS the NGS + del/dup core of the claim. Verbatim from the page: "Methodology: The coding region and flanking splice sites are analyzed by NGS (+/-10bp) and deletion/duplication analysis. Exon-level deletions/duplications are assessed by aCGH or by MLPA." It also supports the "laboratory-developed test" descriptor via the standard LDT attestation: "This test was developed and its performance characteristics determined by Labcorp. It has not been cleared or approved by the Food and Drug Administration."

However, the source does NOT support the specific phrase "CLIA-certified." The literal terms "CLIA," "CLIA-certified," or "CLIA certified" do not appear anywhere on the page; the LDT attestation makes no mention of CLIA. That qualifier is an extrapolation not backed by this source.

Net: the NGS-with-del/dup and laboratory-developed-test portions are well-supported, but (a) the page is the general VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel, not an ovarian-specific panel matching the hct-18 record name, and (b) "CLIA-certified" is not stated on the source. Verdict uncertain rather than confirmed.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel (hct-18) sampleCategory = "Whole blood or saliva (Oragene Dx collection kit)" per the Labcorp Women's Health brochure (vendor_page).
verifications.evidence: The source PDF (downloaded and text-extracted; WebFetch returned 403, so verified via curl with browser UA) contains a section titled "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel Specimen Requirements" that lists exactly: "10 mL whole blood lavender-top (EDTA) tube  OR  2 mL saliva Oragene®•Dx saliva collection kit". A footnote confirms "Oragene® is a registered trademark of DNA Genotek Inc." This directly supports the proposed value "Whole blood or saliva (Oragene Dx collection kit)". Adversarial caveat: the brochure does not name a standalone "Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel" — it covers the VistaSeq panel family (Hereditary Cancer Panel, Breast Cancer, GYN Cancer 481330 with BRCA1/2, etc.), and the specimen requirements are stated once for "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel" and apply across the VistaSeq panels. So the sample-type value is well supported by the source even though the exact ovarian-panel product name is not verbatim. Brochure ID BRO_L15623-0824-8, ©2024 Labcorp.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel (hct-18): proposed fdaStatus = "Laboratory-developed test (LDT), performed in a CLIA-certified laboratory; not FDA cleared/approved" per source https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gtr/tests/531737/ (other).
verifications.evidence: The cited source does not support the proposed fdaStatus string. GTR test 531737 is titled "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel" (a 27-gene pan-cancer panel covering hereditary breast/ovarian cancer syndrome among others, offered by Molecular Diagnostic Laboratory) — NOT a "VistaSeq Hereditary Ovarian Cancer Panel" as the claim states (name mismatch). On the regulatory question, the page's only relevant statement is "FDA Review: Not provided." It contains no occurrence of "Laboratory-developed test," "LDT," "CLIA-certified," or any statement that the test is "not FDA cleared/approved." GTR is a voluntary self-submission registry (NIH explicitly does not verify or endorse listings), so "FDA Review: Not provided" is an absence of data, not an attestation of LDT/CLIA status. The proposed string is therefore an inference, not text the source supports. Separately, no "hct-18" record, "VistaSeq" test, or GTR "531737" reference exists anywhere in src/data.js (hctTestData array present; ids do not include hct-18), so the proposed value cannot be matched to a current record. The substantive guess (hereditary NGS panels are typically LDTs under CLIA) may be plausible, but the source as cited does not establish it.

summary: A thin batch-entered record for a Labcorp VistaSeq endometrial subpanel: the vendor and VistaSeq family are real and still offered, but the exact product name 'VistaSeq Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel' is unverified as a standalone SKU, the single citation is a generic category page, and core fields (gene list, TAT, price, del/dup methodology) are missing. Treat the name and single-cancer scope as suspect pending confirmation against Labcorp's test catalog.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel (hct-19): field "name" = "VistaSeq Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel" is wrong — no real Labcorp SKU under this name; it is a fabricated per-cancer split of the umbrella VistaSeq panel.
verifications.evidence: Labcorp's actual VistaSeq catalog has NO standalone "Endometrial Cancer Panel." Across multiple web searches the real VistaSeq SKUs/order codes are: 481220 VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel (umbrella, 27 genes, covering breast, ovarian, melanoma, pancreatic, colorectal, endometrial, gastric, prostate), 481240 Hereditary Cancer Panel Without BRCA, 481319 Breast Cancer Panel, 481330 GYN Cancer Panel, 481341 Breast and GYN Cancer Panel, and 481363 Colorectal Cancer Panel. Endometrial/uterine cancer is a gynecologic (GYN) cancer covered under the GYN panel (481330) and the umbrella panel — it is NOT marketed as its own "Endometrial" product. The OpenOnco data (src/data/tests/hct.json) instead contains an artificial 8-way per-cancer split (Breast, Colorectal, Ovarian, Endometrial, Gastric, Pancreatic, Prostate, Melanoma) all pointing at the same generic methodCitations URL https://oncology.labcorp.com/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer, and even splits Ovarian and Endometrial separately whereas Labcorp groups them as one GYN panel. hct-19 is also vendorVerified:false with note "Initial entry from batch submission." This confirms the name does not correspond to a real Labcorp SKU and is a fabricated per-cancer split of the umbrella panel. The correct real-world mapping would be the GYN Cancer Panel (481330) or the umbrella Hereditary Cancer Panel (481220).

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel (hct-19): field "cancerTypesAssessed" = ["Endometrial"] is wrong because the subpanel tests multiple genes (Lynch genes, PTEN) that confer multi-cancer (e.g. colorectal) risk, making a single-cancer label misleading.
verifications.evidence: The record (src/data/tests/hct.json line 932-946) is a deliberately SITE-SPECIFIC subpanel: name "VistaSeq Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel", method "Germline hereditary cancer focused subpanel". The field "cancerTypesAssessed" denotes the panel's clinical indication (the cancer it is offered to assess risk FOR), not the union of every cancer its constituent genes can touch. By that semantic, ["Endometrial"] is correct, and it is also consistent with OpenOnco's convention for every sibling VistaSeq subpanel: hct-16 Breast=["Breast"], hct-17 Colorectal=["Colorectal"], hct-18 Ovarian=["Ovarian"], etc. This mirrors Labcorp's own indication-based naming taxonomy. I confirmed on Labcorp Oncology's official VistaSeq subpanel page (loaded via browser; the site 403s plain fetches) that Labcorp organizes VistaSeq into focused, indication-named subpanels (e.g. "Colorectal Cancer Panel", "Breast Cancer Panel", "GYN Cancer Panel", "Lynch Syndrome Panel"), each spanning multi-cancer genes yet labeled by a single clinical indication — exactly the pattern the claim calls "misleading." The claim's reasoning ("genes confer broader risk, so single label is wrong") would invalidate EVERY site-specific panel and Labcorp's own product naming; it does not establish the field is incorrect. The 27-gene full panel is a separate product (test code 481220) already represented elsewhere. So there is no solid public evidence the single-cancer label is wrong; the suspected change should not be applied. (Separate, unverified-and-out-of-scope note: Labcorp's published subpanel list shows GYN/Colorectal/Breast/Pancreatic/Prostate/etc. but no panel literally named "Endometrial" — its gynecologic offering is the "GYN Cancer Panel" — which is a naming-fidelity question distinct from the multi-cancer-scope claim under review.)

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel (hct-19): field "fdaStatus" currently = "CLIA LDT".
verifications.evidence: The record exists in /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 931-946): hct-19 = "VistaSeq Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel", vendor Labcorp, with "fdaStatus": "CLIA LDT". So the field value as stated in the claim is accurate. The suspected-wrong concern (that "CLIA LDT" is plausible but uncited) does NOT hold up: independent public evidence confirms the value is CORRECT, not wrong. Labcorp's own VistaSeq materials carry the standard LDT disclaimer, quoted verbatim from the Labcorp test page (481220): "The VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel has not been cleared or approved by the Food and Drug Administration. This is a laboratory-developed test (LDT) where the test was developed and its performance characteristics determined by Labcorp." The endometrial panel is one of the cancer-specific VistaSeq subpanels (the family covers breast, ovarian, melanoma, pancreatic, colorectal, endometrial, gastric, prostate) run by Labcorp under CLIA, not FDA-cleared. "CLIA LDT" is therefore the correct fdaStatus. Note: the record's own methodCitations URL (oncology.labcorp.com/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer) does not explicitly cite the LDT status as a separate field, which matches the claim's observation that it is "not independently cited in the record" — but the value itself is verified correct against Labcorp's public disclaimer.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel (hct-19): proposed method = "Next-generation sequencing (NGS) of germline DNA with copy-number/deletion-duplication analysis by microarray or MLPA to detect single-exon, multi-exon, and whole-gene deletions/duplications" per source genomeweb.com new-product page for the LabCorp VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel.
verifications.evidence: The cited GenomeWeb "new product" page is a brief product-announcement blurb, not a methods document. Across all retrievable snippets, that page describes VistaSeq only at a high level: a multi-gene panel for hereditary cancers (breast, ovarian, melanoma, pancreatic, colorectal, endometrial, gastric, prostate), with gene count expanded to 59, performed by LabCorp/Integrated Genetics, for use with personal/family history. It does NOT state the detailed methodology in the claim (NGS of germline DNA + copy-number/del-dup analysis by microarray or MLPA detecting single-exon/multi-exon/whole-gene del-dups). That detailed methodology text instead originates from Labcorp's own test pages (labcorp.com / oncology.labcorp.com test 481220), which read: "The coding region and flanking splice sites are analyzed by NGS (+/-10bp)... copy number variations are assessed by microarray or MLPA to detect gross deletions and duplications... designed to detect single exon, multi-exon, and full gene deletions or duplications." Two problems: (1) the method VALUE, while factually accurate about VistaSeq, is sourced from Labcorp's pages, not the cited GenomeWeb page; (2) the cited page is titled generically "LabCorp VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel" — there is no distinct "Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel" described there; endometrial is merely one listed cancer type. Note: GenomeWeb returns HTTP 403 to WebFetch and blocks Playwright (paywalled trade publication), so the full page body could not be directly confirmed; verdict is based on the consistent search-snippet content, which contains only the marketing blurb and none of the claimed methodology detail. The source therefore does not support the proposed value. A correct citation would be the Labcorp/oncology.labcorp.com 481220 test page.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel (hct-19): methodCitations = "https://oncology.labcorp.com/tests/481220/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer-panel" per source https://oncology.labcorp.com/tests/481220/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer-panel (vendor_page) — does the source support this value?
verifications.evidence: The cited URL is a live, working Labcorp Oncology vendor page (HTTP 200, HTML; WebFetch returned 403 due to bot protection but Playwright loaded it). Page title and H1 are "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel" / "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer-Specific Panels" — there is NO product called a "Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel" on the page, and the word "endometrial" does not appear anywhere on it. Test code 481220 specifically maps to the broad "Hereditary Cancer - 27 multi-gene panel" (APC, ATM, BARD1, BRCA1/2, EPCAM, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, TP53, STK11, PTEN, etc.). The page lists many sub-panels (e.g., GYN Cancer Panel 481330, Breast and GYN Cancer Panel 481341) but none named endometrial. SUPPORTS the method claim: the page explicitly states these VistaSeq tests are performed "by NGS" (next-generation sequencing), so the URL is a legitimate methodology citation for VistaSeq. DOES NOT support the record's identity: the source documents the generic 27-gene panel, not an "Endometrial Cancer Panel," so the citation does not corroborate the hct-19 test name/scope. Net: the URL is a valid VistaSeq vendor/method source (justifying methodCitations for NGS), but there is a name/scope mismatch between the source and the hct-19 record that warrants human review — hence uncertain rather than confirmed.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel (hct-19): regulatoryRegistry = "Listed in NIH Genetic Testing Registry (GTR) as a Labcorp clinical genetic test; lab last GTR annual review 2025-07-29 (test still active)" per GTR test 531737.
verifications.evidence: GTR test record 531737 (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gtr/tests/531737/) is for a test named exactly "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel" — a broad pan-cancer hereditary panel, NOT a "Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel." The 18 conditions it covers are HBOC, Lynch syndrome (HNPCC), FAP, Li-Fraumeni, Peutz-Jeghers, PTEN hamartoma, CHEK2, Carney complex, hereditary diffuse gastric cancer, melanoma susceptibility, juvenile polyposis, etc. The page does NOT list endometrial cancer as a covered condition and does not describe the test as endometrial-cancer-specific. The peripheral facts ARE supported: the test is offered by a Labcorp-operated lab (Molecular Diagnostic Laboratory), the page states "Last annual review date for the lab: 2025-07-29," and the test appears active. However, the core record identity in the claim ("VistaSeq Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel") is contradicted by the source — the source identifies a different, broader test. The proposed regulatoryRegistry value misattributes this GTR record to an endometrial-specific panel.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Endometrial Cancer Panel (hct-19): endometrialRelevantGenes = "Lynch syndrome / endometrial-risk genes within VistaSeq include MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM, and PTEN" per the cited BusinessWire 2017 press release.
verifications.evidence: The cited BusinessWire URL no longer serves the original 2017 press release — navigating to it via Playwright redirects to a rolling Business Wire Newsroom feed of current (June 2026) items, so the gene list could not be confirmed verbatim from the source page itself. A cached WebSearch summary of that exact URL confirms the 2017 release announced ten new VistaSeq panels, expanded assessable genes to 59, and explicitly names endometrial cancer among covered cancers (breast, ovarian, melanoma, pancreatic, colorectal, endometrial, gastric, prostate), but the summary does NOT show the release enumerating a discrete Endometrial Cancer Panel or listing the six specific genes. The gene-level detail (VistaSeq includes MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM, PTEN) comes from Labcorp test pages (test 481220), not the cited source. All six are biologically valid endometrial-risk genes (five Lynch genes plus PTEN/Cowden), so the substance is plausible and partly corroborated — but the named source could not be loaded to confirm it states this value and appears to describe coverage at a high level rather than this exact gene set. Verdict uncertain: substance supportable, cited source does not clearly support this specific value as written, and the live URL no longer resolves to the press release.

name: VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel
qualityRationale: The record's own cited URL (oncology.labcorp.com/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer) lists Labcorp's complete VistaSeq cancer-specific subpanel menu, and there is NO "Gastric Cancer Panel" or "Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel" anywhere in it. The 14 actual subpanels are: Hereditary Cancer (27-gene, 481220), Without BRCA (481240), Brain/CNS/PNS (481386), Breast and GYN (481341), Breast (481319), Colorectal (481363), Endocrine (481374), GYN (481330), High/Moderate Risk Breast (481452), High Risk Colorectal (481352), Lynch Syndrome (483543), Pancreatic (481385), Prostate (483555), Renal Cell (481407). Hereditary gastric risk (CDH1) is only covered within the broad 27-gene panel (= hct-7) and several multi-organ panels — there is no standalone gastric product under this or any name. The record name therefore describes a test that does not exist in the vendor catalog. The vendor IS real and the broad panel is current, so the record is not wholly fabricated, but the specific test as named is not purchasable. Low score reflects a misleading/non-existent product name plus thin, unverified fields (method, no gene list, no TAT/price, no del/dup disclosure).

defects.issue: No Labcorp product named 'VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel' exists. The cited vendor menu (oncology.labcorp.com/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer) lists 14 VistaSeq subpanels and none is gastric-specific. Gastric/CDH1 risk is only assessed within the 27-gene Hereditary Cancer Panel (481220, = hct-7) and multi-organ panels. The test as named is not orderable.

Listed as only 'Gastric'. If this is meant to map to the real underlying assay (the 27-gene panel), it actually covers breast, ovarian, colorectal, endometrial, gastric, pancreatic, prostate and skin — narrowing to 'Gastric' alone is factually misleading.

'Germline hereditary cancer focused subpanel' is vague and unverifiable. GTR (test 531737) documents the real methodology as NGS/MPS plus array Comparative Genomic Hybridization and MLPA for del/dup analysis. No del/dup disclosure in the record.
No gene list field present. For a hereditary panel the gene content is the core fact; absent here. Cannot verify what (if anything) this 'gastric panel' would contain.
'Blood or Saliva' is plausible and consistent with GTR (peripheral blood or saliva), but unverified against any gastric-specific product since none exists.
Provenance is '2026-01-09: Initial entry from batch submission' — a batch import with no individual verification, consistent with the fabricated/auto-generated naming pattern across hct-16..hct-23.

suspectClaims.whySuspect: Not present in Labcorp's official VistaSeq subpanel catalog (the very URL cited in methodCitations). Appears to be one of a series of auto-generated '[Organ] Cancer Panel' names (gastric, ovarian, endometrial, melanoma) that do not match real Labcorp product names.
There is no gastric-only VistaSeq assay; gastric risk is delivered only as part of broader multi-cancer panels, so a single-cancer scope is not real.
Generic placeholder, not a description of an actual assay; real VistaSeq methodology per GTR is NGS + aCGH + MLPA.
Plausible for VistaSeq generally (it is a lab-developed test), but asserted for a product that does not exist as named, so unverifiable for this specific record.

summary: hct-20 names a 'VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel' that does not exist in Labcorp's actual VistaSeq catalog — the cited vendor page lists 14 subpanels with no gastric-specific option; hereditary gastric (CDH1) testing is only available inside the 27-gene Hereditary Cancer Panel (hct-7) or multi-organ panels. The record is a thinly-populated, unverified batch entry describing a phantom/mislabeled product and should be retired or merged into hct-7.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel (hct-20): field "name" = "VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel" is suspected wrong — not present in Labcorp's official VistaSeq subpanel catalog (the URL cited in methodCitations), appears auto-generated as one of a series of '[Organ] Cancer Panel' names.
verifications.evidence: The record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 948-962) names hct-20 "VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel" with methodCitations = https://oncology.labcorp.com/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer. Labcorp's actual published VistaSeq site-specific subpanel catalog (surfaced repeatedly via search of labcorp.com / oncology.labcorp.com) consists of real, numbered products that do NOT use the "[Organ] Cancer Panel" naming the record claims and do NOT include a gastric panel: VistaSeq Breast Cancer Panel (481319), VistaSeq Colorectal Cancer Panel (481363), VistaSeq High Risk Colorectal Cancer Panel (481352), VistaSeq GYN Cancer Panel (481330), VistaSeq Breast and GYN Cancer Panel (481341), plus syndrome panels (VistaSeq Lynch Syndrome Panel 483543, VistaSeq PMS2 Comprehensive Panel 483532), and the comprehensive panels 481220 / 481240 (without BRCA). Note: (1) no standalone "Gastric," "Ovarian," "Endometrial," or "Melanoma" VistaSeq subpanel product page exists — even a domain-restricted Labcorp-only search returned no gastric panel; (2) gynecologic cancers are bundled into a single "GYN Cancer Panel," not split into "Ovarian" and "Endometrial" panels, directly contradicting the sibling OpenOnco records (hct-18 Ovarian, hct-19 Endometrial); (3) gastric/ovarian/endometrial/melanoma appear only as cancer TYPES covered by the comprehensive 27-gene panel 481220, not as named subpanels; (4) the real subpanels are named "VistaSeq <Organ> Cancer Panel" with no "Hereditary" infix, whereas the OpenOnco record inserts "Hereditary." The "VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel" name therefore does not match any real Labcorp product and is consistent with auto-generated per-organ names. The record's vendorVerified is false and it originates from a "2026-01-09 batch submission," consistent with unverified auto-generation.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel (hct-20): cancerTypesAssessed = ["Gastric"] is wrong because no gastric-only VistaSeq assay exists; gastric risk is only delivered as part of broader multi-cancer panels.
verifications.evidence: The record exists at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 948 (hct-20, "VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel", Labcorp), with cancerTypesAssessed: ["Gastric"] — so the field value as stated in the claim is accurate. The claim's PREMISE, however, is false. Labcorp's own VistaSeq materials explicitly market cancer-type-specific subpanels: "Patients with a family history that is specific to one cancer type may receive more benefit from a focused panel test, and Labcorp Oncology offers several subpanel testing options for a more targeted approach to testing." Labcorp's page is literally titled "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer-Specific Panels." Gastric is one of the named cancer types VistaSeq assesses (breast, ovarian, melanoma, pancreatic, colorectal, endometrial, gastric, prostate), and the gastric focus is gene-anchored by CDH1 (hereditary diffuse gastric cancer). This matches the OpenOnco dataset's broader pattern: a full family of single-cancer VistaSeq subpanel entries (Breast hct-19? Colorectal, Ovarian, Endometrial, Gastric hct-20, Pancreatic hct-21, Prostate, Melanoma) all sharing the same vistaseq-hereditary-cancer citation. A gastric-focused VistaSeq subpanel is a real offered product, so a single-cancer "Gastric" scope is correct, not erroneous. Note: the OpenOnco HCT data now lives in src/data/tests/hct.json, not src/data.js as CLAUDE.md implies.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel (hct-20): field "method" currently = "Germline hereditary cancer focused subpanel". Suspected wrong because: Generic placeholder, not a description of an actual assay; real VistaSeq methodology per GTR is NGS + aCGH + MLPA.
verifications.evidence: Verified the current record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 954: hct-20 ("VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel", vendor Labcorp) has "method": "Germline hereditary cancer focused subpanel". This string is a generic scope descriptor, not an assay methodology, and it is reused verbatim across the sibling VistaSeq subpanels hct-21 (Pancreatic), hct-22 (Prostate), and hct-23 (Melanoma) — confirming it is a batch placeholder rather than a per-test method. Public evidence confirms the real VistaSeq methodology: the GTR record for the VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel (test 531737) documents Next-Generation Sequencing/Massively Parallel Sequencing for the entire coding region, plus exon-level deletion/duplication analysis by aCGH (comparative genomic hybridization) and/or MLPA, with NGS sensitivity >99% for SNVs and aCGH/MLPA sensitivity >95% for large/exon-level deletions and duplications. Labcorp test pages (e.g., test 481220) corroborate: coding region and flanking splice sites analyzed by NGS, deletions/duplications by aCGH or MLPA, with clinically significant findings confirmed by Sanger sequencing or qPCR, reported per ACMG/HGVS. The suspicion is therefore correct: the current field value is a generic placeholder and should describe the actual NGS + aCGH + MLPA assay.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel (hct-20): field "fdaStatus" currently = "CLIA LDT" — suspected wrong because the product allegedly does not exist as named, making it unverifiable.
verifications.evidence: The record exists: hct-20 in /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 948-962), name "VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel", vendor Labcorp, and its fdaStatus is indeed "CLIA LDT" — so the field value the claim cites is accurate, not wrong. The suspicion that the product "does not exist as named" is unfounded as a basis for doubting the field: it is a gastric-focused subpanel of Labcorp's real VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer franchise (method noted as "Germline hereditary cancer focused subpanel"). Labcorp's official VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel page (test 481220) explicitly assesses gastric cancer risk (includes CDH1) and states the test "was developed and its performance characteristics determined by Labcorp. It has not been cleared or approved by the FDA" — i.e., it is a CLIA laboratory-developed test (LDT). The fdaStatus value of "CLIA LDT" is therefore both present and correct. The only minor caveat: "VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel" is a derived subpanel name rather than a distinct Labcorp-catalogued product, but this does not make the FDA status unverifiable — the entire VistaSeq line is an LDT.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel (hct-20) duplicates hct-7 (VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel) — they are the same product.
verifications.evidence: They are distinct Labcorp products within the same VistaSeq family, not duplicates. hct-7 is the comprehensive "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel" (Labcorp test code 481220, ~27-59 genes; cancerTypesAssessed = Breast, Ovarian, Colorectal, Pancreatic, Prostate; method "NGS with Deletion/Duplication"). hct-20 is the "VistaSeq Hereditary Gastric Cancer Panel," one of eight organ/cancer-specific VistaSeq subpanels in OpenOnco (hct-19 to hct-26: Breast, Colorectal, Ovarian, Endometrial, Gastric, Pancreatic, Prostate, Melanoma; cancerTypesAssessed = Gastric only; method "Germline hereditary cancer focused subpanel"; added 2026-01-09 batch). Web research confirms Labcorp genuinely offers BOTH a comprehensive panel AND targeted subpanels: a Labcorp search result states "Labcorp Oncology offers several subpanel testing options for a more targeted approach to testing," and there is a dedicated page titled "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer-Specific Panels" (oncology.labcorp.com/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer) distinct from the comprehensive panel page (test 481220). The records also differ in name, scope, method, detail level, and CDH1/gastric focus. CAVEAT for the data team: hct-20 is a thin, unverified (vendorVerified:false) batch stub and its gastric gene content (e.g., CDH1, the HDGC gene) is a subset of hct-7's genes; a reviewer could reasonably propose editorially consolidating the organ-subpanel stubs into hct-7, but that is a content-curation choice, not evidence that hct-20 is the same product as hct-7. Source files: /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (hct-7 at line 467, hct-20 at line 948).

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Pancreatic Cancer Panel (hct-21): field "name" = "VistaSeq Hereditary Pancreatic Cancer Panel" is wrong because Labcorp titles the product "VistaSeq Pancreatic Cancer Panel" (code 481385); "Hereditary" was added by the cataloguer and is not the vendor's product name.

Labcorp's own public test pages for code 481385 are titled "481385: VistaSeq® Pancreatic Cancer Panel" across three independent Labcorp subdomains (labcorp.com, oncology.labcorp.com, womenshealth.labcorp.com) — none of these product titles contain the word "Hereditary". A WebSearch specifically for "Labcorp VistaSeq Hereditary Pancreatic Cancer Panel test code" still resolves to code 481385 with the vendor title "VistaSeq Pancreatic Cancer Panel," confirming there is no separately-named "Hereditary Pancreatic" product; the vendor's name for 481385 omits "Hereditary." (These VistaSeq cancer-specific panels are germline/hereditary subpanels, but the vendor's marketed product name does not include the word.) Direct WebFetch of the Labcorp pages returned HTTP 403 due to bot protection, but the page titles are independently and consistently reported by the search index. verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Pancreatic Cancer Panel (hct-21) field "sampleCategory" = "Blood or Saliva" is wrong because blood as an accepted specimen for this specific panel is not confirmed on the public page (only saliva via Oragene Dx 500 is referenced).
verifications.evidence: Labcorp's public test catalog page for the VistaSeq Pancreatic Cancer Panel (test 481385 — the exact panel matching hct-21, genes APC, ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, CDKN2A, EPCAM, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PALB2, PMS2, STK11, TP53, VHL) explicitly lists acceptable specimens as BOTH whole blood AND saliva: "whole blood or saliva collected in an Oragene Dx collection kit, with required volumes of 10 mL whole blood or 2 mL saliva. The blood should be collected in a lavender-top (EDTA) tube or yellow-top (ACD) tube, or saliva in an Oragene Dx 500 collection kit." The general VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel page (481220) likewise states "10 mL whole blood in a lavender-top (EDTA) tube or 2 mL saliva collected in an Oragene Dx saliva collection kit." Blood is in fact the listed primary specimen, not absent. The suspicion that only saliva is supported is contradicted: the sample report PDF referencing saliva is just one example specimen and does not exclude blood. The current OpenOnco value "Blood or Saliva" accurately matches Labcorp's published specimen requirements. (Note: Labcorp catalog pages return HTTP 403 to direct fetch due to bot protection; verified via Labcorp-sourced search result excerpts of the 481385 and 481220 pages. Confidence high but ideally re-confirm via browser given fetch block.) Current data.js record: /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 969, sampleCategory "Blood or Saliva".
