verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Pancreatic Cancer Panel (hct-21): methodCitations = "https://www.labcorp.com/tests/481385/vistaseq-pancreatic-cancer-panel" per the same Labcorp vendor page (vendor_page).
verifications.evidence: The proposed methodCitations value is the source URL itself, so verification requires that this Labcorp page exists, refers to the VistaSeq Pancreatic Cancer Panel, and actually contains methodology content. WebFetch returned HTTP 403 (Labcorp bot protection), but the Playwright browser loaded the exact URL successfully: Page Title = "481385: VistaSeq® Pancreatic Cancer Panel | Labcorp". The page contains an explicit METHODOLOGY section, quoted verbatim: "The coding region and flanking splice sites are analyzed by NGS (+/-10bp) and deletion/duplication analysis. Exon-level deletions/duplications are assessed by aCGH or by MLPA. Analysis is limited to deletion/duplication testing for EPCAM. Analysis is expanded for BRCA1/2 flanking splice sites (+/-20bp) and to include promoter deletions for APC (1B and 1A). Clinically significant findings are confirmed by Sanger sequencing or qPCR. Results are reported using ACMG guidelines and nomenclature recommended by the Human Genome Variation Society (HGVS)." This is exactly the kind of methodology content a methodCitations field should point to, and the URL is the canonical Labcorp test-detail page for test 481385. The source therefore genuinely supports its own use as the method citation. Note: the page title reads "VistaSeq® Pancreatic Cancer Panel" (Labcorp markets it without the word "Hereditary" in the title), whereas the OpenOnco record name uses "Hereditary"; this is a naming nuance only and does not affect the citation's validity — it is the correct test (same gene panel, hereditary cancer risk).

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Pancreatic Cancer Panel (hct-21): proposed method = "Next-generation sequencing (germline DNA sequence analysis) of a 14-gene pancreatic cancer subpanel" per https://www.labcorp.com/tests/481385/vistaseq-pancreatic-cancer-panel

Labcorp catalog 483555 does NOT use "Hereditary" in this subpanel's name. Loading the official page in a real browser, the canonical labcorp.com page (catalog 483555) renders H1 = "VistaSeq® Prostate Panel" and document.body.innerText.includes('Hereditary Prostate') returned false. The oncology.labcorp.com subdomain indexes the same catalog 483555 page as "VistaSeq® Prostate Cancer Panel | Labcorp Oncology". Both official titles omit "Hereditary" from the subpanel name. "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer" is the umbrella brand only — the standalone hereditary-cancer panel is a separate catalog item, 481220 "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel". So inserting "Hereditary" into the prostate subpanel name (hct-22) is inaccurate. Note the official subpanel name is most consistently "VistaSeq Prostate Cancer Panel" (oncology subdomain) / "VistaSeq Prostate Panel" (main site H1) — either way without "Hereditary". Caveat: the labcorp.com main-site H1 drops "Cancer" too, so the cleanest exact match for the catalog name is "VistaSeq Prostate Cancer Panel".

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Prostate Cancer Panel (hct-22) field "sampleCategory" = "Blood or Saliva" is wrong; the 483555 test page specifies only saliva (Oragene Dx 500), and blood was not confirmed for this specific panel.
verifications.evidence: The current OpenOnco record (src/data/tests/hct.json line 985) has sampleCategory: "Blood or Saliva". The official Labcorp primary source for test 483555 (VistaSeq Prostate Panel) directly confirms BOTH specimen types, refuting the claim's premise that only saliva is accepted. Verbatim from the page's Specimen Requirements section (retrieved via Playwright browser, since WebFetch returned 403 due to bot protection): Specimen = "Whole blood or saliva collected in an Oragene Dx collection kit"; Volume = "10 mL whole blood, 2.0 mL saliva"; Minimum Volume = "7 mL whole blood, 0.5 mL saliva"; Container = "Lavender-top (EDTA) tube or yellow-top (ACD) tube or Oragene Dx 500 saliva collection kit"; Collection Instructions = "Blood is collected by routine phlebotomy. Saliva is collected by spitting into the provided container until it reaches the fill line." The page lists both a whole-blood volume/container AND a saliva volume/container, so blood is explicitly accepted for this exact panel (483555) — not merely assumed from the broader VistaSeq line. The existing "Blood or Saliva" value is accurate and should NOT be changed.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Prostate Cancer Panel (hct-22): genesAssessed = "ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, CHEK2, HOXB13, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PALB2, PMS2 (10 genes)" per https://oncology.labcorp.com/tests/483555/vistaseq-prostate-cancer-panel
verifications.evidence: The cited Labcorp source page (test #483555) was loaded directly in a browser (WebFetch returned 403, so verified via Playwright). The page's "Test Includes" and "Genes included" sections both explicitly list exactly the 10 genes claimed, in the same order: "ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, CHEK2, HOXB13 (NGS only), MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PALB2, and PMS2." The 10-gene composition matches the proposed genesAssessed value precisely. Two minor caveats that do not refute the claim: (1) the source's official test name is "VistaSeq Prostate Cancer Panel" / "VistaSeq Prostate Panel," not literally "VistaSeq Hereditary Prostate Cancer Panel" (though synonyms list "Hereditary Cancer testing" and the brochure is titled "VistaSeq Germline Testing for Prostate Cancer"); (2) the source qualifies HOXB13 as "(NGS only)" — i.e., HOXB13 is sequence-only, no del/dup — which the proposed value omits. The gene list itself is fully and accurately supported.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Prostate Cancer Panel (hct-22): method = "NGS of coding regions and flanking splice sites (+/-10 bp) plus deletion/duplication analysis (aCGH or MLPA); clinically significant findings confirmed by Sanger sequencing or qPCR" per https://www.labcorp.com/tests/483555/vistaseq-prostate-panel
verifications.evidence: The cited Labcorp vendor page (test 483555, "VistaSeq Prostate Panel") was retrieved directly via headless browser (WebFetch returned 403, so Playwright was used). Its METHODOLOGY section reads verbatim: "The coding region and flanking splice sites are analyzed by NGS (+/-10bp) and deletion/duplication analysis. Exon-level deletions/duplications are assessed by aCGH or by MLPA. Analysis is expanded for BRCA1/2 flanking splice sites (+/-20bp). Analysis is limited to sequence only analysis for HOXB13. Clinically significant findings are confirmed by Sanger sequencing or qPCR. Results are reported using ACMG guidelines and nomenclature recommended by the Human Genome Variation Society (HGVS)." This is a near-exact match to every element of the proposed value: NGS of coding regions and flanking splice sites (+/-10 bp), deletion/duplication analysis via aCGH or MLPA, and confirmation of clinically significant findings by Sanger sequencing or qPCR. The source genuinely supports the value. Minor note: the page is titled "VistaSeq Prostate Panel" (not "Hereditary Prostate Cancer Panel"), but it is the same test (483555), CPT 81432, listed under hereditary/familial/inherited cancer testing synonyms.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Prostate Cancer Panel (hct-22): proposed methodCitations = "https://oncology.labcorp.com/tests/483555/vistaseq-prostate-cancer-panel" per the same vendor_page source. Does that source support this value?
verifications.evidence: The cited URL is real and resolves to Labcorp test 483555, with the search-confirmed page title "483555: VistaSeq® Prostate Cancer Panel | Labcorp Oncology". The page legitimately describes the test's methodology (NGS sequencing of ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, CHEK2, HOXB13 [NGS only], MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PALB2, PMS2, plus stated sequencing limitations), so it is a reasonable methodCitations source for THIS test. However, two problems prevent confirmation: (1) The record does not exist in src/data.js — grep found 0 matches for "VistaSeq", "hct-22", "483555", or "prostate cancer panel" — so this is a proposed, not existing, value. (2) Name mismatch: the vendor calls it "VistaSeq Prostate Cancer Panel," NOT "VistaSeq HEREDITARY Prostate Cancer Panel." "Hereditary" appears only in Labcorp's separate broad panel "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel" (test 481220, a different test/URL). The claimed record name therefore is not faithfully supported by the source. Direct WebFetch of the page returned HTTP 403 (bot protection), so content was confirmed via Labcorp's own search-result snippet rather than full page render — adding to the uncertainty. The URL is appropriate as a method citation, but the record name it is attached to is not what the source actually says.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Prostate Cancer Panel (hct-22): proposed name = "VistaSeq Prostate Cancer Panel" per source https://oncology.labcorp.com/tests/483555/vistaseq-prostate-cancer-panel (vendor_page)
verifications.evidence: I loaded the exact claimed vendor URL (Labcorp Oncology, test code 483555) in a real browser. The page H1 reads "VistaSeq® Prostate Cancer Panel" and the page title is "483555: VistaSeq® Prostate Cancer Panel | Labcorp Oncology" — exactly the proposed name, with no "Hereditary" in the title. The WebSearch result for "483555" independently returned the same title: "483555: VistaSeq® Prostate Cancer Panel | Labcorp Oncology". The page describes a 10-gene hereditary panel (ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, CHEK2, HOXB13, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PALB2, PMS2) for patients with personal/family prostate cancer history, consistent with the same test currently stored as hct-22. So while the test is functionally a hereditary panel, the vendor's branded product name on this page is "VistaSeq Prostate Cancer Panel," supporting the proposed value. name: VistaSeq Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel
qualityRationale: Vendor is correct (Labcorp) and sample type (Blood or Saliva) and CLIA-LDT status verified against Labcorp/GTR. However the record misrepresents the product: Labcorp sells VistaSeq as a SINGLE 27-gene Hereditary Cancer Panel (test 481220), with a 'Without BRCA' variant (481240) and a few broad disease-area panels (Breast 481319, Breast+GYN 481341) — there is no separately orderable 'Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel.' The cancer-specific names are reporting/marketing subsets of the same assay, so this entry represents a non-purchasable artifact. The record is also very thin: no gene list/count, no methodology detail (actual method is NGS + MLPA + aCGH del/dup, not the vague 'focused subpanel' stated), no TAT, no price, vendorVerified false. Currency/accuracy of what little is present is fair; product framing is misleading.

defects.issue: No standalone 'VistaSeq Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel' is orderable from Labcorp. VistaSeq is a single 27-gene panel (test 481220) / Without-BRCA variant (481240); melanoma is one of the cancers it covers, not a separate purchasable product. The named test does not exist as a distinct orderable assay.

Methodology is vague ('Germline hereditary cancer focused subpanel') and omits the real technical approach: NGS sequence analysis plus MLPA and aCGH for deletion/duplication detection (per Labcorp GTR entry 531737).

Listed as only 'Melanoma,' but the underlying single VistaSeq panel covers breast, ovarian, colorectal, pancreatic, endometrial, gastric, prostate and melanoma. The melanoma-only framing misrepresents the assay's actual scope.
No gene list or gene count present. The underlying panel is 27 genes; melanoma-relevant genes are CDKN2A and CDK4. Absence of gene data is a critical gap for a hereditary panel.
No turnaround time recorded.
vendorVerified is false; record is an unverified batch import from 2026-01-09.

suspectClaims.whySuspect: Cannot find this as a distinct orderable Labcorp test. Labcorp lists VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel (481220) and Without BRCA (481240); cancer-specific subpanels appear to be reporting subsets, not separate products.
GTR entry for VistaSeq (531737) describes NGS + MLPA + aCGH on the full 27-gene panel; there is no evidence of a melanoma-restricted sequencing subpanel as the phrasing implies.
The single VistaSeq assay covers 8 cancer types; restricting to melanoma reflects a marketing label, not a technically distinct test.

Next-generation sequencing (massively parallel sequencing) with MLPA and array CGH for deletion/duplication analysis

summary: A thin, unverified batch-import record for a melanoma-specific VistaSeq panel that does not exist as a separately orderable Labcorp product — VistaSeq is a single 27-gene hereditary cancer panel (test 481220) of which melanoma is one covered indication, making this effectively a duplicate of hct-7 with a misleading name and missing gene/methodology data.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel (hct-23): the test name "VistaSeq Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel" is wrong because it is not a distinct orderable Labcorp test; Labcorp's cancer-specific VistaSeq subpanels appear to be reporting subsets, not separate products.
verifications.evidence: The OpenOnco record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 996-1010 has id "hct-23", name "VistaSeq Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel", vendor Labcorp, method "Germline hereditary cancer focused subpanel", with no test/order code and vendorVerified:false (note: it is a melanoma "Cancer Panel", not "Melanoma Cancer Panel" in source order). I retrieved the authoritative subpanel list directly from Labcorp's oncology site (https://oncology.labcorp.com/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer) via browser (WebFetch was 403-blocked). The page lists EVERY VistaSeq cancer-specific subpanel with its order code: Hereditary Cancer 27-gene (481220), Without BRCA1/2 (481240), Brain/CNS/PNS (481386), Breast and GYN (481341), Breast (481319), Colorectal (481363), Endocrine (481374), GYN (481330), High/Moderate Risk Breast (481452), High Risk Colorectal (481352), Lynch Syndrome (483543), Pancreatic (481385), Prostate (483555), and Renal Cell (481407). There is NO melanoma subpanel. Melanoma risk is covered only inside the comprehensive 27-gene panel (481220) and the Without-BRCA panel (481240) via genes such as CDK4 and CDKN2A; it is not broken out as a separately orderable product. NOTE: the submitter's stated premise is partly incorrect — Labcorp DOES sell many cancer-specific subpanels as distinct orderable tests with their own order codes (contradicting "appear to be reporting subsets, not separate products"). The claim is nonetheless CONFIRMED on its core point: a melanoma-specific VistaSeq panel does not exist as a Labcorp product, so hct-23 does not correspond to any real orderable test. Recommended fix: either remove hct-23, or relabel it to the actual product (VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel, 481220) under which melanoma genes are assessed.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel (hct-23) field "method" = "Germline hereditary cancer focused subpanel" is wrong because GTR 531737 describes NGS+MLPA+aCGH on a full 27-gene panel with no melanoma-restricted sequencing subpanel.
verifications.evidence:
Current data.js value confirmed at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 1002: "method": "Germline hereditary cancer focused subpanel" (vendor Labcorp, cancerTypesAssessed: Melanoma). GTR entry 531737 (VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel) does describe a full 27-gene germline panel (APC, ATM, ..., CDK4, CDKN2A, PTEN, TP53) analyzed by NGS for SNVs plus exon-level del/dup by aCGH or MLPA, confirmed by Sanger/qPCR — i.e. one full-panel sequencing assay, not a melanoma-only sequencing assay. So the claim is CORRECT that there is no separate melanoma-restricted sequencing workflow in GTR.

HOWEVER, the claim's framing that the field is therefore "wrong" is not clearly supported. Labcorp explicitly markets "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer-Specific Panels" (dedicated page: oncology.labcorp.com/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer), which report a cancer-relevant gene subset (e.g. melanoma: CDK4/CDKN2A/PTEN) from the broader VistaSeq panel. Thus describing hct-23 as a "Germline hereditary cancer focused subpanel" is broadly defensible — it IS a germline hereditary-cancer test offered as a cancer-focused subpanel. The field describes scope (a focused subpanel) rather than analytical methodology; it is imprecise (it omits the actual NGS+MLPA/aCGH methodology documented in GTR) but not demonstrably factually false. This is a wording/precision issue, not a clear data error, so I cannot confirm or refute on public evidence. (Note: I could not fetch the Labcorp pages directly — both oncology.labcorp.com/vistaseq-hereditary-cancer and the assets-media/2277 PDF returned HTTP 403 — so the existence of the cancer-specific subpanels rests on Labcorp search-result snippets and the page title rather than full page text.)

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel (hct-23): field "cancerTypesAssessed" = ["Melanoma"] is wrong because the single VistaSeq assay covers 8 cancer types and melanoma-only reflects a marketing label, not a technically distinct test.
verifications.evidence: Field reading is accurate: src/data/tests/hct.json line 996-1010 shows hct-23 "VistaSeq Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel" with cancerTypesAssessed=["Melanoma"]. However the claim's premise that this is an error is refuted by Labcorp's own public materials. Labcorp's official page is titled "VistaSeq Hereditary CANCER-SPECIFIC Panels" and explicitly states Labcorp "offers several subpanel testing options for a more targeted approach to testing" for patients with a family history specific to one cancer type. The 8 cancer types Labcorp names for VistaSeq (breast, ovarian, melanoma, pancreatic, colorectal, endometrial, gastric, prostate) map exactly to OpenOnco entries hct-16 through hct-23, each a distinct named, separately-orderable subpanel, plus the comprehensive panel at hct-7 (27 genes, test code 481220). OpenOnco's data model intentionally catalogs each named Labcorp subpanel as its own record; for the "Melanoma Cancer Panel" product, restricting cancerTypesAssessed to "Melanoma" is the correct and internally consistent representation. Labcorp also offers technically focused variants (e.g. "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel Without BRCA," test code 481240), undercutting the assertion that subpanels are purely marketing labels. Residual minor uncertainty: the exact gene list of the melanoma subpanel could not be confirmed because Labcorp PDFs returned HTTP 403. But this does not make the existing field value wrong — the field correctly describes the named product. No change warranted.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel (hct-23): genesAssessed = "APC, ATM, BARD1, BMPR1A, BRCA1, BRCA2, BRIP1, CDH1, CDK4, CDKN2A, CHEK2, EPCAM, FAM175A (ABRAXAS1), MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, MUTYH, NBN, PALB2, PMS2, PRKAR1A, PTEN, RAD51C, RAD51D, SMAD4, STK11, TP53 (27-gene VistaSeq panel; melanoma-relevant: CDKN2A, CDK4)" per Labcorp test 481220.
verifications.evidence: The 27-gene list in the proposed value matches the Labcorp VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel (test code 481220) exactly. The Labcorp oncology page (returned 403 to direct fetch, but the search-engine snippet of that exact URL) lists all 27 genes: "APC, ATM, BARD1, BMPR1A, BRCA1, BRCA2, BRIP1, CDH1, CDK4, CDKN2A, CHEK2, EPCAM, FAM175A, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, MUTYH, NBN, PALB2, PMS2, PRKAR1A, PTEN, RAD51C, RAD51D, SMAD4, STK11, TP53" and states the panel covers "breast, ovarian, melanoma, pancreatic, colorectal, endometrial, gastric, and prostate cancers." The NCBI GTR record (test 531737) for this same panel independently confirms all 27 genes and corroborates the FAM175A=ABRAXAS1 alias mapping (GTR lists ABRAXAS1). CDKN2A and CDK4 are both present, consistent with the "melanoma-relevant" annotation. CAVEAT: The source page is the GENERAL "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel," not a panel named "Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel" — the gene content is fully supported, but the record's panel NAME (and any implication that it is a melanoma-specific product) is not supported by this source. The genesAssessed VALUE itself is confirmed.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel (hct-23): proposed method = "Next-generation sequencing (massively parallel sequencing) with MLPA and array CGH for deletion/duplication analysis" per source https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gtr/tests/531737/
verifications.evidence: The methodology text is accurate for the test at the cited URL, but the URL points to the WRONG test. GTR test ID 531737 is the "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel" — a comprehensive 27-gene GENERAL hereditary cancer panel (Labcorp/Molecular Diagnostic Laboratory) covering 18 conditions including breast-ovarian, Li-Fraumeni, Peutz-Jeghers, FAP, gastric, colorectal, etc. It is NOT a melanoma-specific panel and the test name does not contain the word "Melanoma" (melanoma appears only as 2 of 18 listed susceptibility conditions). The GTR page lists three methods: (1) Comparative Genomic Hybridization (array CGH) for deletion/duplication analysis, (2) Multiplex Ligation-dependent Probe Amplification (MLPA) for deletion/duplication analysis, and (3) Next-Generation (NGS)/Massively parallel sequencing (MPS) for sequence analysis — which does match the methodology string in the claim. However, because the hct-23 record is for the "VistaSeq Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel" and this source documents a different, broader test, the source does not actually support the value AS ATTRIBUTED to hct-23. The cited source is mismatched to the record (wrong GTR entry); the melanoma-specific panel would have a different GTR ID. Web search confirms 531737 = general VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel, and 531738 = VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel Without BRCA — neither is a melanoma-specific panel.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel (hct-23): proposed sampleCategory = "Peripheral whole blood or saliva" per GTR test 531737.
verifications.evidence: GTR test 531737 lists its specimen source requirements as exactly two values: "Peripheral (whole) blood" and "Saliva". The proposed sampleCategory "Peripheral whole blood or saliva" is a faithful restatement of these two specimen types, so the source supports the value. Caveat: the GTR page 531737 is titled "VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel" (the full pan-cancer LabCorp/Molecular Diagnostic Laboratory assay), NOT a melanoma-specific panel — the hct-23 OpenOnco record is the melanoma subpanel. The specimen type transfers correctly because all VistaSeq subpanels are the same LabCorp assay drawn from the same specimen, but the source is not melanoma-specific. The current data.js value for hct-23 is the more abbreviated "Blood or Saliva"; the proposed "Peripheral whole blood or saliva" more precisely mirrors the GTR wording.

verifications.claim: VistaSeq Hereditary Melanoma Cancer Panel (hct-23) duplicates hct-7 (VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel) — i.e., they are the same product.
verifications.evidence: hct-7 and hct-23 are distinct records, not the same product. hct-7 is the comprehensive VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer Panel (Labcorp test code 481220), a single multi-gene panel (27 genes per data.js; up to 59 in current Labcorp materials) explicitly covering multiple cancers — breast, ovarian, MELANOMA, pancreatic, colorectal, endometrial, gastric, and prostate — in one assay. hct-23 is modeled as a melanoma cancer-type-specific subpanel (method: 'Germline hereditary cancer focused subpanel', cancerTypesAssessed: ['Melanoma']). Labcorp demonstrably offers separate cancer-type-specific VistaSeq subpanels as distinct orderable products: its site has a page titled 'VistaSeq Hereditary Cancer-Specific Panels,' and a confirmed separate test exists — '481319: VistaSeq Breast Cancer Panel' — distinct from the comprehensive 481220 panel. This establishes that cancer-specific VistaSeq subpanels are real, separately-coded products rather than relabels of the comprehensive panel, so hct-7 and hct-23 are not the same product. Caveat: I could directly confirm the BREAST subpanel by test code, but Labcorp blocks automated fetch (HTTP 403) and I could not surface a dedicated test-code page for a standalone melanoma-specific VistaSeq panel; melanoma genes (CDK4, CDKN2A) appear within the comprehensive panel. The existence of the breast subpanel makes a parallel melanoma subpanel plausible but not independently verified. Regardless, the duplication/same-product claim is refuted: hct-7 (full multi-cancer panel) and hct-23 (melanoma-focused subpanel) are distinct offerings.

name: BRCAssure: BRCA1 Targeted Analysis
qualityRationale: Every populated field in the record is factually correct and verified against Labcorp's official test page (code 485066): vendor (Labcorp), single gene BRCA1, CLIA LDT status, and blood sampling all check out, and the test is still actively offered under this exact name. The score is held back by sparseness (no cancerTypes, turnaround, methodology, or price) and one accuracy concern: the record omits the most clinically important fact about this product — it is a single-site test for a KNOWN familial BRCA1 variant, not de novo full-gene BRCA1 sequencing. A user could easily misread genesAnalyzed:1 as a comprehensive BRCA1 screen. The "Saliva" sample claim is also unverified for this specific targeted code.

defects.issue: Record does not convey that this is a targeted/single-site test for a KNOWN familial BRCA1 variant (requires the relative's positive report). It only sequences the specific region containing the familial variant — not the full BRCA1 gene. Without this context the record is clinically misleading vs. a comprehensive BRCA1 analysis.
States 'Blood or Saliva'; Labcorp's official requirement for code 485066 is 7 mL whole blood (EDTA, lavender top). Saliva is not confirmed for this targeted test code.
Missing entirely. Should reference hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (HBOC) syndrome.
Missing; not published in the public-facing page snippet (Labcorp lists TAT on the provider test page).
No methodology field captured though it is publicly documented (NGS/Illumina target capture with bidirectional Sanger rescue, min 40X coverage).
false — record is unverified by vendor and was an initial batch import.

suspectClaims.whySuspect: Labcorp official specimen requirement for test code 485066 is 7 mL whole blood in an EDTA (lavender-top) tube. Saliva is not listed for this specific targeted code; likely copied from a generic Labcorp template.

Numerically correct (BRCA1 only) but misleading without context — the test only analyzes the single region carrying a documented familial variant, not the entire BRCA1 gene, so '1 gene analyzed' overstates coverage.

proposedFills.proposedValue: Blood (7 mL whole blood, EDTA/lavender-top tube)

Next-generation sequencing (Illumina) with custom target-capture enrichment of BRCA1 exons and intron/exon junctions (+/-20 nt); minimum mean coverage 40X; segments failing read-depth rescued by bidirectional Sanger sequencing. Hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (HBOC) syndrome
Known familial variant (single-site) targeted analysis — requires documentation of the relative's positive BRCA1 result; tests only the specific BRCA1 region containing that variant.

summary: A factually accurate but thin record for a real, still-offered Labcorp test (code 485066). Its main weakness is that it presents a single-site KNOWN-familial-variant BRCA1 test as if it were general BRCA1 analysis, and several useful fields (cancerTypes, methodology, turnaround) are missing while the 'Saliva' sample claim is unverified.

verifications.claim: BRCAssure: BRCA1 Targeted Analysis (hct-24) sampleCategory "Blood or Saliva" is wrong; Labcorp test code 485066 requires only 7 mL whole blood in an EDTA tube, with saliva not listed for this targeted code.
verifications.evidence: The official Labcorp Oncology test page for code 485066 (loaded via browser; WebFetch returns 403 due to bot protection) has a "Specimen Requirements" section that explicitly lists BOTH blood and saliva for this specific targeted code: Specimen = "Whole blood or saliva collected in an Oragene Dx collection kit"; Volume = "7 mL whole blood, 2.0 mL saliva"; Minimum Volume = "3 mL whole blood, 0.5 mL saliva"; Container = "Lavender-top (EDTA) tube or yellow-top (ACD) tube or Oragene Dx 500 saliva collection kit." Causes for rejection include "saliva collection in incorrect container," and the page instructs how to order "Oragene Dx 500 saliva collection kits." So saliva is a fully supported specimen type for code 485066, not a generic-template artifact, and the EDTA tube is only one of three accepted containers. The existing OpenOnco value sampleCategory="Blood or Saliva" (in /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json, lines 1011-1026) accurately reflects the vendor's official specimen requirements. No change is warranted.

verifications.claim: BRCAssure: BRCA1 Targeted Analysis (hct-24) genesAnalyzed = "1" is numerically correct but misleading: the test only analyzes the single region carrying a documented familial variant, not the entire BRCA1 gene, so "1 gene analyzed" overstates coverage.
verifications.evidence: The data record (src/data/tests/hct.json line 1012-1026) shows id hct-24, name "BRCAssure: BRCA1 Targeted Analysis", vendor Labcorp, genesAnalyzed: 1, keyGenes ["BRCA1"]. Labcorp's own published test description for this exact test (code 485066) states the scope explicitly: "Only the specific region of the BRCA1 gene containing the familial variant will be tested" and the test "is intended for those individuals who have a family member with a known BRCA1 variant and wished to be tested only for that variant." It requires submitting the positive family member's report documenting the variant. Full-gene BRCA1 sequencing is a SEPARATE, distinct product (BRCAssure BRCA1/2 Comprehensive Analysis, code 485030), to be ordered when no known familial variant exists. Two independent web searches returned the same authoritative Labcorp text. This confirms the claim's reasoning: genesAnalyzed: 1 is numerically defensible (one gene, BRCA1, is involved) but materially overstates analytical coverage — the assay interrogates a single known variant site, not the whole gene. The field is misleading without a qualifier distinguishing single-site/known-familial-variant testing from full-gene analysis. Note: WebFetch on labcorp.com / oncology.labcorp.com returned HTTP 403, so verification rests on the search-engine-extracted Labcorp page content rather than a direct page fetch.

verifications.claim: BRCAssure: BRCA1 Targeted Analysis (hct-24) sampleCategory = "Blood (7 mL whole blood, EDTA/lavender-top tube)" per https://www.labcorp.com/tests/485066/brcassure-brca1-targeted-analysis
verifications.evidence: I loaded the actual Labcorp source page (HTTP 403 on WebFetch due to bot protection, but Playwright browser rendered it successfully; page title "485066: BRCAssure: BRCA1 Targeted Analysis | Labcorp"). The Specimen Requirements section reads verbatim: Specimen = "Whole blood or saliva collected in an Oragene Dx collection kit"; Volume = "7 mL whole blood, 2.0 mL saliva"; Container = "Lavender-top (EDTA) tube or yellow-top (ACD) tube or Oragene Dx 500 saliva collection kit". The blood-specific details in the proposed value (7 mL whole blood, EDTA/lavender-top tube) are all directly and accurately supported by the source. However, the proposed value is materially INCOMPLETE: the source lists SALIVA (Oragene Dx kit, 2.0 mL) as a co-equal, explicitly accepted specimen type, and also lists yellow-top (ACD) tube as an alternative container. The proposed value presents the test as blood-only, which misrepresents the source. verifications.claim: BRCAssure: BRCA1 Targeted Analysis (hct-24) proposed methodology = "Next-generation sequencing (Illumina) with custom target-capture enrichment of BRCA1 exons and intron/exon junctions (+/-20 nt); minimum mean coverage 40X; segments failing read-depth rescued by bidirectional Sanger sequencing. Single-site analysis limited to the region containing the documented familial variant."
verifications.evidence: The Labcorp source page (test 485066) was fetched via browser (WebFetch returned 403, but Playwright rendered the page). The METHODOLOGY section states verbatim: "Next-generation sequencing: Genomic regions of interest are selected using a custom capture reagent for target enrichment and sequenced via the Illumina next generation sequencing platform. Regions of interest include all exons and intron/exon junctions (+/-20 nucleotides) of the BRCA1 (NM_007294.3) and BRCA2 (NM_000059.3) genes... Minimum mean coverage is 40X. Any segment failing minimum read depth coverage is rescued by bi-directional Sanger sequencing to complete sequence analysis." This matches every technical clause of the claim: Illumina NGS, custom target-capture enrichment, exons + intron/exon junctions (+/-20 nt), 40X minimum mean coverage, and bidirectional Sanger rescue for failed read-depth segments. The single-site/familial-variant clause is also supported by the page's Use section: "This test code is intended for those individuals who have a family member with a known BRCA1 variant and wished to be tested only for that variant... Only the specific region of the BRCA1 gene containing the familial variant will be tested." Minor non-material nuance: the capture reagent covers both BRCA1 and BRCA2 regions, but this single-site test code restricts reporting to the documented familial BRCA1 variant region, consistent with the claim's "single-site analysis limited to the region containing the documented familial variant."

verifications.claim: BRCAssure: BRCA2 Targeted Analysis (hct-25) productType = "Central Lab Service" is suspected wrong because it undersells the narrow single-site familial-variant confirmation use.
verifications.evidence: The record at src/data/tests/hct.json line 1028-1042 has productType: "Central Lab Service". In this dataset productType is a delivery-model classifier, not a scope descriptor: 34 of 35 HCT tests use "Central Lab Service" and the only other value is "Direct-to-Consumer" (23andMe). Labcorp performs this as a lab-run CLIA LDT (fdaStatus: "CLIA LDT"), so "Central Lab Service" is factually correct. Labcorp's own test catalog (test 485081) confirms the clinical nature the claim describes — "intended for those individuals who have a family member with a known BRCA2 variant and wished to be tested only for that variant... Only the specific region of the BRCA2 gene containing the familial variant will be tested" — i.e. it IS a single-site familial-variant confirmation test. However, that narrow intended use is not the job of productType; it is already captured elsewhere in the record: name = "BRCAssure: BRCA2 Targeted Analysis", genesAnalyzed = 1, keyGenes = ["BRCA2"]. The claim itself concedes the value is "Not wrong." Since productType is accurate and is not the field meant to convey test scope, there is no defect to fix. Verdict: refuted (the field is correct and is not underselling anything).

verifications.claim: Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel (Pan-Cancer) (hct-26): field "name" = "Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel (Pan-Cancer)" is wrong; vendor's official name is "Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel" with no "(Pan-Cancer)" qualifier, suffix added to disambiguate from duplicate hct-10.

The "(Pan-Cancer)" string does not appear in any Fulgent product title or URL. Timeline supports the disambiguation theory: hct-10 was added 2026-01-08 ("OpenOnco HCT category launch") and hct-26 was added one day later 2026-01-09 ("Initial entry from batch submission") as a sparse duplicate; the parenthetical was evidently appended so the duplicate would not collide with hct-10. Fulgent's own product/specimen pages confirm the panel accepts multiple specimen types: "blood (two 4ml EDTA tubes, lavender top), extracted DNA (3ug in EB buffer), buccal swab, or saliva (kits available upon request)" — with blood preferred. Both records cite the same Fulgent hereditary cancer methodology. verifications.evidence: Confirmed the field value in /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 1053 matches the claim verbatim (record hct-26, genesAnalyzed: 154). I then verified the cited URL directly. curl with a browser user-agent returned HTTP 200 with content-type application/pdf (the WebFetch 403 was bot protection, not a dead link). I downloaded the file: a valid 3-page PDF (699,978 bytes, PDF v1.4). pdftotext extraction shows the document contains "Comprehensive Cancer", "Hereditary Cancer", and "154 genes" — directly corroborating the hct-26 record's genesAnalyzed: 154 value. The premise that the URL could not be confirmed live is false; the premise that content "moved" off fulgentgenetics.com is also false since the flyer still resolves there. (Note: fulgentoncology.com does host a parallel product page, but that does not invalidate the still-live fulgentgenetics.com flyer.) No change to the field is warranted.

verifications.claim: Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel (Pan-Cancer) (hct-26) duplicates hct-10 (Fulgent Genetics, "Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel"). Are they the same product or distinct tests?
verifications.evidence: Both records describe the same Fulgent product. hct-10 (src/data/tests/hct.json:673) and hct-26 (line 1044) share the same vendor (Fulgent Genetics), the same core product name ("Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel"), and the identical gene count (154). Both cite Fulgent's hereditary cancer pages. Fulgent's public catalog confirms there is exactly ONE such broad germline product: the "Full Comprehensive Cancer Panel" covering 154 hereditary cancer genes — search results explicitly describe this as Fulgent's "pan-cancer panel offering." No separately-named "Pan-Cancer Panel" SKU exists; Fulgent's other offerings are disease-specific (Colorectal Focus, Prostate Comprehensive, etc.) with different names and gene counts, plus the FoundationOne Germline partnership (50 genes). Therefore the "(Pan-Cancer)" suffix on hct-26 is a descriptive parenthetical, not a distinct test. hct-26 is the thinner stub (added 2026-01-09 from a batch submission) duplicating the fuller hct-10 (added 2026-01-08 at HCT launch). The two are NOT distinct products — hct-26 is a genuine duplicate of hct-10.

summary: A real, currently-offered single-gene PTEN test whose core data is accurate but whose vendor attribution is critically wrong (Exact Sciences, not Abbott) and which omits publicly-available methodology, turnaround, and full sample-type fields.

verifications.claim: PTEN Hamartoma Tumor Syndrome via the PTEN Gene (hct-28) field "vendor" = "PreventionGenetics (Abbott)" is wrong because PreventionGenetics is an Exact Sciences subsidiary with no Abbott relationship.
verifications.evidence: The record in src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 1077-1080) does show vendor "PreventionGenetics (Abbott)". The claim's stated reason for it being wrong — "No Abbott relationship exists" — is itself false as of today's date (2026-06-02). It is true that Exact Sciences acquired PreventionGenetics in January 2022 for $190M (Exact Sciences/PRNewswire press releases confirm this). HOWEVER, Abbott Laboratories acquired Exact Sciences (a ~$21B equity / $23B enterprise-value deal at $105/share cash) and the acquisition CLOSED on March 23, 2026 (Abbott and PRNewswire press releases: "Abbott completes acquisition of Exact Sciences"). Since PreventionGenetics is a subsidiary of Exact Sciences, and Exact Sciences is now a wholly-owned subsidiary of Abbott, PreventionGenetics IS today an Abbott company. Therefore the "(Abbott)" annotation is accurate at the ultimate-parent level, and the claim that "no Abbott relationship exists" is refuted. (Minor nuance worth flagging to a human: the more precise label would be "PreventionGenetics (Exact Sciences / Abbott)", but the existing value is not factually wrong.)

verifications.claim: PTEN Hamartoma Tumor Syndrome via the PTEN Gene (hct-28): fdaStatus = "CLIA LDT" is plausibly correct, but the test is also NY State CLEP-approved, a regulatory detail not captured in the record.
verifications.evidence: Record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 1077-1094) is hct-28, vendor "PreventionGenetics (Abbott)", single-gene PTEN test, fdaStatus "CLIA LDT". The vendor's own test page (the cited genesAnalyzedCitations URL) confirms the test "was developed, and the performance characteristics validated by PreventionGenetics LLC" under CLIA regulations and "ha[s] not been cleared or approved by the US Food and Drug Administration" — so "CLIA LDT" is the correct fdaStatus. The source is also mislabeled as "vendor_page" when it is a press release. Separately verified that PreventionGenetics does in fact offer this exact test ("PTEN Hamartoma Tumor Syndrome via the PTEN Gene") — but that is documented on its test catalog page (preventiongenetics.com/tests/pten-hamartoma-tumor-syndrome-via-the-pten-gene), which is the source that SHOULD be cited, not the acquisition press release. verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline (hct-29) proposed method = "Next-generation sequencing (NGS) hereditary cancer panel performed by Fulgent Genetics; includes copy number / deletion-duplication analysis" per source https://www.foundationmedicine.com/press-release/hereditary-germline-tests
verifications.evidence: The cited URL is a genuine Foundation Medicine press release (Feb 4, 2025), independently corroborated by BusinessWire, Fulgent Genetics IR, Nasdaq, and 360Dx, ruling out a hallucinated/fabricated source. The page supports the substance of the proposed method: (1) FoundationOne Germline is described as a "next-generation sequencing (NGS)" test analyzing "50 genes associated with hereditary cancers" — supports "NGS hereditary cancer panel"; (2) "Both tests are powered by Fulgent's proprietary technology platform" and the launch is a Foundation Medicine / Fulgent Genetics partnership — supports "performed by Fulgent Genetics"; (3) the tests "analyze single nucleotide and copy number variations" — supports "copy number ... analysis." One minor caveat: the exact phrase "deletion-duplication analysis" does NOT appear in the source; it is a reasonable gloss on "copy number variations" but is interpretive rather than a verbatim quote. Overall the source genuinely supports the proposed value.

verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline (hct-29): clinicalAvailability = "Commercially available in the US since February 28, 2025; orderable alongside Foundation Medicine CGP tests via provider portal" per https://www.foundationmedicine.com/press-release/hereditary-germline-tests
verifications.evidence: The source URL is real and accessible: a Foundation Medicine press release titled "Foundation Medicine to Launch Hereditary Germline Tests, FoundationOne Germline and FoundationOne Germline More, in the United States Through a Partnership with Fulgent Genetics," dated February 4, 2025. The page substantially supports the ORDERING MECHANISM in the proposed value: it states, "The companies are planning for healthcare providers to be able to add FoundationOne Germline or FoundationOne Germline More to any Foundation Medicine comprehensive genomic profiling test, through Foundation Medicine's portal beginning February 28, 2025." This confirms (a) the Feb 28, 2025 date, (b) the provider-portal channel, and (c) orderability as an add-on to FMI CGP tests. HOWEVER, the source does NOT support the assertion that the test is "commercially available... SINCE February 28, 2025." The press release is dated Feb 4, 2025 — before the stated launch date — and uses forward-looking language ("planning," "beginning"). It announces an intended launch target, not confirmed/realized commercial availability. The "since" framing overstates what this specific source establishes; the source cannot confirm the launch actually occurred or that the test is currently commercially available. A separate post-launch confirmation source would be required to support the "commercially available since" claim. Verdict is uncertain (not refuted) because the mechanism and date are accurately drawn from the source, and the only defect is the prospective-vs-realized availability framing, which the source alone neither confirms nor contradicts.

verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline (hct-29): cancerTypes = "Breast, Ovarian, Colorectal, Endometrial, Pancreatic, Prostate, Gastric (hereditary cancer syndromes incl. HBOC, Lynch, Li-Fraumeni, Cowden, FAP)" per https://www.foundationmedicine.com/test/germline-testing
verifications.evidence: The cited vendor page does describe a real test, FoundationOne Germline, but only in generic terms as a "50 gene hereditary cancer germline assay" that "includes high to moderate risk genes linked to hereditary cancers that have clear actionability based on clinical guidelines." It does NOT enumerate the specific cancer types claimed (Breast, Ovarian, Colorectal, Endometrial, Pancreatic, Prostate, Gastric) and does NOT name any of the specific syndromes claimed (HBOC, Lynch, Li-Fraumeni, Cowden, FAP). The page explicitly defers to a separate "Germline Testing One-Pager" PDF for the full gene list — and that is a gene list, not a cancer-type or syndrome list. The proposed cancerTypes value appears to be a reasonable clinical inference about what a 50-gene hereditary panel would cover, but the cited source does not support this specific value as written. The test name and 50-gene scope are corroborated by Foundation Medicine's Feb 2025 launch press release (with Fulgent Genetics). Verdict is refuted because the source does not substantiate the asserted cancer-type/syndrome enumeration; that text should not be attributed to this vendor_page source.

verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline More (hct-30): field "method" currently = "Expanded germline testing offering". Suspected uninformative/unverifiable; real assay is NGS + CNV analysis on Fulgent's platform per the official product PDF.
verifications.evidence:
Verified the record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 1116: "method": "Expanded germline testing offering" with methodCitations pointing to foundationmedicine.com/test/germline-testing. This value is marketing-flavored and conveys no methodology — it does not state what the assay actually does, and is not a verifiable technical claim.

The suspicion is correct. Official Foundation Medicine sources describe a specific, citable methodology: "Both tests are powered by Fulgent's proprietary technology platform and analyze single nucleotide and copy number variations." FoundationOne Germline More is described as "an expanded NGS panel analyzing 154 genes." The official "About the Tests" PDF title literally reads "FoundationOne Germline More — Powered by Fulgent." Sample types are peripheral whole blood (EDTA) or saliva; it is a Fulgent-developed LDT (not FDA cleared/approved), with limitations noted (deep intronic variants >20bp and copy-neutral structural rearrangements not detected).

So the field is not factually wrong, but it is uninformative and unverifiable as written. A more accurate, source-backed value would be something like: "Next-generation sequencing (NGS) detecting single-nucleotide and copy-number variations across 154 genes; performed on Fulgent Genetics' platform (LDT)." Recommend updating the "method" field accordingly.

verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline More (hct-30): methodCitations = "https://www.foundationmedicine.com/test/germline-testing" is the wrong citation because it is a generic germline landing page, not 'More'-specific/methodology-specific, and is reused verbatim across hct-29/30/35/36 so it substantiates nothing unique.
verifications.evidence:
In-data facts confirmed: hct-30 (line 1117 of src/data/tests/hct.json) has methodCitations set to the URL, method = "Expanded germline testing offering". The same URL is reused verbatim on hct-29 (FoundationOne Germline, line 1103), hct-30 (line 1117), and hct-35/hct-36 — which are exact duplicate FoundationOne Germline / Germline More records (lines 1266, 1334) — and on hct-35/36 it also backs genesAnalyzed, cancerTypes, and tat fields.

However, the claim's central rationale is false. I fetched the cited URL and cross-checked via web search. The page is NOT a generic landing page: it is the official Foundation Medicine product page covering both FoundationOne Germline (50 genes) and FoundationOne Germline More, and it explicitly names "FoundationOne Germline More" and describes it as a 154-gene hereditary cancer germline assay that "includes actionable genes from FoundationOne Germline, along with emerging candidate genes associated with lower or uncertain cancer risks." It is the canonical vendor source for the product and directly substantiates hct-30's method field ("Expanded germline testing offering"). Foundation Medicine's own press release (2025-02-04, Fulgent partnership) corroborates the 154-gene "More" product. Since the cited page is the correct, product-specific source for this exact test and supports the method field it is attached to, the citation is not "wrong."

Caveat (separate issue): the verbatim reuse across hct-29/30/35/36 is real, but it reflects duplicate records (hct-35/36 appear to duplicate hct-29/30) and the legitimate fact that one vendor page documents the whole product family — not an incorrect citation on hct-30. That duplication is worth a separate cleanup but does not make hct-30's methodCitations incorrect.

verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline More (hct-30): genesAnalyzed = "154" per https://www.foundationmedicine.com/press-release/hereditary-germline-tests
verifications.evidence: The cited Foundation Medicine press release URL returns HTTP 200 (live, canonical page: "Foundation Medicine to Launch Hereditary Germline Tests, FoundationOne Germline and FoundationOne Germline More, in the United States Through a Partnership with Fulgent Genetics"). The page states: "FoundationOne Germline More is an expanded NGS panel analyzing 154 genes, including well-established cancer-related genes and candidate genes with emerging evidence of association with cancer risk," while the standard FoundationOne Germline analyzes 50 genes. The 154-gene figure for Germline More is independently corroborated by a WebSearch returning the same value from the same press release and related coverage (360Dx, StockTitan, Fulgent partnership documents). The value of "154" matches the genesAnalyzed claim exactly and is attributed to the correct test variant (Germline More, not the 50-gene base Germline). Note: hct-30 was not found in the local src/data.js by grep (no "Germline More" or "hct-30" string match), so I verified only the source-supports-value question, which is affirmative.

verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline More (hct-30): proposed method = "NGS with single-nucleotide and copy-number variant (CNV) analysis; 154-gene expanded hereditary cancer panel powered by Fulgent Genetics, including established and emerging/candidate cancer-risk genes" per the Fulgent IR press release (vendor_page).
verifications.evidence: The cited Fulgent IR page (and its identical mirrors on Business Wire, Foundation Medicine, Nasdaq, 360Dx) supports every element of the proposed method value. The press release states: FoundationOne Germline More "is an expanded NGS panel analyzing 154 genes, including well-established cancer-related genes and candidate genes with emerging evidence of association with cancer risk" — matching the 154-gene expanded hereditary cancer panel and "established and emerging/candidate cancer-risk genes" language. It states both tests are "powered by Fulgent's proprietary technology platform" — matching "powered by Fulgent Genetics." It states the tests "analyze single nucleotide and copy number variations" and use "next-generation sequencing (NGS)" — matching "NGS with single-nucleotide and copy-number variant (CNV) analysis." The 154-gene figure correctly distinguishes Germline More from the 50-gene FoundationOne Germline, so the right test is being described. Note: the Fulgent IR URL itself timed out on direct fetch, but its title and full body text were returned verbatim through web search results pointing at that exact URL, and corroborated by multiple independent mirrors of the same Feb 2025 press release. No discrepancies found; the value is a faithful paraphrase of the source.

verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline More (hct-30): proposed fdaStatus = "CLIA LDT — laboratory developed test by Fulgent Genetics; not FDA cleared or approved" per source https://www.foundationmedicine.com/press-release/hereditary-germline-tests (vendor_page).
verifications.evidence: The source URL is live (HTTP 200) and is a genuine Foundation Medicine page describing FoundationOne Germline More as an expanded 154-gene NGS hereditary cancer panel "powered by Fulgent's proprietary technology platform." The Fulgent Genetics partnership is strongly corroborated by independent sources (Fulgent IR press release, Business Wire 20250204855154, 360Dx, Yahoo Finance), so the "by Fulgent Genetics" portion of the value IS supported. HOWEVER, the cited source does NOT explicitly support the regulatory characterization stated as fact in the proposed value: per the page content, there is no mention of "CLIA," "LDT," or any FDA status — "The page does not specify FDA status or regulatory classification for either germline test" and "There is no mention of 'CLIA' or 'LDT' terminology anywhere." While it is reasonable to infer these are CLIA LDTs (Fulgent is a CLIA lab and no FDA clearance is mentioned), the source itself does not state "not FDA cleared or approved." Verdict is uncertain rather than confirmed: the vendor relationship is supported but the explicit "CLIA LDT / not FDA cleared" claim is an inference not stated on the cited page. Note also a possible labeling concern — the test is marketed as a "FoundationOne" / Foundation Medicine test offered through Fulgent, so attributing it solely to "Fulgent Genetics" as the LDT lab, while technically the performing lab, may understate Foundation Medicine's role; the source frames it as a Foundation Medicine product powered by Fulgent technology.

Each load-bearing element of the claim is corroborated: (1) test name, (2) US availability, (3) February 28, 2025 start date, (4) add-on to any FMI CGP test via the FMI portal. Minor nuance: the press release frames Feb 28 as the date ordering "begins" rather than a retrospective "since," but with today's date (2026-06-02) the test has indeed been available since that date, so the value is accurate. name: BRACAnalysis CDx
qualityRationale: Core identity is accurate and verifiable: real test, still offered, correct vendor, correct 2-gene BRCA1/2 scope, correct FDA PMA companion-diagnostic status (P140020), and genesAnalyzedCitations resolves to the real FDA PMA doc. However the billing block is factually wrong and internally inconsistent, which is high-impact for an accuracy audit: CPT code 0137U is NOT BRACAnalysis CDx (it is Ambry Genetics' RNAinsight for PALB2). The correct code is 81162 (full BRCA1/2 sequence + dup/del). This cascades: medicareRate 282.88, medicareStatus 'Priced', adltStatus:true, and the 'PLA code' wording in cptCodesNotes all describe a PLA/ADLT pricing model that does not apply to Tier-1 code 81162 (~$1,800+ historically). sampleCategory 'Blood or Saliva' also overstates the validated specimen (FDA/vendor = whole blood EDTA only). Record is unverified (vendorVerified:false, batch submission).

summary: A real, currently-offered FDA PMA companion diagnostic whose identity/gene/FDA fields are accurate, but whose billing block is critically wrong: CPT 0137U belongs to a different test (Ambry RNAinsight PALB2) — the correct code is 81162 — which invalidates the medicareRate, ADLT, and PLA-code claims. sampleCategory also overstates validated specimens (whole blood EDTA only). Unverified batch entry needing correction.

verifications.claim: BRACAnalysis CDx (hct-31): field "cptCodes" = "0137U" is wrong; 0137U is the PLA code for Ambry's RNAinsight PALB2, not Myriad BRACAnalysis CDx. The correct code is 81162.
verifications.evidence: Verified the record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 1123-1145: hct-31 = "BRACAnalysis CDx", vendor "Myriad Genetics", with "cptCodes": "0137U" and a cptCodesNotes claiming it is the BRACAnalysis CDx PLA code. Both parts of the claim check out against authoritative public sources: (1) AAPC's official descriptor for PLA code 0137U is "RNAinsight for PALB2 test from Ambry Genetics" — an mRNA sequence analysis of the PALB2 gene. It is an Ambry test, not a Myriad test, and it is PALB2, not even BRCA1/2. (The BRCA1/2 RNAinsight companion is 0138U, also Ambry.) So 0137U is doubly wrong for Myriad BRACAnalysis CDx. (2) CMS's MolDX Billing and Coding article for Myriad's BRACAnalysis CDx (A55224) states explicitly: "To report a BRACAnalysis CDx service, use CPT code 81162 with one (1) unit of service." CPT 81162 is "BRCA1, BRCA2 gene analysis; full sequence analysis and full duplication/deletion analysis (detection of large gene rearrangements)" — which matches both the test's function and the existing cptCodesNotes describing "full BRCA1/2 sequencing plus large rearrangement analysis." Therefore the current value 0137U is incorrect and should be 81162. Note: the medicareRate 282.88 currently attached to this record is the price for 0137U, not 81162, so it would also need correction if the code is fixed.

verifications.claim: BRACAnalysis CDx (hct-31): field "medicareRate" currently = "282.88". Suspected wrong because pegged to wrong code; 81162 historically priced ~$1,825+ on CLFS.
verifications.evidence: The hct-31 record (src/data/tests/hct.json lines 1137-1142) sets cptCodes "0137U", medicareRate 282.88, and a note calling 0137U "the BRACAnalysis CDx PLA code." That coding is wrong on two counts. (1) 0137U is not BRACAnalysis CDx: per the 2025 CPT/PLA code list, 0137U is a PALB2 mRNA sequence-analysis add-on code (reported in conjunction with 81307), unrelated to Myriad's germline BRCA companion diagnostic. 0172U is also a different Myriad test (myChoice CDx, somatic HRD). (2) Authoritative coding analysis states BRACAnalysis CDx germline is billed under CPT 81162 ("the standard everyday CPT code for combined BRCA1-2 sequencing plus dup-del analysis") and that Myriad had not filed a dedicated PLA code for it. The CY2025 CLFS rate for 81162 is $1,825 (confirmed by the note that BillionToOne's 0449U "shifted from gapfill to 81162 $1825"). Therefore the $282.88 value is pegged to the wrong code; the correct code (81162) prices at roughly $1,825, ~6.5x higher. Both the suspected mechanism (wrong code) and the ~$1,825 figure are supported. Caveat: I could not fetch the exact CMS CLFS line item for 81162 directly (the CMS files are downloadable spreadsheets), but the $1,825 figure is corroborated and the 0137U descriptor mismatch is definitive on its own.

verifications.claim: BRACAnalysis CDx (hct-31): field adltStatus = "true" is wrong because 81162 is a Tier-1 CPT code, not a PLA/ADLT single-lab code.
verifications.evidence: The claim's stated reasoning is wrong: the hct-31 record does NOT carry 81162. Its cptCodes field = "0137U" with a note calling it "BRACAnalysis CDx PLA code for full BRCA1/2 sequencing plus large rearrangement analysis." So the claim mischaracterizes which code the record uses, and the "81162 is Tier-1, therefore not ADLT" argument does not apply to this record as written. The claim as stated is therefore refuted. IMPORTANT SEPARATE FINDING (flag for cleanup): the adltStatus=true value is still very likely wrong, but for different reasons. CPT 0137U is actually "RNAinsight for PALB2" from Ambry Genetics — an add-on PALB2 mRNA PLA code used in conjunction with 81307 (confirmed by two AAPC/Codify entries). It is NOT a Myriad BRACAnalysis CDx code at all, so both the record's cptCodes (0137U) and its cptCodesNotes are incorrect for this test. Separately, the Myriad test that actually holds CMS ADLT status is myChoice CDx (0172U), a somatic HRD test granted ADLT since Dec 2019 — not BRACAnalysis CDx, which is a germline FDA-approved companion diagnostic. No authoritative CMS source was found granting BRACAnalysis CDx itself ADLT status. (Could not load the CMS ADLT PDF directly — timed out/403 — so the BRACAnalysis-specific ADLT status is not 100% definitively confirmed, but the 0137U mapping is clearly wrong.) Record path: /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json

verifications.claim: BRACAnalysis CDx (hct-31): field "sampleCategory" = "Blood or Saliva" is wrong because the FDA PMA and vendor specify whole blood EDTA; saliva is not in the approved CDx claim.
verifications.evidence: The OpenOnco record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 1122-1145) defines hct-31 "BRACAnalysis CDx" (Myriad Genetics) with "sampleCategory": "Blood or Saliva" and cites FDA PMA P140020. Public evidence contradicts the saliva portion. Myriad's official BRACAnalysis CDx Technical Information document (www.myriad.com/technical-specifications, mirroring the FDA PMA intended-use language) states the test uses "genomic DNA obtained from whole blood specimens collected in EDTA," contains a dedicated section "Whole Blood Collection and Shipping," specifies EDTA collection tubes (Monoject buffered K2EDTA, lavender stopper), and defines stability only for "Whole Blood Specimens in EDTA blood collection tubes." A full-text search of the document found ZERO occurrences of "saliva" and ZERO of "buccal." FDA SSED/Technical Information Summary for P140020 use the identical whole-blood-EDTA intended-use statement. Conclusion: the approved CDx specimen is whole blood (EDTA) only; saliva is not an FDA-approved or vendor-listed specimen for this companion diagnostic. The sampleCategory should be "Blood" (the codebase already uses "Blood" for blood-only HCT records). Note: the FDA citation URL in genesAnalyzedCitations (cdrh_docs/pdf14/P140020b.pdf) currently returns HTTP 404; the working FDA doc is the Technical Information Summary at cdrh_docs/pdf14/p140020c.pdf.

verifications.claim: BRACAnalysis CDx (hct-31): proposed cptCodes = "81162" per CMS source articleid=55295 (MolDX: Germline testing for use of PARP inhibitors). Does that source support this value?
verifications.evidence: CMS article A55295, "Billing and Coding: MolDX: Germline testing for use of PARP inhibitors," explicitly references "BRACAnalysis CDx" and "Myriad" and lists CPT code 81162. The revision history states verbatim: "Effective 1/1/2019, 81479 is replaced with CPT code 81162." The only molecular codes in the document are 81479 (legacy, replaced) and 81162. Independent verification confirms CPT 81162 is precisely the code for "BRCA1, BRCA2 gene analysis; full sequence analysis and full duplication/deletion analysis," which is exactly what Myriad's BRACAnalysis CDx performs. So the source genuinely supports cptCodes = "81162" for this test. Two caveats that do not change the verdict: (1) the article is RETIRED; (2) the cited URL with ver=20 is no longer live — it redirects to an "Document in Archive" error page; the document is only viewable at the version-less base URL (articleId=55295). The value is correct but the exact citation URL is stale/archived.

verifications.claim: BRACAnalysis CDx (hct-31): proposed sampleCategory = "Blood (whole blood, EDTA)" per FDA source https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/cdrh_docs/pdf14/p140020c.pdf
verifications.evidence: The cited FDA URL is a live document (P140020 "Technical Information Summary" for BRACAnalysis CDx; HTTP 200, contentType application/pdf — the WebFetch 404 was a fetch-layer artifact, verified via Playwright browser + curl download, 168KB PDF). Extracting the PDF text directly, the Intended Use states the device detects BRCA1/BRCA2 variants "using genomic DNA obtained from whole blood specimens collected in EDTA." The specimen/collection section further specifies the Myriad collection kit contains a "Monoject Blood Collection Tube, Silicone Coated Lavender Stopper, buffered EDTA(K3)." verifications.claim: BRACAnalysis CDx (hct-31) turnaroundTime = "~14 days average (7-day priority processing available for pancreatic cancer)" per https://myriad.com/genetic-tests/bracanalysiscdx-germline-test/ (vendor_page)

On the 14-day average, the page states: "BRACAnalysis CDx provides accurate results on average 14 days of sample receipt" and "Every BRACAnalysis CDx test provides actionable results within 14 days to inform cancer treatment decisions." On the priority processing, the page states there is a "1 week or 7 day priority label for patients with pancreatic cancer." verifications.claim: BRACAnalysis CDx (hct-31): proposed methodology = "PCR + Sanger sequencing for SNVs/small indels; multiplex PCR for large deletions/duplications" per https://myriad.com/genetic-tests/bracanalysiscdx-germline-test/
verifications.evidence: The Myriad vendor page (Intended Use section) states verbatim: "Single nucleotide variants and small insertions and deletions (indels) are identified by polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and Sanger sequencing" and "Large deletions and duplications in BRCA1 and BRCA2 are detected using multiplex PCR." This is a near-exact match to the claimed methodology value (PCR + Sanger sequencing for SNVs/small indels; multiplex PCR for large deletions/duplications). The page is a genuine Myriad vendor page and the cited source directly supports the value.

verifications.claim: BRACAnalysis CDx (hct-31) fdaStatusNotes = "FDA PMA P140020 companion diagnostic for PARP inhibitors across breast, ovarian, pancreatic, and prostate cancer (LYNPARZA/olaparib; TALZENNA/talazoparib for breast; ZEJULA/niraparib for ovarian)" per source myriad.com vendor page.
verifications.evidence: The cited Myriad vendor page directly supports nearly the entire proposed value. It states BRACAnalysis CDx is "an FDA-approved test" and is "the only germline test approved by the FDA as a companion diagnostic for treatment with PARP inhibitors." It confirms all four cancers: "Designed for patients with pancreatic, breast, ovarian, or prostate cancer." Its companion-diagnostic indications table maps the exact drugs claimed: breast = LYNPARZA (olaparib) + TALZENNA (talazoparib); ovarian = LYNPARZA (olaparib) + ZEJULA (niraparib); pancreatic = LYNPARZA (olaparib); prostate = LYNPARZA (olaparib). This matches the proposed value precisely (olaparib across all four; talazoparib breast-only; niraparib ovarian-only). One caveat: the PMA number "P140020" is NOT printed on the vendor page, so that single token is not supported by the cited source. However, P140020 is independently verified as the correct PMA for BRACAnalysis CDx via the FDA's own accessdata record (p140020c.pdf, approved Dec 19, 2014, Myriad Genetics). Since the PMA number is factually correct and every substantive clinical/regulatory assertion is backed by the vendor page, the value is accurate; recommend either citing the FDA PMA page alongside the vendor URL or noting the PMA number is FDA-sourced.

name: 23andMe Genetic Health Risk Report for BRCA1/BRCA2 (Selected Variants)
qualityRationale: Vendor, sample type (saliva), DTC product type, and FDA-authorized status are all correct, and the test is still offered (23andMe assets acquired by TTAM in July 2025; consumer health reports remained live). However the record is materially STALE: it describes only the original 2018 3-variant Ashkenazi-founder version (genesAnalyzed framed as 2 genes / 3 variants), while FDA cleared 41 additional variants under K223597 and the current vendor page states the report covers 44 variants across BRCA1/BRCA2. The limitations text and fdaStatusNotes are therefore factually outdated/misleading. Methodology (qualitative genotyping / SNP array, NOT NGS sequencing or del/dup analysis) is absent, and no clinical-validation or guideline reference is present.

summary: Record is accurate on vendor/sample/DTC/FDA-authorized basics and the test is still offered, but it is materially stale — it describes the retired 2018 3-variant Ashkenazi-only version rather than the current FDA-cleared 44-variant report (K223597), and omits methodology (genotyping, not sequencing).
