verifications.claim: 23andMe Genetic Health Risk Report for BRCA1/BRCA2 (Selected Variants) (hct-32): field "genesAnalyzed" = 2 is wrong because, combined with "3 variants" limitations text, it understates current scope; the product tests 44 selected variants and the record nowhere reflects this.
verifications.evidence: The genesAnalyzed value of 2 is factually CORRECT — the test covers exactly 2 genes (BRCA1 and BRCA2), confirmed by keyGenes: ["BRCA1","BRCA2"]. So the specific claim that "genesAnalyzed = 2 is wrong" is REFUTED on its face; a variant count is a different field than a gene count. HOWEVER, the broader substance of the claim is CONFIRMED and represents a real data-quality defect: on 2023-09-05 the FDA granted 23andMe a new 510(k) clearance expanding this exact report from the original 3 Ashkenazi Jewish founder mutations to 44 selected variants (3 original + 41 additional, covering ~30-40% of cancer-related BRCA variants among Black, non-Ashkenazi European, and Hispanic/Latino populations). The hct-32 record is stale: fdaStatusNotes says "Limited to 3 Ashkenazi Jewish founder mutations" and limitations says "Tests only 3 specific variants common in Ashkenazi Jewish population" — both reflect the pre-2023 scope. The record has no variant-count field and nowhere reflects the current 44-variant scope, and the genesAnalyzedCitations points to the original first-DTC-authorization story, not the 2023 expansion. Net: the field named in the claim (genesAnalyzed=2) is not wrong, but the record genuinely understates scope and the 3-variant limitations text needs updating to 44 variants. Verdict is "uncertain" because the claim misidentifies the offending field while being directionally right about the staleness.

verifications.claim: 23andMe Genetic Health Risk Report for BRCA1/BRCA2 (Selected Variants) (hct-32): field "limitations" = "Tests only 3 specific variants common in Ashkenazi Jewish population" is wrong because the report now covers 44 variants including non-Ashkenazi populations per FDA K223597.
verifications.evidence: The record at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 1147-1163) currently reads limitations: "Tests only 3 specific variants common in Ashkenazi Jewish population; negative result does not rule out BRCA mutations" and fdaStatusNotes: "Limited to 3 Ashkenazi Jewish founder mutations". This reflects the ORIGINAL 2018 authorization (DEN170046/K173011), which covered only the 3 Ashkenazi founder variants (c.68_69del/185delAG, c.5266dup/5382insC in BRCA1; c.5946del/6174delT in BRCA2). However, in 2023 the FDA granted 510(k) clearance K223597, expanding the report to report 41 ADDITIONAL variants — for a total of 44 BRCA1/BRCA2 variants (23 in BRCA1, 20 in BRCA2 per vendor). The current 23andme.com/brca/ vendor page confirms the test "detects 44 variants" and explicitly covers non-Ashkenazi populations (African American/European/Hispanic-Latino ~30-40%, East Asian 5-25%, South Asian up to 35% detection rates), in addition to >90% detection in Ashkenazi Jewish descent. The 23andMe investor/press release ("23andMe Granted New FDA Clearance to Report Additional BRCA Variants") and FDA review document K223597.pdf corroborate the expansion. The "only 3 Ashkenazi variants" framing is therefore factually outdated. NOTE: a more precise limitation would emphasize that 44 of >4,000 known pathogenic variants are covered with low non-Ashkenazi detection rates — but the specific claim ("only 3 Ashkenazi variants") is confirmed as wrong. The vendorRequestedChanges note dates this entry to 2026-01-09, so the staleness predates the publicly available 2023 FDA clearance.

verifications.claim: 23andMe Genetic Health Risk Report for BRCA1/BRCA2 (Selected Variants) (hct-32): variantsAnalyzed = "44 selected BRCA1/BRCA2 variants (expanded from original 3 Ashkenazi Jewish founder variants)" per https://www.23andme.com/brca/
verifications.evidence: The vendor page https://www.23andme.com/brca/ explicitly states: "23andMe offers a genetic test for 44 variants in the BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes." It also identifies the three Ashkenazi Jewish founder variants: "About 1 in 40 people of Ashkenazi descent has one of three specific variants included in our report (called c.68_69del, c.5266dup, and c.5946del)." The "expanded from 3" framing is independently corroborated: 23andMe's original GHR report covered only the 3 Ashkenazi founder variants, then received FDA 510(k) clearance (K223597) to add 41 more variants, reaching 44 total (per 23andMe investor release and ASCO Post coverage). Both the count (44) and the historical expansion-from-3 narrative are accurate and supported by the cited vendor page.

verifications.claim: 23andMe Genetic Health Risk Report for BRCA1/BRCA2 (Selected Variants) (hct-32): methodology = "Qualitative genotyping (SNP genotyping array) of genomic DNA from saliva to detect select clinically relevant variants; not sequencing, not comprehensive BRCA analysis" per FDA source K223597.
verifications.evidence: The cited source URL does not support the value because the 510(k) number K223597 does not resolve in the FDA premarket notification database — accessdata.fda.gov returns HTTP 404 for ID=K223597, and web search finds no FDA 510(k) record under that number. The actual FDA authorization for the 23andMe BRCA1/BRCA2 (Selected Variants) Genetic Health Risk Report is the De Novo grant DEN170046 (granted March 6, 2018; decision summary at accessdata.fda.gov/cdrh_docs/reviews/DEN170046.pdf and /pdf17/DEN170046.pdf), with a later 510(k) clearance in Sept 2023 expanding to 41 additional variants. While the methodology DESCRIPTION (qualitative SNP genotyping array of saliva-derived genomic DNA, detecting select variants, not sequencing/comprehensive analysis) is broadly consistent with how 23andMe's PGS BRCA report works, the SPECIFIC source cited (K223597) is wrong/nonexistent and therefore does not substantiate the value. The claim's source-to-value linkage fails verification.

verifications.claim: 23andMe Genetic Health Risk Report for BRCA1/BRCA2 (Selected Variants) (hct-32): fdaStatusNotes = "Originally FDA-authorized 2018 for 3 Ashkenazi Jewish founder variants (DEN170046); expanded under 510(k) K223597 (2023) to report 41 additional variants for 44 total. Still not comprehensive BRCA sequencing." Source: FDA 510(k) database page for K223597.
verifications.evidence: Every factual element of the proposed value is corroborated by FDA records and contemporaneous reporting. (1) 2018 De Novo authorization: On 2018-03-06 the FDA granted 23andMe a De Novo (DEN170046) for a direct-to-consumer test covering exactly 3 BRCA founder variants common in Ashkenazi Jewish populations (BRCA1 185delAG and 5382insC; BRCA2 6174delT). DEN170046 is confirmed as the reference number for this authorization (FDA decision summary at accessdata.fda.gov/cdrh_docs/reviews/DEN170046.pdf; 23andMe press release). (2) 2023 expansion: FDA 510(k) K223597, cleared/announced August 2023, expanded the "Genetic Health Risk Report for BRCA1/BRCA2 (Selected Variants)" to report 41 ADDITIONAL variants, for 44 total (3 + 41 = 44). This is consistently reported by 23andMe's investor release, MedTech Dive, ASCO Post, and Precision Medicine Online, and the FDA decision summary K223597.pdf exists in FDA's database. (3) "Not comprehensive": the FDA decision summary is explicit that the 44-variant panel checks selected variants out of 1,000+ pathogenic BRCA1/BRCA2 variants and does not represent the majority of variants in most ethnicities — i.e., it is not comprehensive BRCA sequencing. CAVEAT: the exact URL in the claim (pmn.cfm?ID=K223597) returned HTTP 404 when fetched; however the K223597 record is genuinely in the FDA 510(k) database (decision summary PDF at accessdata.fda.gov/cdrh_docs/reviews/K223597.pdf), so the cited source type (FDA, K223597) does support the value. The numeric reasoning (3 original + 41 added = 44 total) is internally consistent and matches FDA's own statements.

verifications.claim: 23andMe Genetic Health Risk Report for BRCA1/BRCA2 (Selected Variants) (hct-32): sampleType = "Saliva" per https://www.23andme.com/brca/
verifications.evidence: The vendor page at https://www.23andme.com/brca/ explicitly states the BRCA1/BRCA2 (Selected Variants) test "uses qualitative genotyping to detect select clinically relevant variants in the genomic DNA of adults from saliva." This directly supports a sampleType of "Saliva." It is consistent with 23andMe's well-known direct-to-consumer model, which relies on a mailed saliva collection kit for all of its genotyping reports. The page describes it as the first and only FDA-authorized direct-to-consumer BRCA test (44 selected variants), all from a saliva sample. No alternative sample type (blood, buccal swab, etc.) is mentioned.

verifications.claim: 23andMe Genetic Health Risk Report for BRCA1/BRCA2 (Selected Variants) (hct-32): proposed vendorStatusNote = "23andMe filed Chapter 11 in March 2025; assets acquired by TTAM Research Institute (closed July 2025). Consumer health reports including the BRCA report remain available." per source https://www.hipaajournal.com/genetic-testing-company-23andme-files-for-bankruptcy/
verifications.evidence:
The cited HIPAA Journal article (published July 7, 2025) supports two of the three assertions in the proposed note but not the third, and slightly mischaracterizes the closing.

(1) Chapter 11 in March 2025 — SUPPORTED. Article: "In March 2025, 23andMe filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection and announced that the company was planning to sell substantially all of its assets."

(2) Assets acquired by TTAM Research Institute — SUPPORTED. Article: a federal bankruptcy court "approved the sale of direct-to-consumer genetic testing company 23andMe to TTAM Research Institute," which "was founded by former 23andMe CEO Anne Wojcicki to purchase 23andMe."

(2a) "(closed July 2025)" — NOT FULLY SUPPORTED / MISCHARACTERIZED. The article describes the closing as future/pending, not completed: "with the deal expected to be closed in the coming weeks." As of this source the court had approved the sale in early July 2025 but the deal had NOT yet closed. The note states it "closed July 2025," which the source does not establish (it was only approved, expected to close shortly after).

(3) "Consumer health reports including the BRCA report remain available" — NOT ADDRESSED. The article discusses the assets acquired (Personal Genome Service, Research Services, Lemonaid telehealth) but says nothing about whether health reports or the BRCA report specifically remain available to consumers post-acquisition. This BRCA-specific claim has no support in the cited source.

Verdict: uncertain. The source backs the bankruptcy filing and the TTAM acquisition, but does NOT support the BRCA-report-availability statement (the load-bearing fact for an hct-32 BRCA record), and it contradicts the "closed July 2025" phrasing (deal was only approved/pending, expected to close in coming weeks). The note needs a different/additional source for claim 3 and a wording fix for the closing status.

name: AbsoluteDx
qualityRationale: Record is broadly accurate and current: AbsoluteDx is a real, actively offered Allelica product launched Oct 2025, saliva-based, built on NCCN v1.2026 + ACMG SF v3.3 gene lists, with PRS integrated to modulate monogenic penetrance — all confirmed by the vendor launch release. But several specific claims are wrong or unverifiable: the lab partner is the Broad Institute (per launch PR), not the 'Baylor College of Medicine' stated in prsCitations; the methodology is a blended low-coverage WGS (3.23X) + WES (96.5X) per the PR, not the pure 'clinical exome sequencing' described; and the publication is cited as 'Nature Communications' but the actual Allelica-coauthored hereditary-breast-cancer PRS+NGS validation is Tsoulos et al. in Diagnostics (Basel) 2024 (PMID 39202314). Gene count is unstated (panel = full NCCN+ACMG lists, far more than the 10 keyGenes shown). vendorVerified is false despite a CEO submission.

defects.issue: States 'Baylor College of Medicine collaboration' as the source/lab. The official launch press release names the Broad Institute as Allelica's CLIA/CAP lab partner for AbsoluteDx. No Baylor association found — appears factually wrong.

Cites 'Nature Communications (multi-ancestry PRS)' as the validation publication. The actual Allelica-coauthored hereditary breast cancer PRS+NGS validation is Tsoulos et al., Diagnostics (Basel) 2024, PMID 39202314 — a different journal. The Nature Communications citation is unverifiable as stated.

Describes methodology as 'Clinical exome sequencing'. The launch material describes a blended approach: low-coverage WGS (3.23X) plus WES (96.5X). The PRS component requires genome-wide SNP coverage, which the 'clinical exome' framing omits.

No gene count field. Panel is the full NCCN v1.2026 + ACMG SF v3.3 gene set (dozens of genes); the 10-gene keyGenes list is only a representative subset and could mislead readers into thinking it is a 10-gene panel.
False despite vendorRequestedChanges noting a 2026-01-11 submission directly from Allelica CEO Giordano Botta; verification status appears stale relative to the documented vendor contact.
'Nature Communications (multi-ancestry PRS validation)' lacks a specific title/DOI/PMID — unverifiable citation.

suspectClaims.currentValue: Nature Communications (multi-ancestry PRS validation); Baylor College of Medicine collaboration
suspectClaims.whySuspect: Launch PR names Broad Institute, not Baylor, as the lab partner; no Baylor link found. 'Nature Communications' is unspecified and not the Allelica hereditary-cancer validation paper (which is in Diagnostics).

suspectClaims.currentValue: Clinical exome sequencing (NCCN v1.2026 + ACMG SF v3.3 genes) combined with multi-ancestry PRS
suspectClaims.whySuspect: Vendor materials describe blended WGS (3.23X) + WES (96.5X), not clinical exome alone; gene-list basis (NCCN/ACMG) is correct.
The findable peer-reviewed Allelica hereditary-cancer validation is in Diagnostics (Basel) 2024 (PMID 39202314), not Nature Communications; ASHG 2024 abstracts not independently verified.
Platform vendor (Illumina) plausible but not stated in any sourced material; 'Clinical Exome' descriptor conflicts with the blended WGS+WES method.

Blended low-coverage whole-genome sequencing (~3.23X) plus whole-exome sequencing (~96.5X); monogenic variant calling across full NCCN v1.2026 + ACMG SF v3.3 gene lists combined with genome-wide multi-ancestry polygenic risk scores.
Broad Institute (CLIA- and CAP-certified laboratory)
Non-invasive saliva sample
Comprehensive AbsoluteDx panel plus disease-specific versions: AbsoluteDx Breast Cancer, AbsoluteDx Prostate Cancer, AbsoluteDx Cardio.

summary: AbsoluteDx is a real, currently offered Allelica product (launched Oct 2025) and the record captures its core PRS-modulated-penetrance concept correctly, but it contains a factually wrong lab partner (Baylor instead of the Broad Institute), an over-simplified 'clinical exome' methodology (actually blended WGS+WES), and a misattributed publication ('Nature Communications' instead of the verifiable Diagnostics 2024 paper, PMID 39202314). No gene count and stale vendorVerified=false despite a CEO submission.

verifications.claim: AbsoluteDx (hct-33) prsCitations = "Nature Communications (multi-ancestry PRS validation); Baylor College of Medicine collaboration" is wrong: the launch PR names Broad Institute (not Baylor) as the lab partner, and "Nature Communications" is unspecified / not a hereditary-cancer validation paper.
verifications.evidence:
Field value confirmed in /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 1204. External evidence supports the suspected-wrong reasoning on both points:

1) LAB PARTNER = BROAD, NOT BAYLOR. The AbsoluteDx launch press release (PRNewswire, Oct 9 2025) explicitly states: "AbsoluteDx is offered in partnership with the leaders in innovation and precision medicine at the Broad Institute, one of Allelica's national CLIA- and CAP-certified laboratories." Baylor College of Medicine is NOT mentioned anywhere in the launch PR. Baylor appears only in a separate, earlier arrangement (PRNewswire, March 19 2024) where Allelica provides its PREDICT PRS software to Baylor College of Medicine's Human Genome Sequencing Center for clinical implementation — a standalone PRS-provisioning collaboration, not the AbsoluteDx lab partner and not a hereditary-cancer validation. So naming "Baylor College of Medicine collaboration" as the AbsoluteDx lab/citation is incorrect; the actual lab partner is the Broad Institute.

2) "NATURE COMMUNICATIONS" IS MISLEADING/UNSPECIFIED. The launch PR cites NO specific peer-reviewed publication for PRS validation. Allelica's actual Nature Communications paper is "Ancestry-specific polygenic risk scores are risk enhancers for clinical cardiovascular disease assessments" (Nat Commun, s41467-023-42897-w) — a CARDIOVASCULAR/CAD PRS paper, not a hereditary-cancer PRS validation. Thus the unqualified "Nature Communications (multi-ancestry PRS validation)" in a hereditary-cancer record points to a CVD paper, not cancer validation.

Recommended fix: replace Baylor with Broad Institute as the lab partner, and either remove the unqualified "Nature Communications" reference or specify it is the CVD/CAD paper (not cancer-specific validation).

verifications.claim: AbsoluteDx (hct-33): field "method" = "Clinical exome sequencing (NCCN v1.2026 + ACMG SF v3.3 genes) combined with multi-ancestry PRS" is wrong because the vendor describes a blended WGS (3.23X) + WES (96.5X) approach, not clinical exome alone; the gene-list basis (NCCN/ACMG) is correct.
verifications.evidence: The current record (src/data/tests/hct.json line 1173) sets method = "Clinical exome sequencing (NCCN v1.2026 + ACMG Secondary Findings v3.3 genes) combined with multi-ancestry polygenic risk scores..." and sequencingPlatform = "Illumina (Clinical Exome)". However, Allelica's own AbsoluteDx FAQ page describes the sequencing as low-coverage whole genome sequencing (WGS) at 3.23X coverage PLUS whole exome sequencing (WES) at 96.5X coverage, combined with genome-wide genetic ancestry assessment for calibrated, ancestry-specific risk thresholds. Three independent web searches returned the same exact figures (3.23X WGS + 96.5X WES) sourced from the vendor FAQ, confirming a blended WGS+WES methodology rather than clinical exome sequencing alone. The low-pass WGS component is technically necessary for genome-wide PRS computation (imputation of common variants), which a clinical exome cannot provide on its own — consistent with the vendor description. The gene-list/PRS framing (NCCN, ACMG SF, multi-ancestry PRS) is corroborated and not in dispute. Therefore the sequencing-technology portion of the method field ("Clinical exome sequencing") is inaccurate and should be revised to reflect the blended low-pass WGS (3.23X) + WES (96.5X) approach. Note: direct WebFetch of the vendor FAQ returned HTTP 403 (bot protection), so evidence rests on the search engine's indexed snippets of that page rather than a first-hand page render — but the figures are consistent and specific across multiple queries.

verifications.claim: AbsoluteDx (hct-33) numPublicationsNotes = "Nature Communications (multi-ancestry PRS); ASHG 2024 presentations" — suspected wrong because the findable peer-reviewed Allelica hereditary-cancer validation is in Diagnostics (Basel) 2024 (PMID 39202314), not Nature Communications, and the ASHG 2024 abstracts are not independently verified.
verifications.evidence: The current field value is misleading and the suspicion is correct. Allelica's actual Nature Communications paper (s41467-023-42897-w, "Ancestry-specific polygenic risk scores are risk enhancers for clinical cardiovascular disease assessments") is about coronary artery / cardiovascular disease — NOT hereditary cancer. Citing it as publication support for an HCT (hereditary cancer) test conflates the company's cardiovascular PRS work with cancer evidence. The genuinely findable, peer-reviewed Allelica HEREDITARY CANCER validation is PMID 39202314, "Polygenic Risk Score (PRS) Combined with NGS Panel Testing Increases Accuracy in Hereditary Breast Cancer Risk Estimation," Diagnostics (Basel) 2024 (DOI 10.3390/diagnostics14161826), which includes Allelica-affiliated author George Busby and states "PRS results were obtained from an external laboratory (Allelica)." A second relevant cancer publication exists in Genetics in Medicine 2024 (Tyrer-Cuzick + multi-ancestry PRS combined risk score, 130,058-patient cohort). On ASHG 2024: the only independently verifiable Allelica ASHG 2024 presentation is a methods/diversity-modeling poster ("8 billion risk models: modelling human genetic diversity without labels"), not a cancer-specific clinical abstract — so the cancer-relevant ASHG 2024 claim is unverified. Recommended correction: replace the Nature Communications citation with the Diagnostics (Basel) 2024 hereditary-breast-cancer paper (PMID 39202314) and optionally the Genetics in Medicine 2024 combined-risk-score paper; the Nature Communications CAD paper should not be cited as cancer evidence. (Note: numPublications=1 also undercounts the verifiable cancer publications.) The file at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 1223 holds the field; prsCitations on line 1204 carries the same Nature Communications attribution and has the same problem.

verifications.claim: AbsoluteDx (hct-33) field "sequencingPlatform" = "Illumina (Clinical Exome)" is wrong because Illumina is not stated in sourced material and "Clinical Exome" conflicts with the blended WGS+WES method.
verifications.evidence: The official Allelica AbsoluteDx FAQ ("How is this test performed?") states the method verbatim: "Sequencing of genetic variants utilizing low-coverage whole genome sequencing (WGS) at 3.23X coverage plus whole exome sequencing (WES) at 96.5X coverage." This is a blended WGS+WES protocol, NOT a "Clinical Exome," so the "(Clinical Exome)" descriptor in the field is inaccurate and conflicts with the vendor's own described method. It also matches the test's own "method" field in hct.json (line 1173) which says clinical exome sequencing — itself an oversimplification of the documented WGS+WES approach. Regarding the platform vendor: "Illumina" is NOT stated anywhere in the AbsoluteDx product or FAQ material as the sequencing instrument. The only Illumina reference found on Allelica's site is an unrelated embedded press-release title, "Illumina Licenses Allelica Polygenic Risk Score Software" — a software licensing arrangement, not evidence that AbsoluteDx is run on Illumina sequencers. Thus the platform attribution is unsourced and the "(Clinical Exome)" descriptor is contradicted by primary vendor documentation. Both prongs of the suspected error are corroborated. (Vendor site blocked WebFetch with HTTP 403; verified via Playwright browser reading the live page DOM, including the collapsed-accordion answer text.) Record location: /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json lines 1166-1226, sequencingPlatform at line 1175.

verifications.claim: AbsoluteDx (hct-33): numPublicationsNotes = "Tsoulos et al., 'Polygenic Risk Score (PRS) Combined with NGS Panel Testing Increases Accuracy in Hereditary Breast Cancer Risk Estimation,' Diagnostics (Basel) 2024 (Allelica co-author G. Busby; PRS provided by Allelica)." per https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39202314/
verifications.evidence: The PubMed source (PMID 39202314) confirms every load-bearing element of the proposed note. (1) Title matches verbatim: "Polygenic Risk Score (PRS) Combined with NGS Panel Testing Increases Accuracy in Hereditary Breast Cancer Risk Estimation." (2) Journal and year match: Diagnostics (Basel), 2024 (published Aug 21, 2024). (3) First author is Nikolaos Tsoulos, supporting "Tsoulos et al." (4) George Busby is a listed author (16th of 18) affiliated with Allelica Inc., New York; the conflict-of-interest statement states "G.B. is employed by and holds equity in Allelica, Inc." — confirming "Allelica co-author G. Busby." (5) The study explicitly combines PRS with NGS panel testing for hereditary breast cancer risk estimation. The single mild caveat: "PRS provided by Allelica" is not a verbatim quote I extracted, but it is strongly substantiated — the paper concerns PRS integration and has an Allelica-employed co-author, making Allelica's role in supplying the PRS well-founded rather than fabricated. All factual citation elements are accurate.

verifications.claim: AbsoluteDx (hct-33): proposed method = "Blended low-coverage whole-genome sequencing (~3.23X) plus whole-exome sequencing (~96.5X); monogenic variant calling across full NCCN v1.2026 + ACMG SF v3.3 gene lists combined with genome-wide multi-ancestry polygenic risk scores."
verifications.evidence: The PRNewswire press release supports the general framing but NOT the specific, load-bearing sequencing parameters in the claimed method value. The source confirms only that AbsoluteDx is "a comprehensive, clinical genetic test that combines monogenic and polygenic risk assessment," with "monogenic testing across the full list of NCCN Version 1.2026 and ACMG Secondary Findings v3.3 genes" plus "multi-ancestry PRSs for these same diseases," run from "a single, non-invasive saliva sample." It does NOT mention: (1) low-coverage whole-genome sequencing, (2) ~3.23X WGS depth, (3) whole-exome sequencing, (4) ~96.5X WES depth, or (5) any "blended WGS+WES" approach or sequencing methodology at all. The detailed technical specifications (3.23X WGS, 96.5X WES, blended approach, genome-wide PRS) are fabricated/unsupported by this source — they appear nowhere in the press release. Because these specific numeric/technical specs are the distinguishing content of the stated method value and are not present in the cited vendor source, the claim that "this source supports this value" is refuted. Only the gene-list/PRS framing is corroborated.

verifications.claim: AbsoluteDx (hct-33) labPartner = "Broad Institute (CLIA- and CAP-certified laboratory)"
verifications.evidence: The cited PRNewswire press release ("Allelica Launches AbsoluteDx...") explicitly states: "AbsoluteDx is offered in partnership with the leaders in innovation and precision medicine at the Broad Institute, one of Allelica's national CLIA- and CAP-certified laboratories." This directly supports the proposed labPartner value of "Broad Institute (CLIA- and CAP-certified laboratory)." verifications.evidence: The PRNewswire press release (Allelica's official launch announcement for AbsoluteDx) explicitly states: "With a single, non-invasive saliva sample, patients have access to the full spectrum of genetic insights available." This directly confirms the product (AbsoluteDx), the company (Allelica), and the proposed sampleType value of "Non-invasive saliva sample" verbatim. The phrasing in the proposed value matches the source language nearly word-for-word.

verifications.claim: AbsoluteDx (hct-33): productVariants = "Comprehensive AbsoluteDx panel plus disease-specific versions: AbsoluteDx Breast Cancer, AbsoluteDx Prostate Cancer, AbsoluteDx Cardio."
verifications.evidence: The cited Allelica/PR Newswire press release directly supports every element of the claimed value. It describes a comprehensive AbsoluteDx panel that combines monogenic testing across the full NCCN Version 1.2026 and ACMG Secondary Findings v3.3 gene lists with multi-ancestry polygenic risk scores spanning hereditary cancer syndromes, cardiovascular, metabolic, and neurological diseases. It then introduces three disease-specific versions named verbatim: "AbsoluteDx Breast Cancer, AbsoluteDx Prostate Cancer, and AbsoluteDx Cardio," described as targeted panels giving specialists a tailored tool to identify and manage patients at risk of specific diseases. The product naming and the comprehensive-plus-disease-specific structure in the proposed productVariants value match the source exactly.

name: Risk MAPS
qualityRationale: Core identity (vendor, name, WES approach, panel structure, cancer types) is accurate and confirmed against the live vendor page (proteanbiodx.com/riskmaps and /risk-panels). Test is currently offered. However the record contains one factual error (sample type listed as Saliva; vendor specifies cheek swab), a misleading gene count (22,000 WES backbone presented as genesAnalyzed when the actual reported comprehensive panel is 135 genes), and omits two readily-sourced facts: 2-3 week turnaround time and explicit NCCN/ASCO guideline adherence. No price is public. Net: accurate skeleton with a wrong field and missing high-value fields.

defects.issue: Record states sample type is 'Saliva' (and sampleTubeType 'Saliva collection kit', sampleCollectionNotes 'Saliva sample'), but the vendor's official page explicitly states the test is performed on a CHEEK SWAB sample. Saliva and buccal/cheek swab are distinct collection methods; this is factually wrong.

genesAnalyzed=22000 is misleading for a hereditary cancer panel. 22,000+ is the whole-exome backbone; the actual reported Total Comprehensive Cancer Risk Panel is 135 genes (with smaller site-specific panels of 19-62 genes). A user comparing HCT panels would be badly misinformed by '22000'.
No turnaround time field present. Vendor states 2-3 weeks for sequencing and analysis — a standard, readily-sourced HCT field that is missing.
Vendor states the test follows NCCN and ASCO guidelines; record does not capture this guideline alignment anywhere.

Notes claim syndromes 'including HBOC, Lynch syndrome, FAP, Peutz-Jeghers' — plausible given gene content (e.g., STK11, APC, MLH1/MSH2) but not explicitly stated on the cited vendor page; presented as fact without direct citation.

Vendor's panel page lists a Total Comprehensive Cancer Risk Panel of 135 genes; 22,000 is the exome capture size, not the analyzed/reported cancer-predisposition gene set.

verifications.evidence: The record exists at /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 1228-1264): id "hct-34", name "Risk MAPS", vendor "Protean BioDiagnostics", approach "Whole exome sequencing", genesAnalyzed: 22000, genesAnalyzedNotes "Whole exome (22,000+ genes). Comprehensive cancer panel or site-specific panels available...". The vendor's own Risk MAPS panels page (proteanbiodx.com/risk-panels) lists seven discrete cancer-risk panels verbatim, topped by "Total Comprehensive Cancer Risk Panel: 135 Gene Panel" (plus Breast/Ovarian 38, Gastric 32, Colorectal 27, Pancreatic 23, Prostate 19, Combined Hereditary 62). The main riskmaps page presents "Whole Exome | Over 22,000 Genes" as the underlying exome-capture breadth that also covers non-cancer inherited syndromes and polygenic risk scores, distinct from the focused cancer-predisposition gene set that is actually analyzed/reported. verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline (hct-35) field "geneticCounselingIncluded" = "Yes - free post-test genetic counseling via DNAvisit for patients with pathogenic variants" is wrong because the vendor allegedly does not name DNAvisit nor restrict counseling to pathogenic-variant carriers, making those details unverifiable.
verifications.evidence: The current field value in src/data/tests/hct.json line 1323 reads exactly: "Yes - free post-test genetic counseling via DNAvisit for patients with pathogenic variants". The official Foundation Medicine vendor page (foundationmedicine.com/test/germline-testing) directly confirms every disputed element: (1) DNAvisit is explicitly named — verbatim: "a company called DNAvisit will reach out directly to the patient to coordinate a genetic counseling session"; (2) it is no-cost — "Post-test genetic counseling is available at no-cost to qualifying patients"; (3) it IS restricted to pathogenic-variant carriers — "Qualifying patients include all individuals who are identified to carry a diagnostic pathogenic or likely pathogenic variant through germline testing." Both the provider name (DNAvisit) and the eligibility restriction (pathogenic / likely pathogenic variant carriers) are therefore verifiable from the official source, directly contradicting the claim's stated suspicion. The field is accurate. (One minor pedantic nuance: official text covers "pathogenic OR likely pathogenic" variants while the field says "pathogenic variants" — a reasonable shorthand, not an error worth flagging.) The cited foundationmedicine.com/faq/patient-faqs page does NOT contain these specifics; the germline test page is the authoritative source.

verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline (hct-35): field "cptCodes" = "81432, 81433" — suspected wrong (no citation; standard HBOC-panel codes that may not reflect what Fulgent bills for a 50-gene panel).
verifications.evidence: Record confirmed: /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json line 1316 has cptCodes "81432, 81433" with NO cptCodesCitations field, and reimbursementNote says "Billing handled by Fulgent Genetics." The suspicion is well-founded but I cannot fully refute the value. CPT 81432 is a genomic sequence analysis PANEL code scoped to "hereditary breast cancer-related disorders" (hereditary breast/ovarian/endometrial cancer) requiring a defined minimum gene set (BRCA1/2, CDH1, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PALB2, PTEN, STK11, TP53, ATM, etc.); 81433 is its matching duplication/deletion panel code. These are HBOC-panel codes, NOT a comprehensive-hereditary-cancer code. FoundationOne Germline is a 50-gene PAN-hereditary-cancer panel spanning HBOC, Lynch, Li-Fraumeni, Cowden, FAP and others — broader than the 81432/81433 definition. Comprehensive 50-gene multi-syndrome panels are typically billed with 81479 (unlisted molecular pathology) or proprietary PLA codes, often in addition to or instead of the HBOC GSP pair. Critically, no public source confirms 81432/81433 for this product: Foundation Medicine's official "About the Tests" PDF lists NO CPT codes, FM's germline test page lists none, and FM directs all billing inquiries to Fulgent Genetics (billing@fulgentgenetics.com). So the field value is uncited, conceptually mismatched to a 50-gene multi-syndrome panel, and unverifiable from public sources — but not provably false (Fulgent could bill 81432/81433 as a partial/primary code). Recommend removing or flagging the codes pending a Fulgent-sourced citation.

verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline (hct-35): deletionDuplicationAnalysis = "Yes" is wrong/unsourced because the vendor page does not explicitly confirm del/dup (CNV) analysis is included in the 50-gene tier.
verifications.evidence: The "Yes" value is correct and adequately sourced. Three independent lines of evidence support that the 50-gene FoundationOne Germline tier includes deletion/duplication (CNV) analysis: (1) FoundationOne Germline is a laboratory-developed test "powered by Fulgent Genetics," and Fulgent's hereditary cancer NGS platform provides deletion/duplication detection down to single-exon (1 exon) resolution via NGS with orthogonal confirmation (Sanger, qPCR, MLPA) — the technical basis for the FMI product. (2) Foundation Medicine's launch press release / "About the Tests" materials describe deletion/duplication analysis "evaluated at a single exon resolution level in relevant genes," not restricted to the 154-gene More tier. (3) The record itself lists CPT codes "81432, 81433" — 81433 is the dedicated hereditary-cancer deletion/duplication-analysis code (paired with the 81432 sequencing code), a billing pattern that only makes sense if del/dup analysis is performed. The data.js method field also explicitly reads "NGS with copy number variant (CNV) analysis; 50-gene hereditary cancer panel powered by Fulgent Genetics." The claim's concern was that the consumer-facing germline-testing webpage does not spell out CNV — true, that page is non-technical — but the underlying Fulgent platform documentation and the CPT coding directly source the capability. Verdict: the suspicion is refuted; the field value "Yes" is confirmed as correct.

verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline More (hct-36): field "deletionDuplicationAnalysis" = "Yes" is wrong/unverified because the cited vendor page does not state del/dup or CNV analysis is performed.
verifications.evidence: The field value "Yes" is factually correct, so the claim that it is wrong is refuted. It is true that the exact URL cited in the record (https://www.foundationmedicine.com/test/germline-testing) is a thin marketing landing page that does NOT mention CNV or deletion/duplication analysis — I fetched it and confirmed no such text appears. However, Foundation Medicine's official "About the Tests" document for FoundationOne Germline / Germline More explicitly confirms del/dup (CNV) analysis is performed. Its Analytical Performance table states: Coverage = "Deletion/Duplication detection as low as single exon resolution"; Specificity and Sensitivity = "Copy Number Variants: Sensitivity >97%, Specificity >99%"; Orthogonal Confirmation = "Sanger sequencing and long-range PCR for selected variants, MLPA/qPCR for CNVs". The Limitations section only excludes copy-neutral structural rearrangements (e.g., translocations), not deletions/duplications. Therefore deletionDuplicationAnalysis = "Yes" is accurate. The only legitimate defect is a citation-quality issue: the methodCitations/genesAnalyzedCitations URL points to the generic landing page rather than the authoritative PDF; the data value itself is correct and should not be changed. Recommend updating the citation to the About the Tests PDF.

verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline More (hct-36) field geneticCounselingIncluded = "Yes - free post-test genetic counseling via DNAvisit for patients with pathogenic variants" is wrong/stale because the vendor page describes Fulgent referral-form counseling and does not name DNAvisit.
verifications.evidence: The record exists in src/data/tests/hct.json (lines 1334-1392), where hct-36 = "FoundationOne Germline More" (Foundation Medicine) and geneticCounselingIncluded is exactly the quoted value, citing https://www.foundationmedicine.com/test/germline-testing. The suspicion is incorrect on both points. The current vendor germline-testing page DOES explicitly name DNAvisit, and the Fulgent referral form and DNAvisit are not alternatives — they are sequential steps in one workflow. Per the page and corroborating press coverage: post-test genetic counseling is no-cost for qualifying patients (those carrying a diagnostic pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant); the ordering provider submits a genetic counseling referral form to fmisupport@fulgentgenetics.com, after which "a company called DNAvisit will reach out directly to the patient to coordinate a genetic counseling session." This matches the data field: free post-test counseling via DNAvisit, for patients with pathogenic variants. The "via DNAvisit" attribution is current, not stale. Note for accuracy: the data file is src/data/tests/hct.json, not src/data.js as the CLAUDE.md docs imply.

verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline More (hct-36): field "tat" currently = "14-21 days". Suspected wrong because: accurate in substance (vendor says 2-3 weeks) but stated as a precise day range the vendor does not give verbatim; minor over-specification.

The value is substantively correct and properly cited. The only "issue" is that the database expresses the range in days rather than the vendor's verbatim "weeks" phrasing — a stylistic/normalization choice, not a data error. verifications.claim: FoundationOne Germline More (hct-36) duplicates hct-30 (FoundationOne Germline More, Foundation Medicine) — they are the same product.
verifications.evidence: Both records exist in /Users/adickinson/Documents/GitHub/V0/src/data/tests/hct.json and carry the identical name "FoundationOne Germline More" and vendor "Foundation Medicine". hct-30 (line 1110) is a sparse stub from the 2026-01-09 batch submission: method "Expanded germline testing offering", no gene count, productType "Central Lab Service", sampleCategory "Blood or Saliva". hct-36 (line 1334) is a fully-populated record (dateAdded 2026-03-23) for the SAME product: name "FoundationOne Germline More", Foundation Medicine, method "154-gene expanded hereditary cancer panel powered by Fulgent Genetics", genesAnalyzed 154, same sample categories. External verification confirms "FoundationOne Germline More" is one single real product: Foundation Medicine's press release and product pages describe it as the 154-gene expanded version of the 50-gene FoundationOne Germline (both powered by Fulgent Genetics, 2-3 week TAT, blood/saliva).
